In PAM4 mode, the optical combination of three LDs extends the transmitter's linear operating region by approximately 2.4-fold and the CNN-LSTM demodulation algorithm achieves a 0.8 dB improvement in attenuation tolerance compared to fixed-threshold decisions. Through software-defined reconfiguration between each modulation mode, the system achieves a 6.9 dB improvement in attenuation tolerance and extends the usable transmission rate range seven-fold (100-700 Mbps) compared to fixed-modulation baselines. This work provides a feasible solution for dynamically balancing data rate and link robustness in complex underwater optical communication networks, showing promising improvements in both link performance and transmission rate flexibility.

title: Energy-Closure Framework for Physics-Consistent Electric Vehicle Fleet Estimation
authors: Rahul Kumar Dubey
abstract: Accurate energy estimation is critical for electric vehicle (EV) range prediction and fleet optimization, yet existing approaches suffer from systematic bias, limited parameter stability, and weak alignment with industrial standards. This paper presents a physics-consistent, two-stage calibration framework that achieves analytically enforced integral energy closure through a non-optimized analytic recentering mechanism (P∗ const). Stage 1 identifies global, vehicle-invariant parameters {CdA, Crr20, ηbase, Qair} using grouped cross-validation with variance-regularized Huber loss and Differential Evolution. Stage 2 refines trip-specific modifiers {ktrac,i, ΔPaux,i, ηregen,i} under physics-consistent constraints, including state-of-charge and speed-gated regenerative behavior. From the calibrated power chain, field-derived inverter and motor efficiency maps are extracted, enabling explainable digital-twin updates. Validated on 70 real-world BEV trips (raw ≈ 1.1 × 106 samples logged at 10 Hz and uniformly resampled to 1 Hz for modeling), the framework achieves strict fleet-level energy closure (ΔE≈0%), high pointwise accuracy (R2 > 0.93), and strong parameter stability (bootstrap CoV: 0.3–6.6%). The proposed method satisfies five industrial standards (IEC 62660-2, ISO 26262-11, IEEE P2668, UL 1974, AIS-038 Rev. 2), enabling standards-aligned application in fleet analytics and digital-twin calibration for transportation electrification.

title: IEEE Sensors Letters Publication Information
authors: N/A

title: A Fast Robust Adaptive Filter using Improved Data-Reuse Method
authors: Yi Peng, Haiquan Zhao, Jinhui Hu
abstract: Adaptive filter in complex scenarios demands algorithms that integrate fast convergence, low complexity, and robust performance under diverse noise conditions. To address this challenge, we propose an online censoring robust total generalized adaptive filter using improved data-reused method (RTGAIDROC) algorithm. The proposed RTGA variant possesses the advantages of both the total least squares (TLS) strategy and the robust generalized adaptive (RGA) function. This algorithm not only effectively handles input noise under the errors-invariables (EIV) model but also achieves excellent performance across diverse noise environments. Furthermore, to meet the high demand for convergence speed in practical applications, an improved data reuse (IDR) method is introduced, enabling faster convergence in the early stages of iteration without compromising steady-state performance. The increased computational complexity brought by the IDR method is mitigated using the online censoring (OC) strategy. We also modify the OC threshold for real-valued algorithms, as the original threshold was defined for the complex domain. Beyond these algorithmic enhancements, a local stability analysis for the proposed algorithm is provided, and the theoretical steady-state mean-square deviation (MSD) is derived. Finally, simulation experiments in system identification and acoustic echo cancellation (AEC) scenarios validate the superior performance of the proposed algorithm.

title: A Large-dimensional Analysis of ESPRIT DoA Estimation: Inconsistency and a Correction via RMT_supp1-3683673.pdf
authors: Zhenyu Liao
abstract: In this paper, we perform asymptotic analyses of the widely used ESPRIT direction-of-arrival (DoA) estimator for large arrays, where the array size N and the number of snapshots T grow to infinity at the same pace. In this large-dimensional regime, the sample covariance matrix (SCM) is known to be a poor eigenspectral estimator of the population covariance. We show that the classical ESPRIT algorithm, that relies on the SCM, and as a consequence of the large-dimensional inconsistency of the SCM, produces inconsistent DoA estimates as $N, T →$∞ with $N/T →c$ ε (0,∞), for both widely- and closely-spaced DoAs. Leveraging tools from random matrix theory (RMT), we propose an improved G-ESPRIT method and prove its consistency in the same large-dimensional setting. From a technical perspective, we derive a novel bound on the eigenvalue differences between two potentially non-Hermitian matrices, which may be of independent interest. Numerical simulations are provided to corroborate our theoretical findings.

title: Optically Navigated Surgical Robotic System for Automated In Situ Bioprinting in Confined Anatomical Spaces
authors: Zitong Wang, Yuncheng Wu, Shuoyu Ji, Zhihong Cao, Liang Feng, Changru Zhang, Jinwu Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Medical Robotics and Bionics
abstract: Robot-assisted in situ bioprinting allows for the direct deposition of biomaterials at defect sites, offering a minimally invasive alternative to traditional scaffold implantation. Yet, its clinical translation remains hindered by limited accessibility in anatomically constrained regions and the lack of precise control. To address these challenges, this study introduces optical navigation from computer-assisted surgery into in situ bioprinting to achieve explicit image–patient registration and translate anatomical localization into accurate robot motion. An optically navigated active remote center of motion (RCM) strategy is further developed to support constrained motion in confined anatomical spaces. The system was validated through autonomous in situ printing of a two-layer scaffold on a femoral defect phantom using a six-degree-of-freedom robotic arm. Quantitative evaluation demonstrated a target registration error of 0.583 mm and a maximum RCM distance error of 0.052 mm, indicating accurate localization and constrained motion. The printed scaffold exhibited high geometric fidelity based on multiple structural metrics. These results highlight the precision and reliability of the proposed framework and its potential for autonomous in situ bioprinting in deep or subdermal environments.

title: Efficient Volume-Hiding Encrypted Conjunctive Search with Leakage Suppression for Cloud-Assisted IoT
authors: Yi Dou, Chaoran Zhou, Haiping Huang, Huaqun Wang, Hua Dai, Man Ho Au
abstract: In resource-constrained environments such as IoT sensors and mobile devices, there is a strong demand for efficient conjunctive keyword search over privacy-sensitive data. However, existing schemes struggle to simultaneously suppress sterm equality leakage, the cross-query intersection pattern (IP), and the volume pattern without incurring prohibitive overhead. In this paper, we present XORCMM, a practical volume-hiding encrypted conjunctive multi-map (EMM) designed for robust leakage suppression. First, we shift the index construction from single keywords to global-ordering co-occurrence pairs, which ensures that search tokens are no longer tied to static keyword identities, thereby suppressing sterm equality leakage. Second, we integrate an incremental multiset hash aggregation mechanism directly into a fully padded Xor filter. This allows the server to aggregate multiple conjunctive results into a single, fixed length response, concealing both IP and volume patterns while eliminating the data redundancy of prior schemes. Third, we employ a prefix-constrained PRF to compactly encode keyword pairs, generating succinct query tokens whose size is independent of keyword volumes. Formal security analysis proves that XORCMM is adaptively secure with sterm equality, IP, and volume leakages hidden. Experimental results demonstrate that XORCMM achieves up to a 2.99× speedup in client setup, a 3.3× speedup in server query time, and reductions of 47% in response size and 84.61% in search token size, providing a stronger security guarantee with significantly higher efficiency.

title: Novel AI-empowered Memristive Biosensing Computational Approach for Biomarker Detection
authors: Manel Bouzouita, Fakhreddine Zayer, Ioulia Tzouvadaki, Sandro Carrara, Hamdi Belgacem
abstract: Nowadays, computational models of resistive random access memory (RRAM) structures are emerging as a pre-fabrication stage thanks to their capability for simulating device variables and dynamics. However, existing models face persistent challenges in multiphysics representation, readout reliability, device variability, and noise robustness. In this work a dynamic electrochemical simulation model of a metal-oxide memristive biosensor is presented and demonstrated for highly accurate bio-detection of C-reactive protein (CRP) and prostate-specific antigen (PSA). AI-tools are investigated to improve prediction of PSA biomarker levels. A multiphysics simulation framework was employed to model the coupled chemical and physical behaviour of a metal oxide (MoX) memristive biosensor. Bio-molecular immobilization was analysed by modelling partial differential equations (PDEs), and interfacial chemical reactions. The simulated electrical response to prostate specific antigen (PSA) was validated against previously reported experimental data and classified using a support vector machine (SVM) to reduce noise and improve accuracy. The obtained results show strong agreement with experimental measurements, achieving a mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 4.5%. The simulated biosensor exhibits a sensitivity of 7.34 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">7</sup>Ω · m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>/g and a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.67 pM. Additionally, the proposed AI-based classification model attains an accuracy exceeding 99.5 %. This study demonstrates the suitability of AI-enhanced memristive platforms for highly accurate biosensing and motivating further development of advanced edge-biosensing architectures.

title: Information Freshness and Energy Efficiency Tradeoffs in IEEE 802.11: Age of Information Performance Analysis of Target Wake Time Mechanism
authors: Po-Hao Ting, Cheng-Wei Tsai, Wen-Di Shen, Hung-Yu Wei
publish: IEEE Transactions on Green Communications and Networking
abstract: The IEEE 802.11ax standard introduces Target Wake Time (TWT) as a crucial power-saving mechanism that schedules the wake-up times of stations (STAs) for receiving packets, while the remaining time is spent in a Doze State to conserve power. Although TWT significantly reduces energy consumption and mitigates channel contention, its impact on information freshness—a key requirement for real-time and IoT applications—remains underexplored. In particular, Age of Information (AoI), which quantifies the timeliness of received data, has rarely been considered in existing TWT analyses. This paper presents a comprehensive TWT analysis with a focus on AoI metrics alongside power consumption. We derive closed-form expressions for expected AoI, expected peak AoI, and average power consumption under various TWT configurations. The results demonstrate a strong correlation between theoretical analysis and simulation outcomes, validating the accuracy of the proposed model and offering valuable insights into optimizing TWT scheduling for improved network performance.

title: RideAgent: An LLM-Enhanced Optimization Framework for Automated Taxi Fleet Operations
authors: Xinyu Jiang, Haoyu Zhang, Mengyi Sha, Zihao Jiao, Long He, Junbo Zhang, Wei Qi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Efficient management of electric ride-hailing fleets, particularly pre-allocation and pricing during peak periods to balance spatio-temporal supply and demand, is crucial for urban traffic efficiency. However, practical challenges include unpredictable demand and translating diverse, qualitative managerial objectives from non-expert operators into tractable optimization models. This paper introduces RideAgent, a Large Language Model (LLM)-powered agent framework that automates and enhances electric ride-hailing fleet management. First, an LLM interprets natural language queries from fleet operators to formulate corresponding mathematical objective functions. These user-defined objectives are then optimized within a Mixed-Integer Programming (MIP) framework, subject to the constraint of maintaining high operational profit. The profit itself is a primary objective, estimated by an embedded Random Forest (RF) model leveraging exogenous features. To accelerate the solution of this MIP, a prompt-guided LLM analyzes a small sample of historical optimal decision data to guide a variable fixing strategy. Experiments on real-world data show that the LLM-generated objectives achieve an 86% text similarity to standard formulations in a zero-shot setting. Following this, the LLM-guided variable fixing strategy reduces computation time by 60.22% compared to solving the full MIP with only a 2.96% average optimality gap. Moreover, this variable fixing strategy outperforms five cutting plane methods by 42.3% time reduction with minimal compromise to solution quality. RideAgent offers a reliable and scalable automated framework for objective modeling and accelerated optimization. This framework empowers non-expert fleet operators to personalize operations and improve urban transportation system performance.

title: MANR-Net: Morphology-Aware and Noise-Repair Network for 3D Object Detection
authors: Wenyu Ji, Zhuang Miao, Yang Li, Jiabao Wang, Xiaoyu Lu
abstract: Multi-modal 3D object detection for autonomous driving faces challenges such as inaccurate depth estimation and noise interference. Existing methods often struggle to strike a balance between feature extraction effectiveness and data integrity. To address these issues, we propose the Morphology-Aware and Noise-Repair Network (MANR-Net), featuring two novel modules: the Morphology-Aware blocks enhance robustness in sparse scenes by dynamically focusing on structural key points through stochastic point discard and cascaded perception; the Noise-Repair blocks restore geometric information by guiding noise points with repair vectors instead of applying forced filtering. Evaluated on KITTI and nuScenes datasets, MANR-Net achieves state-of-the-art performance, reaching 86.70% 3D mean Average Precision (mAP) on KITTI, which is 1.14% higher than the baseline. Ablation studies verify the effectiveness of both blocks, with the Noise-Repair blocks making a notable contribution to the overall performance gain.

title: DOA Estimation Exploiting Moving Sparse Uniform Linear Array With Coprime Frequencies
authors: Long Zhang, Sheng Wang, Chengcheng Liu, Guan Gui
abstract: This paper presents an innovative aperture synthesis strategy for moving sparse arrays with coprime frequencies, effectively integrating array motion and frequency diversity to significantly enhance the virtual array aperture. The proposed synthesis method achieves a substantial increase in virtual array elements compared to existing sparse array aperture synthesis techniques. A closed-form expression for the distributions of the synthetic virtual array is derived to characterize its structural properties. Building on this foundation, a direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation method is developed within a group sparse framework, effectively leveraging the phase differences induced by coprime frequencies. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing aperture synthesis techniques in both DOA estimation accuracy and achievable degrees of freedom (DOFs), highlighting its superiority for high-resolution array signal processing.

title: Cavity-Enhanced Gas Raman Spectroscopy With Passively Locked Blue Laser Diodes
authors: Yuji Ichikawa, Ippei Asahi, Tatsuo Shiina, Shigeru Yamaguchi
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Quantum Electronics
abstract: Cavity enhanced Raman spectroscopy (CERS) is an emerging technique for real time analysis of gas mixtures. In this paper, the authors employed a passive self-injection locking technique utilizing an anti-reflection (AR) coated blue laser diode and a high finesse external power build-up cavity (PBC) for trace gas analysis by CERS. With a 20 to 30-mW laser diode passively injection locked to resonant frequencies of the PBC by optical feedback, a power build-up factor greater than 5000 and internal power storage of over 100 W were achieved. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of a power build-up cavity pumped by a passively frequency locked AR coated laser diode in the violet-blue wavelength region. In an implementation of this method, limit of detection (LOD) of 0.7 ppm for hydrogen gas at atmospheric pressure was successfully demonstrated in a 1-minute measurement. This system is simple and robust without any electronic controls, suitable for compact field applications.

title: Statistical Horizontal Squeezing Transform and Its Application in Reflection Interface Identification of Deep Carbonate Reservoirs
authors: Yunli Fei, Hui Chen, Ying Hu, Xuping Chen, Yutao Xie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Carbonate reservoirs exhibit complex internal structures and strong heterogeneity. Their seismic responses commonly show multicomponent superposition and pronounced nonstationary characteristics, which readily induce inter-component interference in time-frequency (TF) representations and pose challenges to the characterization of reflection interfaces. To address this issue, we propose a statistical horizontal squeezing transform (SHST) that reformulates group delay (GD) estimation from a pointwise derivative operation to a statistical estimation problem within local TF neighborhoods. Specifically, a statistical group delay estimator (SGDE) is constructed by introducing a weighted short-time Fourier transform (STFT) spectrum, allowing GD to be inferred through local TF statistical consistency rather than single-point gradients. This strategy enhances robustness against component interference and stabilizes energy reassignment. Experiments on synthetic signals and simulated geological models demonstrate that SHST achieves superior TF concentration and robustness under conditions of closely spaced multicomponent signals and noise. Applications to real deep carbonate seismic data further verify the effectiveness of the proposed method in characterizing the time-depth distribution of reflection interfaces, providing a reliable TF analysis tool for seismic interpretation of complex carbonate reservoirs.

title: Effect of Interpupillary Distance Mismatch on Distance and Orientation Perception in Action Space across HMDs
authors: Hunter C. Finney, Maggie K. McCracken, Eric Zhuo, Sarah H. Creem-Regehr, Jeanine K. Stefanucci
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Historically, distances are underestimated in virtual environments, and a mismatch between a participant's inter-pupillary distance (IPD) and the inter-axial distance (IAD) of the head-mounted display (HMD) could contribute to underestimation, but prior work has not examined how this interacts with lens type or target location in the visual field. Prior work testing the effects of IPD mismatch on distance perception in immersive virtual environments (IVEs) shows mixed results for distances within 2-10 meters. One limitation is that this work did not consider the variety of optical lenses employed in different HMDs. The lens type could affect distance perception differently depending on IPD and could affect it even more for targets appearing on the sides of the display rather than in front of the user. We examined whether the HMD type and location of targets in the virtual visual field affect perception when IPD is mismatched and matched. Using a blind walking task, participants judged the distance to targets that were either in front or to the side of the participant. One group viewed targets in the Varjo XR-3 and the other viewed them in the HTC Vive Pro. We found that distances were judged to be farther in the periphery than in front of users for both devices. Only in the HTC Vive Pro did an increase in IPD mismatch result in increased distance estimates for targets presented directly in front of the participant. Directional angular error (error observed in orienting to the target) increased as the target angle increased for both devices. However, in the Varjo XR-3, a higher IPD mismatch resulted in greater angular error when viewing targets to the side. Overall, the findings show that a mismatched IPD can affect depth and orientation judgments depending on target position in the display. However, there are differences in both FOVandresolution that could influence distance and orientation perception. A second experiment was conducted to examine whether these factors contributed to perception. To test for this, we down-sampled the Varjo XR-3 to match the horizontal field of view and resolution of the HTC Vive Pro. We found that orientation error was mostly affected by resolution and FOV as the adjusted Varjo XR-3 yielded similar orientation perception as the HTC Vive Pro. With this knowledge, researchers should consider HMDs, IPD, and viewing area in the display as possible influences on distance and orientation perception in order to make VR accessible and accurate to all users.

title: Strategic Location and Generation Optimization in Radial Power Systems Using Voltage Stability Indices and Fuzzy Logic Optimization
authors: Hernan Ramirez Pereira, Rodney Fariña Martinez, David Franco Leon
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper addresses the problem of strategic location and generation optimization in radial power systems to enhance voltage stability. A methodology is proposed, combining voltage stability indices to identify critical system nodes, and fuzzy logic optimization to determine the optimal location and capacity of distributed generation. Fuzzy logic allows for consideration of multiple criteria and constraints. Results demonstrate that the proposed methodology effectively improves voltage stability by increasing the load margin in 42 %, while also reducing active power losses in radial distribution systems in 94.9 %.

title: Spatial Fusion and Multivariate Analysis for Powdery Mildew in Vineyards: a Pca, Clustering, and Geostatistical Approach
authors: Yordanis Garcia-Dousat, Miguel Araya-Alman, Héctor Valdés-Gómez, Ruber Hernández-García
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Powdery mildew (Erysiphe necator (Schw.) Burr.) is a major phytosanitary threat to vineyard sustainability, often causing severe yield and quality losses under high disease pressure. Despite advances in precision viticulture, reproducible and spatially validated workflows for disease management remain scarce. This study aimed to develop and validate a reproducible pipeline for the spatial characterization of powdery mildew in commercial vineyards of the Maule Region, Chile. Two blocks (Sauvignon Blanc and Chardonnay) were monitored at fruit set and ripening, measuring disease incidence/severity, canopy traits, trunk cross-section, leaf area index, soluble solids, and yield. Data adequacy was confirmed for Sauvignon Blanc (KMO <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\geq 0.6$</tex>), whereas Chardonnay exhibited a lower KMO. In both varieties, Bartlett's test was highly significant (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p<0.001$</tex>). Principal component analysis with Varimax rotation identified three agronomic domains-vigor, pathogen pressure, and productivity-explaining up to 76.2 % (Sauvignon Blanc) and 66.1 % (Chardonnay) of variance. K-means clustering (k=3, silhouette > 0.5) delineated agronomically consistent zones. Ordinary kriging outperformed IDW and spline in LOOCV (RMSE = 1.28 vs. 1.44/78.8 in Sauvignon Blanc; 1.77 vs. 2.22/1525.2 in Chardonnay) and provided variance maps quantifying spatial uncertainty. The contribution lies in a validated, open-source pipeline that extends multivariate, clustering, and geostatistical methods to vineyard disease management. Future work will integrate Sentinel-2/UAV imagery, microclimatic covariates, and geo-aware machine learning to enhance predictive capacity.

title: Continuous Mobile Audio Monitoring for Sleep Apnea Detection
authors: Jing Han, Tong Xia, Cecilia Mascolo
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Audio-based sleep apnea detection methods hold great potential to improve access to diagnosis, by providing unattended sleep apnea screening at home via sound collected from mobile sensors during sleep. Our research involved a thorough comparison and evaluation of tracheal and ambient microphone recordings for sleep apnea detection with different granularities. Utilising a variety of acoustic representations and sophisticated deep learning architectures, we performed an extensive analysis on the open PSG-Audio dataset, which encompasses over 850 hours of audio data from 194 subjects. For sleep apnea classification, the most effective model showed a 90.8 % accuracy in detecting sleep apnea, 83.3 % accuracy when hypopneic and apneic events were detected separately, and 75.7 % accuracy when apneic events were further divided into three sub-categories. On overnight recordings, the model achieved a sensitivity of 0.93 and a specificity of 1.0 for moderate sleep apnea screening, and a sensitivity of 0.84 and a specificity of 0.97 for severe sleep apnea screening. This research also provided a unique study to compare and combine respiratory sounds from two different types of sensors for sleep apnea detection. The high performance of our model provides a promising avenue for enabling remote diagnosis and monitoring of sleep apnea.

title: IEEE Power & Energy Society Publication Information
authors: N/A

title: Experimental Verification of Adaptive Synchronization System under Grid Fault Conditions Using a 2 MW Wind Power Converter Test Bench
authors: J. Ignacio García, J. Ignacio Candela, Pedro Catalán
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: This paper presents a grid synchronization structure for a current-controlled voltage source converter (VSC), known as an adaptive vector grid synchronization system (AVGS). The main blocks of the system are a pre-filter stage, consisting of a filtered quadrature signal generator (FQSG) and a vector-based phase-locked loop (VB-PLL) with adaptive gains. AVGS allows fast detection of magnitude, frequency, and phase of the three-phase grid voltage, and enables the accurate separation of its symmetrical components meeting the transient operating requirements imposed by grid codes. The system rejects disturbances, such as voltage harmonics or imbalances, and improves transient stability with grid faults. A previous publication analyzed the small-signal stability of the synchronization system. The excellent behavior of the proposed system is verified through experimental tests carried out on a 2 MW wind power converter. The evaluation is performed in a dedicated test bench (FC PT0022) specifically designed to enable rated-power testing and high-fidelity emulation of grid disturbances.

title: Coupled PV Step-up Resonant Converter Modules with an Enhanced Efficiency Optimization Scheme for the Power Balancing Stage
authors: Kajanan Kanathipan, John Lam
abstract: An enhanced efficiency optimization control scheme for a modular photovoltaic (PV) step-up converter with embedded high frequency coupled power balancers over a wide operating range for Medium Voltage (MV) DC distribution is proposed in this article. By identifying and minimizing the duty-ratio of the lowest power module, the proposed interlinking power balancer controller optimizes the transmission of mismatched power through. This minimizes excess power transfer and power loss across each module's active voltage quadrupler (AVQ) switch which in turn enhances the overall system efficiency across a wide operating range. The proposed interlinking controller simultaneously achieves soft-switching operation on all AVQ circuits while maintaining a high step-up voltage conversion and per-module maximum power point tracking (MPPT). The configuration of the proposed system allows for additional modules to be added without requiring parameter changes to the existing system. The detailed analysis of the proposed efficiency optimization scheme and the power balancing technique of the presented modular PV converters are provided. Simulation results on a 15kW, 10kV-output (5 modules converter system) and hardware results on a scaled down proof-of-concept 1kW, 900V output (3 modules converter system) are given to highlight the features of the proposed system.

title: Linear Quadratic Integral Control with Double Integrator for Platooning System: A Numerical Study of String Stability
authors: Carlos Escobar, Luis Severino, Héctor Ramírez, Andrés Peters, Francisco Vargas
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: In this work, we study string stability in vehicle platoons based on linear quadratic integral control (LQI). We focus on platoons modeled as continuous time LTI systems under a predecessor-following configuration with a time headway spacing policy. To ensure zero steady-state tracking error, we employ the LQI technique with double integration. Through numerical simulations, we show the relationship between the time headway constant and the weighting matrices of the LQI cost functional and how these design parameters affect string stab

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 2nd World Conference on Computer and Information Security (WCCIS)

title: Research on Image Classification Based on ResNeXt and Attention Mechanisms
authors: Zifan Song
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Image classification, as a fundamental task in computer vision, serves as the backbone for operations such as object detection and image segmentation and holds substantial economic and societal significance. However, most existing image classification models face challenges including high computational costs and limited generalization ability. To address these issues, we propose a novel image classification network architecture that integrates dual attention mechanisms into ResNext. Specifically, our model incorporates both channel and spatial attention modules to assign adaptive weights to different positions and channels within feature maps, thereby suppressing irrelevant features while enhancing salient ones. Extensive comparative and ablation experiments conducted on the CIFAR-10 dataset demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed approach.

title: Graph Based Structural Anomaly Scoring and Hybrid Ranking for Audit Oriented Screening of Accounting Transaction
authors: Osahor Deborah, Lei Chen, Lixin Li, Temitope Elijah
abstract: Large-scale public sector accounting systems generate transaction records that are difficult to review efficiently using manual procedures, especially when confirmed labels for fraud or error are limited. This study analyzes <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 4, 5 0 0}$</tex> transaction line items from the Receiver General accounting transactions dataset published by the Government of Canada, covering April 2025 to January 2026. The department and voucher type bipartite graph is built to profile the patterns of concentrations, as well as to determine the month-specific structural rarity with the help of surprisal, whereas a hybrid transaction ranking is utilized to balance structural rarity with amount deviation based on circumstances to rank the candidates and subject them to review. Findings indicate that voucher-type activity is skewed whereby the best voucher type has about 40 percent of the transactions and the proportion of new department voucher relationships decreases over months in early periods to zero in the last months of the study. The hybrid ranking puts greater emphasis on structurally rare and contextually unusual transactions that are non-amount-driven regarding an amount-driven baseline, to aid interpretable screening of continuous monitoring in the case of missing or incomplete labels.

title: Kalman-Augmented Estimation for Enhancing the Reliability Analysis of Power Electronic Converters via Sensor-Error Mitigation
authors: Esther Omoyiwola, Masoud Davari
abstract: The lifetime and reliability of power electronic converters strongly depend on accurate thermal and electrical monitoring during operation. This paper presents a comprehensive reliability assessment of a buck converter system under sensor-induced uncertainties and introduces an augmented Kalman-based framework for error mitigation. A <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$12-\text{kW}$</tex>, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$300-$</tex> to-125-V buck converter is modeled in Simscape using a halfbridge IGBT-diode structure with detailed loss calculation and a Cauer-type thermal network. The monitoring subsystem records junction temperature, total losses, and load power, along with other measurements, while enforcing a shutdown above 150° C. The study investigates four distinct cases: a baseline converter with ideal sensing, an error-injected model with bias, drift, and gain distortion, a basic Kalman filter for partial correction, and an augmented Kalman filter incorporating sensor fault dynamics. The converter is modeled in MATLAB/Simulink using a coupled electro-thermal structure to evaluate steady-state performance, junction temperature, and lifetime using the Arrhenius degradation model. Results show that sensor errors can elevate the mean junction temperature by over 16° C and artificially extend lifetime prediction by more than 160%. The augmented Kalman filter successfully restores temperature and reliability estimates to within 4% of the baseline, confirming its ability to compensate for both random and systematic sensor deviations. The proposed estimation approach demonstrates a physics-consistent pathway for improving converter health monitoring and reliability prediction in systems affected by sensor degradation.

title: A Feature Space Maturity Model for ABM Frameworks: Definition and Experimentation
authors: Luis Carlos Lara-Lopez, José Helo-Guzmán, Ignacio Trejos-Zelaya, Santiago Núñez-Corrales
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Agent-Based Modeling (ABM) lacks systematic methods to evaluate frameworks and protocols to detect differences across implementations, leading to brittle models. This article addresses both challenges by introducing the Feature Space Maturity Model (FSMM), a set of numerical indicators derived from salient ABM features used to evaluate how well a given framework matches the conceptual requirements of a proposed modeling task. We demonstrate the FSMM through four canonical ABM experiments across five frameworks. Our results suggest that the combination of the FSMM and the proposed protocol can provide valuable information for both ABM framework builders and modelers along a path of complex technical and research choices.

title: DF-YOLOv11n: Dynamic Feature Fusion Lightweight Detector for Multi-Scale Targets in VisDrone Aerial Imagery
authors: Haicheng Pan
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Aerial object detection in UAV imagery remains challenging due to extreme scale variations, dense small objects, and limited onboard computational resources. To address these issues, this paper proposes a lightweight detector termed DFYOLOv11n, which integrates dynamic multi-scale feature fusion with efficient convolutional optimization. Specifically, a Dynamic Weight Allocation Module (DWAM) is introduced to reconstruct the feature pyramid, enabling adaptive reweighting of multi-level features according to scene-level responses, thereby enhancing the representation of both small and large targets. Meanwhile, depthwise separable convolutions are incorporated into the backbone and neck to significantly reduce computational overhead. Extensive experiments on the VisDrone2019-DET benchmark demonstrate that DF-YOLOv11n outperforms the baseline YOLO11n by 3.8% mAP@0.5 and 3.1% mAP@0.5:0.95, while reducing parameters by 16.7% and GFLOPs by 18.2%, achieving a favorable balance between detection accuracy and efficiency.

title: Interpretable Machine Learning for Female Fetus Chromosome Abnormality Detection in NIPT: XGBoost with Cox Regression and SHAP Analysis
authors: Hongzhou Liu, Shaobo Lin, Keyuan Yuan
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Non-invasive prenatal testing (NIPT) for female fetus chromosome abnormalities presents unique challenges due to absent Y-chromosome quality markers and severe class imbalance. We develop an interpretable machine learning framework integrating chromosome Z -score analysis, maternal characteristics, and sequencing metrics for automated trisomy detection (T13, T18, T21). Comprehensive evaluation on 137 female fetus cases demonstrates XGBoost's superior performance (AUC <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.817$</tex>, precision <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=98.6 {\%}$</tex>, recall <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=38.6 {\%}$</tex>) among seven benchmarked ensemble methods. Cox proportional hazards regression identified GC content <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\text{HR}=3.042, \mathrm{P}=0.012)$</tex> and X chromosome Z -scores <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\text{HR}=3.413, \mathrm{P}=0.005)$</tex> as independent predictors of abnormality risk, validating their clinical utility. SHAP analysis revealed DNA concentration (47% variance), chromosome 21 Z -scores, and X-chromosome metrics as primary drivers, with synergistic interactions amplifying detection sensitivity. The mechanistic transparency facilitates clinical validation, regulatory approval, and expert review of borderline cases. Our framework achieves exceptional precision essential for minimizing false alarms in population screening while addressing female-specific NIPT challenges.

title: Zoom-Aware UAV Deep Learning for Graded Bolt-Looseness Inspection of Communication Towers
authors: Tao Jiang, Mingye Jing, Ying Yu, Meng Sun, Yangyang Yu, Aqiang Li, Xiaoxing An
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Bolted joints are critical to the structural reliability of communication towers, yet progressive loosening is difficult to identify during routine inspections. UAV-based inspection improves safety and efficiency, but safe standoff flight limits the resolvability of small fasteners and outdoor conditions can destabilize recognition. This paper proposes a zoom-aware UAV deep learning framework for graded bolt-looseness inspection. A two-stage workflow first localizes candidate connection regions in wide-view imagery and then triggers zoom-guided ROI acquisition to enable close-view fastener analysis without risky close-range flight. Interpretable evidence cues, including nut rotation, washer displacement, and thread exposure, are fused into an evidencedriven score and mapped to a four-level severity scheme (L0 - L3). Robustness is enhanced through image-quality screening, temporal consistency verification, and confidence-weighted multiview fusion. To support reproducible evaluation under limited looseness annotations, a hierarchical protocol is designed using public UAV infrastructure datasets for long-range localization and public fastener benchmarks for detection and rotation-based grading. The framework is deployable and extensible to related tower defects.

title: Interference Suppression and Detection Algorithm for MIMO-OTFS in High-Mobility Scenarios
authors: Zijun Jiang, Chanfei Wang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: In high-mobility scenarios, time-varying channels and Doppler spread cause severe symbol coupling interference in multiple-input multiple-output orthogonal time frequency space (MIMO-OTFS) systems, which limits the performance of traditional linear detection methods. To address this issue, a Turbo interference cancellation detection (Turbo-IC) algorithm based on adaptive soft-decisions is proposed, which employs a progressive interference cancellation strategy to suppress inter-antenna interference, incorporates an exponentially decaying soft-decision fusion mechanism to enhance convergence speed and steadystate performance, and adopts a dynamic convergence criterion to enable early termination and reduce complexity. Simulation results show that at the mobile speed of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$300 \text{km} / \mathrm{h}$</tex>, the proposed algorithm yields better bit error rate (BER) performance than conventional methods, thereby validating its superiority in highmobility and strong-interference environments.

title: Performance Evaluation of Hardware Architectures for Convolutional Neural Networks
authors: Vincent Truong, Dali Wang
abstract: This paper presents a benchmark study of convolutional neural network (CNN) inference performance across CPU, GPU, and TPU accelerators. Four representative CNN architectures - VGG-16, AlexNet, ResNet-50, and EfficientNet - are evaluated on the ImageNet validation set (50,000 images) with respect to throughput, latency, and power consumption. The evaluated platforms include three generations of NVIDIA GPUs (T4, L4, and A100), two generations of Google Cloud TPUs (v5e and v6e), and two Intel Xeon CPUs (4C and 6C). The results indicate that TPU v6e achieves the highest inference throughput and energy efficiency. Among GPUs, the Tesla T4 attains throughput comparable to or exceeding that of the A100 for lightweight models while operating at lower power, whereas the L4 exhibits the most consistent power efficiency across workloads. CPU-based inference remains practical for lightweight models such as AlexNet and EfficientNet but becomes significantly less competitive for deeper and more compute-intensive architectures. Overall, the findings demonstrate that inference performance and energy efficiency vary substantially with both model complexity and hardware characteristics, and that theoretical peak compute (FLOPs) alone is an unreliable predictor of real-world inference behavior, where memory bandwidth and data movement often constrain achievable performance.

title: Computer-Aided Innovative Design of “Tai Tai Hua” Motifs in Xiangxi Tujia Brocade
authors: Yue Sun
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: As a quintessential and symbolic element of Xiangxi Tujia brocade, the “Tai Tai Hua” motif encapsulates profound cultural heritage. To tackle the innovation stagnation in its contemporary preservation, this study focuses on the “Tai Tai Hua” patterns through a computational lens. Initially, we systematically explored its historical lineage and stable formal logic. Subsequently, the complex patterns were deconstructed into fundamental unit motifs. By leveraging Shape Grammar, these units underwent an innovative deductive process to establish a set of parametric and computable generation rules. Finally, the proposed model was implemented in design practice. Through rule-driven parameter manipulation, we generated a series of innovative motifs that not only safeguard the cultural “DNA” of the original brocade but also align with modern aesthetics. This research indicates that Shape Grammar facilitates a transformative bridge for traditional patterns, moving from “intuitive experience” toward “algorithmic generation,” providing a fresh methodology for the living inheritance of Tujia motifs.

title: Integrating Automated Accessibility Checks in Web and Mobile Workflows
authors: Soumya Karnala
abstract: In the development of modern software, digital accessibility is a critical requirement. When developing and testing mobile applications, it is essential to consider individuals with disabilities. This research paper will examine the importance of integrating accessibility tools into the development process. The study integrates automated accessibility evaluation tools across web, mobile, and hybrid platforms to assess compliance with Web Content Accessibility Guidelines(WCAG) 2.1 standards. Chromebased accessibility extensions were used during early development stages, while underlying automated testing engines, including aXecore, Web Accessibility Evaluation Tool (WAVE), and Lighthouse, supported large-scale and cross-platform analysis. Automated results were complemented by manual audits and user testing to identify limitations related to dynamic content and cognitive accessibility. Findings demonstrate that early integration of accessibility testing reduces remediation effort and supports more inclusive digital design practices. Manual testing methodologies and automated analysis reveal gaps in current practices, such as dynamic content issues and the detection of cognitive accessibility barriers. The findings of this research indicate that “shifting left” is advantageous in accessibility testing, as it reduces remediation costs. The paper also provides recommendations for a hybrid testing framework that involves user testing and automated tools, with expert evaluation. The purpose of these recommendations is to enhance compliance with comprehensive accessibility. Through these insights, developers gain valuable guidance, enabling them to implement accessibility testing protocols while optimizing resource allocation.

title: Filling the Gaps in System Monitoring – Zombie Exporter: A Prometheus Exporter for Zombie Process Detection
authors: Timothy W. Tron, Abdelbaset Hamza
abstract: In academic labs, novice programmers frequently create zombie processes, with daily accumulations of 15-20 processes and deadline peaks exceeding 500. Left unmonitored, zombies can exhaust the process table and disrupt services. Prometheus Node Exporter (NE) exposes per-process-state counts via an optional processes collector (e.g., node_processes_state{state=“Z”}). However, this signal is (i) disabled by default, (ii) low-cardinality (no PID/PPID/command labels), and (iii) lacks first-class node identity (e.g., a stable node_ip label) in the emitted series. In practice, the only locator is the scrape-derived instance label, which is brittle across DNS changes, NAT, and relabeling and is insufficient for automated attribution and remediation workflows. We present Zombie Exporter (ZE), a lightweight Prometheus-compatible exporter that emits a labeled, stabilized metric including the host IP, enabling reliable attribution and policy actions. In production use, ZE maintained negligible overhead and detected zombie presence within typical scrape intervals. Consequently, we adopt ZE as the primary signal and treat NE as a secondary crosscheck.

title: Virtual Screening and Explainability Analysis of GAPDH-Siah1 Protein-Protein Interaction Inhibitors Using Ligand-Based Fingerprints and LightGBM
authors: Xun Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: This study aims to develop a machine learning model based on molecular fingerprints and a gradient boosting decision tree algorithm for the efficient virtual screening of small molecule inhibitors targeting the GAPDH-Siah1 protein-protein interaction (PPI). Diverging from structure-based approaches that rely on docking poses, this research utilizes extended-connectivity fingerprints (ECFP4) to encode the detailed topological and chemical features of small molecules into feature vectors. We employed these features to train a LightGBM classifier, which demonstrated exceptional performance in distinguishing potential inhibitors from decoy molecules. Through SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) analysis, we elucidated the key chemical substructures critical for inhibitory activity. The methodology presented in this study offers an efficient and highly interpretable new strategy for virtual screening campaigns targeting specific PPIs.

title: SMR: A Shrink-Map-Release Algorithm for Global Path Planning in Autonomous Driving
authors: Tian Guo, Kailin Chen, Wensheng Yu, Ling Fan
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: In autonomous driving scenarios, conventional global path planning algorithms often suffer from low computational efficiency due to ambiguous road network structures and vast search spaces. This paper proposes a Shrink-Map-Release (SMR) algorithm for global path planning, which reduces the graph scale through node contraction and topological simplification prior to querying, subsequently performing rapid pathfinding on the reduced graph and expanding the results into the original path. Experiments conducted on both 46-node and 120-node road networks demonstrate that SMR significantly reduces node expansions and planning time while maintaining optimal path costs. These results validate the efficiency, feasibility, and scalability of the SMR algorithm for global path planning.

title: Design and Implementation of an Automated Optical Inspection System for Electronic Components in Custom PCB Manufacture
authors: Guillermo Campos, Pablo Pérez, Sebastián Barker, Cristian Muñoz
abstract: This paper describes the process of implementation of a computer vision method for optical inspection applied to PCB (Printed Circuit Board) manufacturing during the quality control using comparison and defect analysis on electronic circuit boards, focusing on detecting missing components, displacements and solder joint defects on a controlled environment with fixed lighting and positioning. Experimental results obtained from multiple test cases are presented to evaluate the reliability of the system in identifying manufacturing errors.

title: Neural-Network-Based Modeling of Three-Phase Grid-Forming Inverters
authors: Jacob Levesque, Dingrui Li
abstract: As the power grid becomes increasingly reliant on inverter-based resources (IBRs), grid-forming (GFM) inverters will become increasingly important in maintaining grid voltage and frequency. Accurate models of GFM inverters are critical for power grid planning, though these models are usually not provided by inverter vendors. There has been much work on modeling for grid-following inverters, but less so for GFM inverters. As such, this paper proposes a neural-network-based (NN-based) approach to modeling GFM inverters. In this model, inverter output voltages are selected as the model outputs, and different input current structures are compared for the model development. It is shown that using the filter inductor current together with the load current as the inputs results in the highest accuracy. Moreover, both steady-state and dynamic scenarios are considered in the developed model. The developed model accuracy is verified by comparing with the electromagnetic transient (EMT) simulation results in MATLAB Simulink, achieving similar results up to 200 times faster.

title: Independently Controllable Triple Pass Band Filter with Defective Ground Surface for Wireless Applications
authors: Debjit Kar, Jayee Majumdar, Pradipta Maiti, Arindam Sadhu, Anirban Neogi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: One triple band pass filter with central pass band frequencies at 2.5, 5.3, 3.7 and 9 GHz is proposed in this article for the Wireless applications like Wireless Local Area Network, WiMax and Satellite Communication. Two types of resonator were used in designing of the filter, one as microstrip resonator and another one as Defective ground Surface (DGS) slot resonator. The DGS resonator is responsible for generating first to pass band, where as the third pass band is created by the microstrip resonator. The simulations of the proposed filters are carried out with HFSS13 software. Arlon AD 250 with dielectric Constant 2.5 is used as substrate. The miniaturized size of the filters is (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$27.95 ~\text{mm} \times 15 ~\text{mm}$</tex>). The in- band Insertion Losses (IL) filter are 0.2, 0.3 and 0.8 dB respectively.

title: Dynamic Multi-Scale Hybrid Framework for EEG-Driven Chinese Speech Imagery
authors: Weiran Xu, Lei Cao
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Weiran Xu College of Information Engineering, Shanghai Maritime University Shanghai, China 202430310254@stu.shmtu.edu.cn Abstract-Electroencephalography (EEG)-based speech imagery decoding has attracted increasing attention due to its potential to enable natural language communication for individuals with speech impairments. However, EEG signals are inherently weak, highly non-stationary, and easily contaminated by noise, posing significant challenges for accurate feature representation and decoding-particularly for tonal and logographic languages such as Chinese. To address these challenges, we propose a Dynamic Multi-Scale Hybrid Network (DMHN) that integrates a U-Net backbone for hierarchical spatiotemporal feature extraction, a multi-scale convolutional module for adaptive dynamic feature fusion, and a Transformer unit for modeling global dependencies. The proposed framework effectively combines local fine-grained representations with longrange contextual information. Experiments conducted on our Chinese speech imagery dataset demonstrate that DMHN consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in terms of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. Ablation studies reveal the complementary contributions of the U-Net and multi-scale convolutional modules to the overall decoding performance. These results highlight the potential of DMHN as a robust and efficient framework for advancing EEG-based speech imagery decoding.

title: Intelligent Prediction and Feature Analysis of Ceramic Flexural Strength Using Interpretable Machine Learning Approaches
authors: Ruizheng Chen
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Advanced ceramic materials require precise control of mechanical properties through optimization of chemical composition and processing parameters. Traditional empirical approaches are time-consuming and resource-intensive when exploring vast compositional spaces. This study proposes a machine learning framework integrating six algorithms (SVR, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, CatBoost, ANN) with SHAP interpretability analysis for predicting flexural strength in multi-component oxide ceramics. Experiments on 8,000 samples demonstrate that XGBoost achieves superior accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathrm{R}^{2}=0.949$</tex>, RMSE =57.043 MPa). SHAP analysis reveals density, porosity, and grain size as dominant predictors, while partial dependence plots elucidate complex feature interactions following Hall-Petch relationships and densification principles. This interpretable framework enables rational design of ceramic materials with targeted mechanical performance.

title: An Enhanced Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)-Guided Hybrid Planner for Unmanned Aerial Vehicle Path Planning in Complex Environments
authors: Muhammad Zain Chaudhary, Ammar Ahmed, Abdullah Ahmed, Muhammad Abdullah Shaikh, Ahmed Sherif, Rabab Abdelfattah
abstract: Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) are currently essential for disaster response, environmental monitoring, logistics, precision agriculture, and surveillance. UAVs must plan paths that are globally efficient, locally responsive, and kinematically smooth in cluttered, dynamic spaces. Artificial intelligence (AI) methods, especially deep reinforcement learning (DRL), have shown promise in reducing brittle hand-tuning while complementing classical planners such as A*, which remains a reliable global backbone. We introduce a DRL-guided hybrid that supervises an A* global planner and a dynamic window approach (DWA) local navigator by reweighting the A* heuristic, managing waypoint sub-goals, and adaptively tuning DWA's scoring terms from real-time risk features; this learned meta-control replaces fixed gains and outperforms prior A* + DWA fusions and end-to-end DRL by retaining deterministic guarantees while adding context-aware adaptability. Our performance evaluations show that our DRL-guided planner reduces pathfinding runtime by over 55% and total mission time by 24% while preserving safety. Trajectories are markedly smoother, showing a 24.2% reduction in total corner degree, which better matches UAV kinematic constraints. Additionally, we observe consistent improvements in obstacle clearance and fewer evasive dead-ends than a tuned A* + DWA baseline. Replanning latency is lower across all dynamic scenarios, yielding faster recovery from environmental changes.

title: A Mathematical Framework for Wise AI
authors: Santiago Grijalva, Nicholas Grijalva
abstract: As artificial intelligence systems become increasingly autonomous and consequential, ensuring they make not merely accurate but genuinely wise decisions has emerged as a critical challenge. This paper presents a framework for Wise AI—systems that integrate short-term and long-term utility optimization with ethical reasoning, safety constraints, and long-term value preservation. Unlike conventional AI that maximizes narrow performance metrics, our framework formalizes stochastic multi-objective optimization with hard ethical constraints, domain-specific operational constraints, epistemic uncertainty quantification, and human-in-the-loop mechanisms. We introduce the wisdom function to balance these objectives subject to inviolable safety constraints, problem-specific resource constraints, and uncertainty thresholds. We propose methods for estimating ethical alignment and long-term value using both domain-specific metrics and reinforcement learning. Through simulation across six diverse scenarios—hospital resource allocation, autonomous vehicle collision avoidance, financial trading, social media content moderation, medical diagnosis, and hiring algorithms—we illustrate how Wise AI can achieve higher wisdom scores while maintaining perfect safety compliance. This work provides a framework for developing AI systems aligned with human values and societal welfare.

title: Developing a Modular Framework for IEC 61850 Compliant Communication and Data Modeling in Smart Photovoltaic (PV) Power Plants
authors: Yessica Monges, Maiara Palacios, Enrique Buzarquis, Sonia Silvera
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: The growing adoption of photovoltaic energy is transforming power generation and distribution by driving decentralized supply schemes, while introducing new challenges in real-time supervision, control and response. In Paraguay, the 2040 generation master plan promotes Non-Conventional Renewable Energies to enhance grid security and efficiency, and the IEC 61850 standard has demonstrated its versatility in managing Distributed Energy Resources, including photovoltaic systems. However, previous studies have focused on laboratory prototypes or simulations and have not comprehensively evaluated field performance. This paper presents an IEC 61850 integration model applied to a field-testing buoy implementing a real photovoltaic system, measuring communication performance and response times via a real-time SCADA monitoring platform.

title: Post-Earthquake Structural Damage Classification from Ground-Level Imagery Using Transfer-Learning Convolutional Neural Networks
authors: Aditi Roy, Anandi Dutta
abstract: Rapid identification of structural damage following an earthquake is critical for emergency response and community resilience. Manual inspections are reliable but often slow, labor intensive, and hazardous in dense urban environments. This study presents an applied benchmarking framework for automated post-earthquake damage classification from ground-level imagery using a curated subset of the Image Database for Earthquake Damage Annotation (IDEA) dataset with expert-annotated field photographs. Two classical baselines were evaluated, including logistic regression using global pixel-intensity statistics and Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG) features with a linear Support Vector Machine (SVM). These were compared with three transfer-learning Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) backbones, MobileNetV2, ResNet50, and EfficientNetB0, under a consistent train/validation/test split. Light data augmentation, class-weighting for imbalance, and a two-stage fine-tuning strategy were applied. Performance was assessed using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices. The transferlearning CNNs outperformed the logistic regression baseline, with ResNet50 achieving approximately 0.983 test accuracy, while the HOG plus SVM baseline provided a strong classical comparator with an accuracy of 0.976. The results support the feasibility of data-driven methods for rapid post-earthquake damage screening from ground-level photographs.

title: Robust Socioeconomic Community Discovery from Census-Derived Indicators via KNN Graphs and Louvain Stability–Conductance Analysis
authors: Farahnaz Hosseini, Ting Xiao
abstract: Community detection on graphs constructed from census-derived indicators offers an interpretable way to uncover latent socioeconomic structure among geographic units. However, modularity-based partitions can be sensitive to graph construction and hyperparameter choices, and heuristic optimizers such as Louvain may yield different solutions across random seeds. We propose a robustness-first pipeline that (i) preprocesses and standardizes census features, (ii) constructs an undirected <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k$</tex>-nearest-neighbor (KNN) similarity graph, (iii) applies Louvain community detection across sweeps of neighborhood size <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k$</tex> and resolution <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\gamma$</tex>, and (iv) assesses reliability using modularity (structural quality), run-to-run stability via ARI/NMI, and conductance (boundary sharpness). In a ZIP-code case study with multi-domain socioeconomic indicators, we observe that smaller neighborhood sizes tend to increase modularity, whereas stability peaks at moderate connectivity. Finally, we interpret the resulting communities through socioeconomic profiles (income, rent, poverty, diversity, age, and density) and report conductance values to quantify separation between groups.

title: Efficient AI-Powered Decision Support Systems: A Framework for Scalable Real-Time Predictions
authors: Krishnam Raju Narsepalle, Sreepal Reddy Bolla, Revanth Reddy Bandaru
abstract: The growing complexity of making decisions in real-time dynamic environments necessitates the development of efficient AI-driven Decision Support Systems (DSS) for realtime predictions. In this context, the paper introduces a direction towards a new framework based on scalable approaches using state-of-the-art machine learning models, edge computing methods, cloud-based systems, which can speed up the process while improving the overall performance and accuracy of the decisions. In this context, the proposed framework allows for two crucial elements: processing large amounts of data close to real-time and leveraging AI analytics to not only detect but also predict patterns while being responsive with low-latency and scalable. Here, this study presents a model selection strategy that minimizes computational cost in favor of predictive performance, enabling ease of implementation through numerous industrial settings. We illustrate the effectiveness of our framework on both real-world datasets and experimental evaluations covering applications in healthcare, financial services, and smart cities, showing high accuracy and timely results. The findings illustrate programmatic possibilities for leveraging an AI-enabled decision support system to drive impact in data-driven decision-making and improve real-time operational performance.

title: SMOTE-Enhanced Random Forest Classifier with Bayesian Optimization for Lung Cancer Prediction
authors: Ritwika Mukherjee, Ashifuddin Mondal, Arnanil Mitra, Arko Pal, Sankhyayan Dey, Sourayan Sur
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: Lung cancer is one of the most common diseases among humans and one of the major causes of growing mortality. Most of Lung Cancer symptoms can be noticed and the cancer can be predicted by just people's behaviours and places of residence. Due to the advancement of Machine Learning, medical fields utilize this to predict many diseases based upon early symptoms. In this paper, a predictive model is developed for lung cancer classification using clinical and demographic attributes obtained from structured dataset. Due to data class imbalance, Synthetic Minority Over-Sampling Technique (SMOTE) is used to mitigate class imbalance. As the primary classifier, the Random Forest (RF) Classifier is used due to its efficiency and ability to handle mixed feature types. Optuna's Bayesian optimization framework was used for hyperparameter optimization. The final model's training incorporates five-fold cross-validation based on the macro F1-score to ensure model reliability and mitigate overfitting. The simulation result shows that, the RF performs better than other models in terms of accuracy, precision, recall and F1-score with a value of 0.90, 0.90, 0.89 and 0.90 respectively. Furthermore, feature importance analysis is performed to identify risk factors influencing prediction.

title: Nonlinear Model Predictive Control N-MPC for a Conical Tank
authors: Luis Enrique Vargas Paredes, Karina Belen Montaluisa Herrera, Jacqueline Del Rocio Llanos Proaño, Paola Velasco
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Model predictive control (MPC) uses a model of the plant to predict its future behavior therefore, to ensure good performance it is important to consider a model as close to the real plant as possible. In this context, in this research work, a nonlinear predictive (N-MPC) is proposed that seeks to minimize the steady-state error while avoiding severe actions on the actuator considering plant operating restrictions, to solve the optimization problem, a particle swarm optimizer (PSO) is considered. To demonstrate the efficiency of the controller, a comparison is made with a linear MPC, set-point changes and disturbances are evaluated, demonstrating better performance of the N-MPC with a 13 % reduction in overshot and 67 % of the settling time, the tests are performed at different operating points.

title: An Improved YOLO-Based Object Detection Framework for Dense Small Targets in VisDrone Scenarios
authors: Tingyu Wang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Object detection in drone-captured scenes (represented by the VisDrone dataset) is constrained by challenges including dense small targets, complex background clutter, and scale variation. To address these issues, this paper proposes an enhanced YOLO-based framework (named YOLO-Drone) optimized for drone-specific visual tasks. The framework redesigns three core modules: 1) A SPPF-enhanced CSPDarknet Backbone to strengthen multi-scale feature representation; 2) A bidirectional feature fusion Neck (PANet+) with adaptive up/down-sampling to complement shallow fine-grained features and deep semantic features; 3) A scale-aware YOLO-Head with adjusted anchor boxes tailored to VisDrone target distributions. Extensive experiments are conducted on the VisDrone2021-DET dataset: YOLO-Drone achieves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$34.2 \% \text{mAP}$</tex> (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.5: 0.95$</tex>) and 42 FPS on a single NVIDIA RTX 3090 GPU, outperforming state-of-the-art methods (e.g., YOLOv7, Faster R-CNN) by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.8-5.1 \%$</tex> in mAP while maintaining real-time inference. Ablation studies verify that each module contributes to performance gains, especially for small targets (mAP improvement of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{6. 7 \%}$</tex> for targets <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<\mathbf{3 2} \times \mathbf{3 2}$</tex> pixels). This work provides an efficient solution for practical drone-based object detection tasks.

title: Factors Impacting Performance of Multiclass Image Classification: A Study Using Mars Images
authors: Samarth Das, Venkata Shaurya Mantrala, Ravanasamudram N. Uma
abstract: In image classification problems, such as classifying martian images into classes with science and engineering interests, we observe that the performance of a pre-trained convolutional neural network (CNN) model such as ResNet50 deteriorates when moving from a binary classification problem to a multiclass classification problem. Through experimentation, we identify the factors affecting the performance of multiclass classification problems: number of classes, sample size, and the degree of class imbalance in the dataset measured using Shannon Equitability Index. We also derive conditions under which the balanced accuracy metric could exceed the accuracy metric in a binary classification problem.

title: Control Strategy for a STATCOM Under Dynamic Load Implemented in MATLAB/Simulink
authors: S. Martinez, A. Peña, L. Moreno, G. Campos, P. Pérez
abstract: This paper presents a control strategy implemented for a Static Synchronous Compensator (STATCOM) with an NPC topology, focused on independent active and reactive power control via Clarke and Park transforms (abc → dq). A cascaded control scheme is used where the outer loop regulates the DC-bus voltage and the inner loop controls dq-axis currents using PI controllers. Additionally, a capacitor voltage balancing system for the DC bus and a PLL-based grid synchronization logic are proposed and included to ensure stable and efficient connection under dynamic load conditions.

title: KubeNetLLM: An Architectural Framework for Context-Aware Kubernetes Network Configuration Using LLMS and MCP
authors: Sonika Arora, Prashanth Josyula, Ankit Rajput
abstract: As organizations scale their containerized infrastructures, managing Kubernetes network configurations has become increasingly complex, with configuration-related issues accounting for 67% of production outages. This paper introduces KubeNetLLM, a comprehensive architectural framework that automates Kubernetes network configuration generation by leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) enhanced with the Model Context Protocol (MCP). Our approach features a sophisticated four-component architecture with hierarchical validation, successfully generating deployable configurations across our test scenarios. Through extensive experiments on real Kubernetes clusters, we demonstrate that KubeNetLLM reduces configuration time from hours to an average of 10.5 seconds while eliminating configuration errors. The framework processes natural language requirements using locally-deployed LLMs (Ollama with llama3.2:3b), consuming only 2,809 tokens across five experimental scenarios. We present a novel MCP-based architecture designed for context-aware configuration, though our current implementation uses simulated MCP responses. The framework includes comprehensive validation, security analysis, and demonstrates the feasibility of natural language-driven infrastructure automation while establishing architectural patterns for future context-aware systems.

title: Identifying Affective Engagement States in Digital Learning Using Synchronized Electroencephalography
authors: Menekse Adar, Serkan Varol, Zack Ridder
abstract: Understanding affective engagement is important in digital learning environments. This study investigates emotional engagement states using electroencephalography (EEG) data collected during online learning activities. EEG signals were recorded from seven undergraduate engineering students while they interacted with four courses that followed different instructional structures. Affective engagement was represented using EEG-derived proxies for valence and arousal, computed from frontal alpha asymmetry and beta–alpha ratios. Temporal patterns, statistical distributions, and spectral band-power maps were analyzed at both participant and course levels. The results show that different course structures are associated with distinct affective engagement patterns. Case-based and chronological courses exhibit higher arousal variability, while structured modular courses show more stable engagement. These findings suggest that instructional design influences learners' affective states and highlight the potential of EEG-based analysis for evaluating digital learning experiences.

title: Building Information Modeling as a Strategic Tool to Implement PV+BESS Microgrids That Enhance Grid Resilience and Sustainability: A Case Study in the World Heritage Island of Fernando De Noronha, Brazil
authors: N/A
abstract: This paper presents an extensive case study on the application of Building Information Modeling (BIM) in the development of the Noronha Verde Project (where “verde” means “green” in Portuguese), a 22.82 MWp photovoltaic complex combined with a Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) located on Fernando de Noronha Island, Brazil. The project posed significant challenges due to its remote location, 350 km off the Brazilian coast, its environmental sensitivity as a UNESCO World Heritage site, and complex logistical constraints. The study explores how BIM methodology addresses these challenges in remote renewable energy projects, focusing on risk mitigation, interdisciplinary coordination, environmental impact analysis, and resource optimization. A federated modeling strategy was implemented across three hierarchical levels, supported by cloud-based collaboration through Autodesk Construction Cloud, which served as the Common Data Environment (CDE). 3D coordination tools, automatic clash detection, and more accurate quantity estimates helped identify issues earlier and improved the predictability of the project. Early identification of environmental conflicts—such as flood—prone areas detected during the basic design phase-helped prevent costly delays. The methodology also enabled effective coordination among geographically dispersed teams and ensured precise material specifications, which are critical in logistically constrained environments. Despite these benefits, the study identified challenges such as resistance to methodological changes, uneven BIM expertise among stakeholders, software interoperability issues, and the need for accurate baseline data. Nevertheless, results confirm BIM's strong viability and substantial advantages in remote and environmentally sensitive contexts. The paper concludes that BIM should be highly recommended for renewable energy projects in isolated areas, particularly those with complex environmental constraints and distributed teams. Furthermore, the research highlights the promising potential of integrating BIM with Digital Twin technologies to extend benefits beyond design and construction into operation and maintenance of remote energy infrastructure.

title: Greedy-Cover and Greedy-Demand: Two Enhanced Algorithms for the Maximum Happy Set Problem
authors: Sini Song, Weidong Chen
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Within social network analysis, the Maximum Happy Set (MaxHS) problem—first introduced in 2021 and proven NP-hard—aims to select a fixed-size vertex subset that maximizes the number of “happy” vertices: those whose closed neighborhoods are entirely contained within the chosen subset. Though relevant to network optimization tasks, research on MaxHS remains limited; existing greedy methods, while simple, prioritize immediate gains and often produce suboptimal solutions by overlooking vertices' potential future contributions. Leveraging the well-established efficiency of greedy algorithms in combinatorial optimization, this paper refines the state-of-the-art Greedy-Pick framework with two enhanced approaches. First, Greedy-Cover introduces a cover-degree metric to resolve ties among vertices with identical minimum need-degree, optimizing resource utilization in selection. Second, Greedy-Demand employs a dynamic demand-degree function to quantify each vertex's potential to boost future happy vertex counts, enabling more forward-looking choices. Extensive experiments on 21 real-world social network datasets show that both algorithms outperform Greedy-Pick: Greedy-Cover improves the number of happy vertices by 4.7% on average, while Greedy-Demand achieves a 19.7% average gain—validating their ability to elevate solution quality over conventional greedy strategies.
