title: Development of an ECG Phantom Prototype for Educational and Training Applications
authors: Togo Arredondo, Antonio Rienzo, Valeria Muñoz
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Given the importance of training and educating medical students and other healthcare professionals, this paper presents the foundation for and describes the design and development of a prototype ECG phantom for teaching purposes, based on a previous project by the authors. The ECG signal and the leads required for proper electrode placement and accurate interpretation of cardiac rhythms are explained in detail. The methodology used is explained; the project was developed in four stages: prototype analysis, mobile solution design, application implementation, and system validation. The electrocardiographic signals used in this project were extracted from PhysioBank ATM, a public database containing ECG signal records. The results, functionality, and performance tests are presented, with a detailed discussion of all the work performed. Conclusions and future work are highlighted. Conclusions and future work are highlighted.

title: Ultrafast Infant Brain Quantitative MRI Using Overlapping-Echo Acquisition with Volumetric Physical Simulation of Slice-level Non-Idealities
authors: Nuowei Ge, Qinqin Yang, Junying Cheng, Yanan Ren, Yong Zhang, Zhong Chen, Qin Lin, Jianfeng Bao, Congbo Cai, Shuhui Cai
abstract: Objective: Quantitative MRI (qMRI) is sensitive to brain microstructural and metabolic changes; however, existing techniques often unsuitable for assessing postnatal brain development due to prolonged scan time, non-ideal imaging conditions, and severe infant motion. In this study, we propose a robust qMRI framework tailored for infant brain imaging to address abovementioned limitations. Methods: The proposed framework integrates multiple overlapping-echo detachment (MOLED) acquisition and synthetic data-driven deep learning quantitative reconstruction. To overcome non-ideal imaging conditions, a volumetric physical simulation (VoluSimu) method was proposed to generate physics-informed synthetic training data, ultimately enabling robust T2, T2 *, and T2' mapping of infant brain. Numerical and phantom experiments were used to validate the quantitative accuracy and robustness of proposed framework under various non-ideal imaging conditions. Additionally, we applied the framework to a cohort of 30 infants (0-1 year), obtaining their T2, T2 *, and T2' maps to quantitatively characterize early brain development. Results: Validation studies demonstrated that, despite imperfect RF pulses and B0 inhomogeneity, network trained on VoluSimu-generated synthetic data provided accurate quantitative results. In in vivo study, MOLED-derived maps were free of motion artifacts. T2 and T2 * values showed mono-exponential age-related changes, while T2' values decreased linearly in white matter and cortical gray matter. Conclusion and Significance: The proposed framework enables high-fidelity, ultrafast infant brain qMRI, overcoming challenges posed by non-ideal imaging conditions and motion. The resulting quantitative parameters offer valuable insights into microstructural and metabolic changes associated with brain maturation, demonstrating the potential of the proposed framework for longitudinal monitoring of early brain development.

title: Synergistic Composite Electron Transport Layer and Interfacial Engineering for High-Efficiency and Stable Perovskite Solar Cells_supp1-3677493.docx
authors: Akbar Ali Qureshi
abstract: The Organic-inorganic perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have achieved remarkable power conversion efficiencies (PCEs), yet their commercialization remains limited by interfacial recombination losses and insufficient operational stability. Here, we present a dual-interface engineering strategy that synergistically combines a Fe2O3-WO3 composite electron transport layer (ETL) with an ultrathin piperazine dihydriodide interlayer (IL) to address these challenges. The Fe2O3-WO3 composite, prepared via solution processing, integrates the stability and deep conduction band of Fe2O3 with the high electron mobility of WO3, forming uniform, transparent, and compact films that facilitate efficient electron extraction and suppressed recombination. Meanwhile, the IL at the perovskite/hole transport interface passivates surface defects, introduces interfacial dipoles for optimized energy-level alignment, and inhibits ion migration. Together, these modifications improve perovskite film morphology, reduce trap density, and enable superior charge transport. Devices incorporating composite ETL and IL achieved a champion PCE of 18.46% with a VOC of 1.14 V, JSC of 21.82 mA·cm−2, and FF of 74.23%, outperforming single-oxide counterparts. Moreover, the dual-engineered devices exhibited enhanced stability, retaining ∼84% of their initial efficiency under prolonged operation. This work demonstrates that composite ETLs combined with molecular interfacial passivation provide a scalable pathway to efficient, stable, and reproducible PSCs.

title: Zero-Ripple Converter: Membrane Protection in Electrolyzers and Pem Fuel Cells
authors: Sebastián Riffo, Natalia Soto, Catalina González-Castaño, Carlos Restrepo
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Currently, the use of fossil fuels has led to a significant i ncrease int he g lobal a verage t emperature, resulting in serious environmental consequences. In response to this issue, the use of renewable energy sources has been widely explored, with solar and wind energy standing out. In Chile, green hydrogen is also considered a key future energy vector. However, its production and utilization require power converters. These converters typically generate a triangularshaped current ripple that can negatively impact both fuel cells and electrolyzers due to the chemical reactions involved and the sensitivity of the materials used in their construction, which are particularly affected by high-frequency ripples. In this context, the present article proposes a power converter with zero current ripple at both its input and output, aiming to prevent premature degradation of fuel cells and electrolyzers in green hydrogen applications.

title: XAI-Driven Root Cause Analysis (XAI + RCA)
authors: Raymi Vásquez M, Werner Kristjanpoller, Grinda Sierra G
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: We present XAI-RCA, a practical framework that couples explainable AI with Root Cause Analysis to make RUL-driven maintenance decisions auditable in industrial settings. A compact Transformer-based model estimates Remaining Useful Life (RUL) on NASA̧s C-MAPSS (FD001-FD004), and post-hoc explanations are used to bridge predictions with engineering insight. Beyond SHAP, we discuss when Integrated Gradients (IG) and Layer-wise Relevance Propagation (LRP) are preferable (e.g., robustness vs. compute), and how explanation rankings can be mapped to RCA branches (HPC/LPT/Bleed/Operating Conditions) to prioritize inspections. Results show competitive predictive performance across subsets (e.g., R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>≈0.79 in FD001 and R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>≈0.32 in FD004), while explanations consistently highlight pressures and temperatures in intermediate stages (PS30, T30, T50) and bleed-related variables (W31/W32, htBleed) as early failure precursors. The paper details practical choices. background sets for SHAP, local vs. global views, and windowing near failure thresholds.and analyzes the accuracy.interpretability trade-off for time-series PHM. Overall, XAI-RCA preserves predictive performance and turns model attributions into actionable failure hypotheses, improving traceability and decision-making for maintenance teams.

title: An Adaptive EKF-enhanced Hybrid Flux Observer for Position Sensorless SynRM Drives with Stator Resistance Adaptation
authors: Fengtao Gao, Yanyong Yang, Yanping Zhang, Pinjia Zhang, Zhonggang Yin
abstract: Position sensorless synchronous reluctance motor (SynRM) drives offer a reliable and cost-effective solution for electrified transportation systems. However, their position estimation accuracy degrades under uncertainty noise interference and stator resistance mismatch. In this paper, the SynRM position estimation method based on adaptive extended Kalman filtering-enhanced hybrid flux observer (AEKF-HFO) is proposed, which combines the wide speed range of HFO with the strong anti-interference ability of Kalman filtering. A flux prediction equation combining the current and voltage models is developed for designing an EKF-based flux observer, in which an online stator resistance adaptation scheme is integrated to enhance estimation consistency under the parameter variations. To alleviate the problem of position estimation accuracy decline caused by stator resistance estimation error at low speed, a structured Sage-Husa adaptation scheme is developed. The proposed method differentiates flux-and resistance-related components in the system noise covariance matrix and propagates the uncertainty of stator resistance into the flux subspace through sensitivity analysis. In addition, the stability of the proposed method is demonstrated in details. The experimental results show that the proposed method has superior position estimation accuracy and robustness under challenging conditions including noise interference, parameter mismatches, and inverter nonlinearities.

title: Analysis of Bursting Oscillations in a TiO 2- x Memristive Neuron Circuit
authors: Fang Yuan, Shu Peng, Yue Deng, Yuxia Li, Guangyi Wang, Herbert Ho-Ching Iu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: In the nervous systems, neural bursting signals, as a non-random and specific discharge pattern, play an irreplaceable and critical role in information encoding and neural regulation. In this study, an active memristor was designed and constructed by integrating TiO2-x nano-memristors with a negative resistance circuit, which was subsequently applied to a neuron circuit for an in-depth analysis of various bursting phenomena and the underlying mechanisms of action potential signal generation. The research findings indicate that external excitation and activity are pivotal in modulating burst behaviors. Furthermore, the experiment demonstrates that adjusting the duty cycle of the input voltage enables effective control and regulation of the discharge behaviors in the neural circuit. Finally, the hardware experimental results corroborate the accuracy and reliability of both numerical simulations and theoretical analyses.

title: Digital Security Awareness and its Impact on the Sustainability of Digital Marketing Operations: A field study on the OpenSooq platform in Iraq
authors: Hussein muhammad Ali Kashkool, Mohammed nabeel Hadi Haboobi, Nidhal abdullah Yassen Al-maliki, Zain Mohammed Al-Hamami, Haider adel Ahmed Khudair al-saadi
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 2nd International Conference on Computing and Emerging Sciences
abstract: None

title: Secure and Offline-Capable Enterprise CRM Using On-Device Large Language Models
authors: Vijaya Sai Munduru
abstract: Recent trends in moving CRM systems into cloud-based infrastructures create serious bottlenecks concerning latency, data privacy, and offline access of field professionals. We present an architectural framework that describes on-device deployment of quantized LLMs for seamless, offline-capable CRM. A compressed model version of LLaMA-2-7B was fine-tuned using QLoRA techniques on 427 unique enterprise sales interaction logs. 1 For model deployment, PyTorch Mobile was used, optimized inference utilizes ONNX Runtime, and model orchestration is done through LangChain. Our results show that 4-bit quantization combined with hardware-aware pruning can execute these models locally on-device, executing intent recognition at 94% of full-precision cloud models while reducing the inference latency by 60% and removing data egress vulnerabilities. This work confirms the possibility of executing complex CRM tasks like meeting summarization, sentiment analysis, and automatic entry generation on locally run standard enterprise-grade tablets and smartphones. It sets a new bar for secure, resilient enterprise mobile computing and will ensure productivity on the go, independent of network conditions.

title: GAPointNet: A Semantic Segmentation Network for Indoor Point Clouds Based on Geometric Perception and Attention Mechanisms
authors: Zikang Yan, Fang Wan, Guangbo Lei, Li Xv, Chaoxia Zheng
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Point cloud semantic segmentation techniques for 3D indoor scenes are crucial in areas such as autonomous robot navigation and digital twin construction. However, how to realize accurate segmentation of point clouds from geometrically complex indoor scenes is a challenging task. In this paper, we propose a point cloud semantic segmentation network GAPointNet for indoor scenes, which introduces a multi-frequency positional coding module to enhance the perception of local geometric details, and uses a multi-statistic aggregation strategy to integrate regional features and retain richer semantic information. Then, the adaptive channel attention mechanism mitigates the problem of semantic confusion and gradient attenuation of deep features during propagation to promote high-quality detail recovery. We tested the method on the publicly available S3DIS dataset, and the results show that GAPointNet achieves better segmentation performance in complex indoor scenes.

title: Dense U-Net: A Dense Block-Based U-Net Architecture for Precise Skin Cancer Segmentation
authors: Priyanka Chakraborty, Bidyutmala Saha, Promita Chakraborty, Sajal Sasmal, Sayan Nath, Sayani Konar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: Image segmentation plays a essential role in both the prevention of diagnostic errors as well as the early and precise detection of skin cancer. These identification techniques are becoming progressively more effective with the help of the latest advancements in computer vision. To increase segmentation accuracy, this study implements a superior U-Net architecture with dense blocks. Instead of using standard convolutional layers, the network utilizes densely connected layers at every step of the network it more effectively preserves and employs feature information. Due to this updated layout, based on the encoderdecoder architecture of U-Net, the model can now learn highly complex patterns in dermatological images. It also goes beyond that by patient variability and different lighting condition problems. The achievement of the proposed model has been evaluated with the help of the ISIC 2018 dataset, and the desired results led to a Dice coefficient of 91.38%, a IoU of 84.78%, and an accuracy of 97.72%. These findings are indicative of the model as a precise and reliable instrument for skin cancer segmentation.

title: Efficient Human Pose Estimation Based on Dilated Convolution and Dual Attention Calibration
authors: Yu Zou, Qishen Li, Hua Huang, Qiufeng Li, Ying Xiong
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Human pose estimation has important application value in motion recognition, human-computer interaction and other fields. Deep learning-based methods have achieved a favorable balance between accuracy and efficiency. However, the scale variation of human structures and redundant information from multi-scale feature fusion in complex scenarios still limit the improvement of key point localization accuracy. To address this issue, this paper improves the SPPF module in RTMpose and proposes a MSDF-DRA module for 2D human pose estimation. The MSDF-DRA module covers full-scale features through five parallel branches: a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1 \times 1$</tex> convolution branch captures local fine-grained features, three <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3 \times 3$</tex> dilated convolution branches with different dilation rates adapt to medium-to-large scale limb structures, and a global average pooling branch supplements global contextual information. Meanwhile, a parallel channel-spatial dual attention calibration mechanism is designed to suppress redundant noise and enhance key point-related features. Experimental results on the COCO-WholeBody show that the improved model achieves an AP of 60.9% and an AR of 69.3%, representing a 2.9-percentage-point AP improvement compared to the baseline RTMpose-m, with a computational cost controlled at 3.35 GFLOPs, providing an efficient feature enhancement solution for human pose estimation in complex scenarios.

title:
Q
                    <sup>2</sup>
                    SAR: A Quantum Multiple Kernel Learning Approach for Drug Discovery
authors: Alejandro Giraldo, Daniel Ruiz, Mariano Caruso, Javier Mancilla, Guido Bellomo
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship (QSAR) modeling is a cornerstone of computational drug discovery. This research demonstrates the successful application of a Quantum Multiple Kernel Learning (QMKL) framework to enhance QSAR classification, showing a notable performance improvement over classical methods. We apply this methodology to a dataset for identifying DYRK1A kinase inhibitors. The workflow involves converting SMILES representations into numerical molecular descriptors, reducing dimensionality via Principal Component Analysis (PCA), and employing a Support Vector Machine (SVM) trained on an optimized combination of multiple quantum and classical kernels. By benchmarking the QMKL-SVM against a classical Gradient Boosting model, we show that the quantumenhanced approach achieves a superior AUC score, highlighting its potential to provide a quantum advantage in challenging cheminformatics classification tasks.

title: Device-Native Autonomous Agents for Privacy-Preserving Negotiations
authors: Joyjit Roy, Samaresh Kumar Singh
abstract: Automated negotiations in insurance and business-to-business (B2B) commerce encounter substantial challenges. Current systems force a trade-off between convenience and privacy by routing sensitive financial data through centralized servers, increasing security risks, and diminishing user trust. This study introduces a device-native autonomous Agentic AI system for privacy-preserving negotiations. The proposed system operates exclusively on user hardware, enabling real-time bargaining while maintaining sensitive constraints locally. It integrates zero-knowledge proofs to ensure privacy and employs distilled world models to support advanced on-device reasoning. The architecture incorporates six technical components within an Agentic AI workflow. Agents autonomously plan negotiation strategies, conduct secure multi-party bargaining, and generate cryptographic audit trails without exposing user data to external servers. The system is evaluated in insurance and B2B procurement scenarios across diverse device configurations. Results show an average success rate of 87 %, a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2.4 \times$</tex> reduction in latency relative to cloud baselines, and strong privacy preservation through zero-knowledge proofs. User studies show 27 % higher trust scores when decision trails are available. These findings establish a foundation for trustworthy autonomous agents in privacy-sensitive financial domains.

title: Developing a Vietnamese Text Summarization Large Language Model on Limited Hardware
authors: Tin Pho, Bernardo Flores
abstract: Text summarization models have achieved significant growth during the last few years because of major Large Language Model (LLM) technological advancements, and are now are widely used in news distribution (TL;DR news), translation tools (DeepL Translate), or virtual assistants. However, the progress has not yet reached all languages equally. The Vietnamese language is used by more than 90 million people, but for LLM purposes, it is still considered a low-resource language and therefore faces a certain gap when. This can lead to many disadvantages for the Vietnamese monolingual population around the world, as they cannot catch up with the current informational society. This paper addresses this gap by performing a comparative study to fine-tune and evaluate two state-of-the-art Vietnamese-specific models-BartPho-syllable (BARTbased) and ViT5 (T5-based)-for the task of abstractive news summarization. The models were trained using a parameterefficient (QLoRA) approach on a curated corpus combining the public nam194/vietnews dataset with freshly scraped articles from major Vietnamese news outlets. The key finding of this research is that both sophisticated fine-tuned models were ultimately outperformed on standard quantitative metrics (ROUGE and BERTScore) by simpler extractive baselines, particularly the Lead-3 heuristic. The evaluation scores and final validation loss demonstrated that ViT5 achieved better results than BartPhosyllable in the two fine-tuned models.

title: TAGS-Avatar: Temporally-Aware Gaussian Avatar for Photorealistic Rendering
authors: Zedan Zheng, Zhuo Su
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Differentiable rendering techniques have significantly advanced 3D human reconstruction with 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) techniques. However, previous methods still struggle to capture complex appearance details due to conditioning on a single pose. In this paper, we discuss that accurate human appearance modeling also depends on the preceding poses except the current pose. We therefore propose TAGS-Avatar, which effectively integrates temporal information for enhanced avatar reconstruction. Our approach fully utilizes motion dynamics from input videos by calculating pose and point coordinate changes across a sampled sequence, thereby incorporating both skeletal and surface vertex motions. To further enhance the learning of fine-grained details, we employ an image encoder to extract pixel-aligned features for 3D Gaussians. Considering the challenge of self-occlusion, we introduce the visibility-weighted feature extraction module that using the SMPL mesh to approximate the visibility of each 3D Gaussian, improving robustness in feature aggregation. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, which preserves rendering quality in varied poses and clothing types.

title: X-AD: Explainable Anomaly Detection in Serverless Systems
authors: Brij Mohan, Uday Kiran Chilakalapalli, Rananjay Kumar
abstract: This paper presents X-AD, a framework for explainable anomaly detection in serverless computing environments. Serverless architectures offer scalability and cost efficiency but pose challenges for monitoring and anomaly detection due to their dynamic and distributed nature. Traditional anomaly detection methods often lack the interpretability required to diagnose the root causes of anomalies, hindering efficient incident response. X-AD addresses this by combining a time series Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) autoencoder for effective anomaly detection with explainable AI techniques, specifically SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) and counterfactual analysis. The LSTM autoencoder is trained on normal operational data to learn expected temporal patterns. Anomalies are detected based on high reconstruction errors. SHAP is then used to attribute the anomaly score to specific features (e.g., invocation rate, duration, memory, CPU, error rate) over the relevant time window, providing insights into why an anomaly occurred. Counterfactual analysis complements this by suggesting minimal changes to the input features that would have resulted in a normal classification, offering actionable recommendations for remediation. We evaluate X-AD on a synthetic serverless dataset simulating realistic workload patterns and anomaly types. The results demonstrate that X-AD achieves competitive detection performance compared to traditional methods such as Isolation Forest while providing valuable, interpretable explanations and actionable insights, significantly improving the efficiency of incident response in serverless systems.

title: Hybrid Quantum Repeater Protocol for Long Distance Secure Quantum Communication
authors: Karthikhesvaran S. S., Mahesh Kumar Reddy Vennapusa, Prasanna Venkatesan G K D
abstract: So far, quantum communication has promised unconditionally secure information transfer through protocols such as quantum key distribution. The practical deployment of QKD faces severe limitations due to decoherence, photon loss, and limited transmission distances in optical fibers and free space. The current systems are not scalable and require trusted relays, which are costly and introduce security vulnerabilities. This paper solves the core problem of the range extension in quantum networks by proposing HQRP, a novel hybrid quantum repeater protocol, for high-rate entanglement distribution in which single-spin photon transducers are combined with multiplexed ensemble-based atomic frequency comb memories. The HQRP uniquely combines the principles of twin-field QKD and error-corrected entanglement swapping, achieving secret key rates of tens of bits per second over 1000 km with only nine repeater nodes. Simulations demonstrate a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 x}$</tex> improvement of the key rate with respect to traditional measurement-device-independent QKD without repeaters, including robustness against 20 % photon loss. These results demonstrate the feasibility of using near-term hardware, such as room-temperature telecom-compatible memories, and pave the way toward global quantum networks. The effective efficiency of the protocol in practical environments is still affected by variations in environmental temperatures, which affect AFC memories, and require sub-nanosecond timing precision to coordinate nodes over <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 0 0 ~ k m}$</tex>. Future versions will employ adaptive timing based on machine learning to address these engineering issues. Index Terms-Quantum repeaters, entanglement distribution, QKD, decoherence mitigation, hybrid quantum networks.

title: A GAN-Based MoE-Style High-Fidelity Pose Transfer Model with Step-Wise Mechanism
authors: Lili Yin, Ze Zhao, Jie Gao, Shuaijie Wang, Yunze Xie, Yuehan Wang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Generating high-fidelity, smoothly animated character sequences from a single portrait image and driving video remains a core challenge in computer vision. Existing methods still face limitations in handling large-angle pose changes and maintaining temporal consistency in character appearance, particularly facial and clothing textures. To address this, we propose a novel generative adversarial network architecture named Step-Animate GAN. Step-Animate GAN combines the powerful temporal feature extraction capabilities of the Wan2.2 MoE (Mixed of Expert) architecture with the incremental pose transfer mechanism of the Step Network. This architecture first utilizes the Wan2.2 feature encoder to extract deep features with temporal consistency and high visual fidelity. Subsequently, the core Step-Animate module decomposes the pose transfer process into fine-grained steps, strictly controlling spatial structural changes at each stage to effectively prevent pose disconnection and texture flickering. Furthermore, we retain the style discriminator and face region loss mechanism from Step Network, significantly enhancing the preservation of clothing textures and the realism of facial details. Extensive experiments on public datasets demonstrate that Step-Animate GAN significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in identity preservation, motion accuracy, and video quality.

title: Score-Guided Synthetic Experience Replay for Stable Off-Policy Reinforcement Learning
authors: Precious Nwaorgu, Olamide Osigbemeh, Chinwendu Enyioha
abstract: Experience replay is a critical component of offpolicy reinforcement learning, improving sample efficiency and stabilizing training. Recent approaches have explored augmenting replay buffers with synthetic transitions generated by adversarial models; however, such methods often suffer from training instability and distributional drift, which can degrade policy learning. In this paper, we propose Score-Guided Synthetic Experience Replay (SG-SER), a lightweight framework that incorporates score-based geometric regularization into adversarial replay generation. A score network is trained to estimate the gradient of the empirical transition density and is used to guide a generative adversarial network toward high-density regions of the replay buffer distribution. This regularization mitigates mode collapse and improves the fidelity of generated transitions while retaining the efficient single-step sampling of GANs. We evaluate SG-SER on the CartPole-v1 control task using Deep Q-Learning and compare it against standard replay and unregularized GAN-based replay. Experimental results demonstrate that score-guided replay improves training stability and yields more consistent performance than unregularized GAN-based.

title: A Comprehensive Review of Reinforcement Learning Approaches for Automated Rubik's Cube Solving
authors: Tanushree Brajabasi, Md Ariyan, Arkanil Karchaudhuri, Subhrotosh Chakraborty, Gopal Pramanik, Kishalay Bairagi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive survey of reinforcement learning (RL) approaches for automated Rubik's Cube solving and examines how they compare with traditional algorithmic methods. Classical solvers such as Korf's Algorithm, Thistlethwaite's Algorithm, Kociemba's Two-Phase Algorithm etc. are reviewed with respect to efficiency, optimality, and computational requirements. The survey then analyzes modern RL-based techniques-including Q-learning, Deep Q-Networks (DQN), and Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)-highlighting their ability to learn heuristic functions, generalize to higherdimensional cubes, and integrate with robotics. Comparative tables summarize the fundamental differences between classical and learning-based methods in terms of scalability, memory usage, adaptability, and real-time performance. Overall, this review provides an organized synthesis of major developments in both algorithmic and RL-based cube solving, offering insights into why RL has emerged as a flexible and powerful framework for combinational puzzle solving and related AI applications.

title: A Modulation Scheme for a Single-Phase AC/AC Matrix Converter
authors: Marcos Gomez-Redondo, Marco Rivera, Javier Muñoz, Patrick Wheeler, Jakson Bonaldo, Sergio Toledo
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Single-phase matrix converters can serve as an interface for sharing power between a three-phase and a single-phase electrical system. However, to operate this topology using linear control techniques, a suitable modulation scheme is necessary. This paper analyses modulation schemes for the single-phase matrix converter, identifying two degrees of freedom that can be exploited to achieve the control objectives. Moreover, this article proposes to determine the duty cycles of the space-vector modulation by formulating and solving a linear programming problem. The simulation results show that the proposal leads to a lower total harmonic distortion of the output current, compared to a popular method found in the literature.

title: A Stress-Testing Approach for Multimodal Fusion Under Missing Modalities in Cyber-Physical Systems
authors: Laure Bien Aime, Debashis Das, Pushpita Chatterjee, Uttam Ghosh
abstract: Resilient decision-making in cyber-physical systems (CPS) increasingly relies on multimodal data fusion. However, real-world deployments frequently operate under missing or degraded modalities due to sensor faults, communication disruptions, and operational constraints. This paper investigates robust multimodal fusion under missing-modality conditions through a unified stress-testing protocol evaluated across two representative CPS domains: smart-grid event classification and human fall detection. The proposed framework groups features into semantically coherent modalities, trains modality-specific classifiers, and introduces stochastic modality removal during training to mitigate over-reliance on any single sensor group. We systematically compare early- and late-fusion baselines and assess robustness under controlled modality masking with training-time modality dropout. Performance degradation is analyzed using macro-averaged metrics and class-level minority performance to expose resilience failures that are often hidden by high overall accuracy under class imbalance. Experimental results reveal strong modality-dependent robustness patterns and demonstrate that rare but safety-critical classes are disproportionately affected by missing modalities. These findings highlight the necessity of resilience-oriented training and evaluation strategies for multimodal CPS operating under realistic sensing constraints.

title: WIDENet: A Novel Lightweight CNN for Robust Skin Lesion Segmentation
authors: Ghulam Ali, Mohd Khalid Awang, Javed Rashid, Muhammad Hamza, Osama A. Khashan, Anwar Ghani
abstract: Proper and accurate classification of skin lesions is essential for melanoma diagnosis. Distinguishing lesion regions from surrounding healthy skin is difficult due to blurred tissue patterns, irregular shapes, and faint boundaries. Conventional segmentation techniques often lose high-frequency structural details during downsampling, which compromises boundary precision. This article proposes WIDENet, a lightweight segmentation framework that preserves fine lesion boundaries while maintaining computational efficiency. WIDENet combines a multi-resolution wavelet, a transformer-based contextual encoder, and an attentive feature refinement mechanism. In contrast to classical encoder-decoder systems, spatial downsampling is performed in the frequency domain via wavelet decomposition, thereby reducing feature dimensionality while preserving multiscale structural information. The encoder based on transformers enables long-range semantic perception. In contrast, the attention-learned refinement framework focuses on information related to boundaries and suppresses background noise. We tested WIDENet using three benchmark dermoscopic datasets, including <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathrm {PH}^{2}$ </tex-math></inline-formula>, ISIC 2017, and ISIC 2018. Comparison with state-of-the-art models using U-Net, U-Net++, DeepLabV3(+), PAN, PSPNet, and LinkNet demonstrates that WIDENet consistently outperforms them. In particular, it achieves Dice scores of 0.9384 on <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathrm {PH}^{2}$ </tex-math></inline-formula> and 0.8752 on ISIC 2017, and high precision, recall, and IoU across all datasets. These results demonstrate that WIDENet is not only correct but also highly computationally efficient, making it a promising tool for real-world melanoma screening and clinical decision support systems.

title: Dsfformer: Dual-Scale Frequency Attention for Multivariate Time Series Forecasting
authors: Licheng Yang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Forecasting multivariate time series plays a pivotal role in practical applications such as energy management and traffic flow control. However, current methodologies encounter two primary obstacles: conventional attention mechanisms often suffer from localized overfitting, resulting in biased weight distribution; additionally, channel-independent approaches neglect the potential of inter-variate correlations, which typically exhibit distinct patterns in the frequency domain. To surmount these limitations, we introduce DSFFormer, a novel framework enhanced by Dual-Scale Frequency Attention (DSFA). Specifically, we employ Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) to translate time-domain data into spectral representations, thereby capturing global correlations among variables. Furthermore, the proposed DSFA mechanism is engineered to concurrently model cross-variate and intra-scale dependencies while alleviating attention concentration bias. Comprehensive evaluations on five standard benchmarks verify that DSFFormer delivers state-of-the-art (SOTA) accuracy in both short- and long-term forecasting tasks.

title: Comparative Study of Metaheuristic Algorithms for Mobile Robot Two-Dimensional Path Planning in Grid-Based Environments
authors: Chenyu Zhao
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Path planning is a core problem in mobile robot research. Robots need to find collision-free paths from start to goal in environments filled with obstacles. This paper compares five metaheuristic algorithms for grid-based path planning: Genetic Algorithm (GA), Differential Evolution (DE), Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO), and Salp Swarm Algorithm (SSA). The workspace is modeled as an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{n} \times \mathrm{n}$</tex> binary grid matrix. Each cell is marked as either free space or an obstacle. The fitness function we design includes two parts: one measures the Euclidean path length and the other adds a penalty for collision. To ensure fair comparison, all algorithms run under the same conditions with identical population size, maximum iterations, and random seeds. We test these algorithms on a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$20 \times 20$</tex> grid map with two rectangular obstacle regions positioned to create a challenging navigation scenario. Experimental results reveal that DE achieves the fastest convergence because its differential mutation mechanism explores the search space more efficiently. GWO also shows strong performance thanks to its hierarchical wolf pack structure. We apply the Wilcoxon ranksum test to verify that performance gaps among algorithms are statistically significant. For practical use, we recommend DE and GWO when fast planning is needed. SSA is a better choice if the application requires smoother trajectories.

title: Energy Efficient Comparator in Quantum Dot Cellular Automata
authors: Ananya Banerjee, Debasmita Manna, Manali Dhar, Aurpan Majumder, Saradindu Panda, Bansibadan Maji
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: An achievable alternative for CMOS technology is quantum dot cellular automata, which offers a fascinating paradigm in computation and communication. QCA is very favorable due to its ultrahigh packing density, extremely low energy dissipation, and quick functioning speed. This work employs a layered T-logic reduction methodology to propose two designs of a 1-bit magnitude comparator module. Total and average energies, along with the cost of the proposed LT modules, are computed and compared with those of currently available designs. The recommended energy-efficient 1-bit magnitude comparator designs are excellent for providing the lowest energy dissipation. Among the two proposed designs, Design 2 features more compact circuitry, offering optimum energy efficiency. Although Design 1 is scalable, it allows inputs and outputs to be readily accessible. Gallium-arsenide heterostructure parameters are utilized to build layouts and estimate energy using the coherence-vector and bistable-approximation simulation engines of QCADesigner 2.0.3 and QCADesigner E 2.2.

title: Evaluating the Impact of a Game-Based Hackathon on Cybersecurity and AI Confidence: A Quantitative Study with Exploratory Gender Analysis
authors: Seshadri Paravastu, Ferdinand Rivera, Rajan Selvarajan Thirumalai Thattai, Melody Moh
abstract: Game-based learning interventions such as hackathons are increasingly used to address gaps in cybersecurity and artificial intelligence (AI) literacy; however, empirical evaluations that quantify their impact using rigorous statistical methods remain limited. This paper presents a quantitative evaluation of a game-based cybersecurity and AI hackathon, examining changes in participants' self-reported confidence across three constructs: cybersecurity knowledge, AI conceptual understanding, and application of cybersecurity and AI concepts. Anonymous pre- and post-event survey data were analyzed using independent-samples <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$t$</tex>-tests and effect size measures (Cohen's <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$d$</tex> and Hedges' g). Results indicate statistically significant and practically meaningful gains in cybersecurity knowledge confidence and applied confidence, with moderate and small-tomoderate effect sizes, respectively. In contrast, confidence in AI conceptual understanding did not exhibit statistically significant change and showed negligible effects. Exploratory gender-based analyses revealed consistent directional patterns across groups, with stronger gains observed for cybersecurity-related constructs than for AI concepts. These findings suggest that short-term, experiential hackathons are particularly effective for concrete cybersecurity learning outcomes, while AI conceptual confidence may require longer or more explicitly scaffolded instructional approaches.

title: Experimental Assessment of a DC/DC Interleaved Converter for Hydrogen Generation
authors: Luis Pua, Alexander Rojas, Daniel Velasquez, Macarena Villa, Matias Diaz, Mauricio Espinoza
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Green hydrogen is presented as a key energy source for decarbonizing difficult-to-electrify sectors, y et i ts production faces challenges, including limited electrolyzer efficiency and high operational costs. This study describes the design, hierarchical control, and experimental validation of a multiphase interleaved DC-DC converter that minimizes output current ripple for proton exchange membrane (PEM) electrolysis. The converter operates in continuous conduction mode with synchronous switching and features parallel out-of-phase stages that attenuate current fluctuations while maintaining energy efficiency and system stability. A 5 kW, three-leg prototype, controlled via a dSPACE MicroLabBox platform, was tested under a resistive load representative of electrolyzer conditions. The results demonstrate stable operation, reduced current distortion, and enhanced suitability for direct coupling with PEM electrolyzers under fluctuating input conditions. This supports improved durability and performance.

title: Four-Valued Model Checking for Byzantine Fault Tolerant Consensus Verification
authors: Jefferson O. Andrade
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Byzantine Fault Tolerant (BFT) consensus protocols are fundamental for the security and reliability of distributed systems, including Distributed Ledger Technologies, yet their formal verification in the presence of uncertainties and sophisticated adversarial behaviors remains a significant challenge. Classical model checking, restricted to Boolean logic, often inadequately captures states of partial information (e.g., due to message loss under partial synchrony) or explicit conflict (e.g., arising from Byzantine equivocation). To address this gap, this paper introduces a bilattice-based model checking approach, employing Belnap's four-valued logic, to explicitly represent and reason about these nuanced epistemic states within BFT protocols. We demonstrate this methodology through a Promela/SPIN model of a simplified PBFT consensus algorithm, where core state variables are four-valued. Our model incorporates mechanisms for simulating message loss leading to unknown states and Byzantine primary equivocation, designed to manifest as conflict states in honest replicas' beliefs. Our preliminary verification runs, while underscoring the computational challenges inherent in this expressive approach, illustrate an enhanced capacity to distinguish between lack of information and detected inconsistency. This offers deeper insights into protocol behavior and improves the detection of subtle consensus faults that might be obscured by traditional binary analysis. This approach contributes to more robust and comprehensive formal verification of BFT consensus.

title: UAV-Based Bolt Diameter Measurement via 3D Point Cloud and RGB Images
authors: Kuixiang Xiong, Zhejing Bao, Wenjun Yan
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: In industrial field, there are numerous scenarios requiring bolt diameter measurement, where traditional manual measurement often pose safety and efficiency challenges. To address this issue, this paper proposes a bolt diameter measurement approach based on 3D point cloud and RGB images, which can be deployed on unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) to enable remote, non-contact, and autonomous inspection. The approach employs a depth camera and an RGB camera to capture tilted side-view shots of bolts, acquiring their 3D point cloud data and RGB images. To overcome perspective distortion in oblique views, a contour-matching strategy is introduced. First, actual bolt contours are segmented from RGB image using a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{U}^{2}$</tex>-Net deep-learning model. Then, using positional data from 3D point cloud, a simulated bolt contour is generated in RGB image. Finally, the degree of match between the actual and simulated contours is computed to search for the best-matching diameter parameter of the simulated bolt, which is taken as the measured diameter. The proposed approach is not constrained by shooting angles, allows for simultaneous measurement of multiple bolts in a single image, and is suitable for deployment on lightweight platforms such as drones. Engineering practice has verified that this approach can achieve the desired measurement accuracy.

title: Strategic LED Placement for Blooming Mitigation in Optical Camera Communication
authors: Leonardo Muñoz Rosel, Francisca Vera Vera, Lisandra Bravo Alvarez, Gabriel Saavedra
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: The growing demand for higher wireless data capacity has highlighted the limitations of radio frequency technologies. In this context, Optical Camera Communication stands out as a promising solution; however, deploying this system in realworld scenarios poses technical challenges, including optical distortions such as blooming. This work presents an experimental OCC system designed to reduce such distortions. The approach employs a practical method for arranging LEDs in a way that minimizes blooming. Experiments conducted with multiple LEDs in various spatial configurations, at distances ranging from 30 to 200 cm, enabled a detailed assessment of blooming intensity. The results show that proper LED placement can significantly reduce this optical distortion and enhance signal decoding stability. In particular, diagonal configurations achieved near error-free transmission (BER <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.0$</tex>) at 30 cm, while the 6-LED DHV arrangement delivered strong performance in the Mitigate Blooming metric, with an average value of 0.625, whereas other configurations typically scored below 0.4, with the 1-LED baseline at just <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 2 5 7}$</tex>.

title: A High-Resolution Vernier Time-to-Digital Converter Based on Three-Stage Nested Delay-Locked Loops for LiDAR Applications
authors: Donghang Wang, Zekuan Lin, Shaolin Liao, Xianbo Li
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: This paper presents a high-resolution Vernier time-to-digital converter (TDC) based on three-stage nested delay-locked loops (DLLs) with controllable delay ratios for light detection and ranging (LiDAR) applications. To support high resolution while maintaining area and power efficiency, three-stage nested DLLs are utilized to stabilize the resolution of TDC against PVT variations and precisely lock the ratio between the coarse and fine least significant bit (LSB) sizes to the fourth power of two, therefore eliminating the need for additional arithmetic circuits and reducing hardware overhead. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed TDC achieves a coarse resolution of 244.14 ps and a fine resolution of 15.26 ps, with a precisely maintained power-of-two ratio, specifically 2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sup>, between the two steps. The TDC achieves a maximum range of 3.91 ns with a power consumption of of 8.99 mW from a 1.8 V power supply.

title: Towards the Development of a Deep Learning Based Maize-Weed Detection System Using YOLO Architectures
authors: Taliha Abiodun Folorunso, Jibril Abdullahi Bala, Trusting Inekwe, Adegboyega Adebayo, Anjana Ran Yadav, William Thistleton
abstract: Weeds are a primary constraint to agricultural productivity, potentially reducing crop yields by 30% and making crop production challenging. Conventional and chemical management methods are often labour-intensive or environmentally damaging; thus, there is a need for a better approach that leverages advances in deep learning techniques. This study investigates the performance of nine YOLO variants (v8-v12) in nano and small sizes for real-time maize-weed detection in precision agriculture. The evaluation shows a significant trade-off between computational efficiency and detection robustness. While the newer YOLOv12s architecture is more optimised, with 21.2 GFLOPs, the older YOLOv8s maintains a performance lead, achieving a peak mAP@50 of 0.870 and a recall of 0.861. The results indicate that YOLOv8n achieves the fastest inference time <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(4.4 \text{ms})$</tex>, making it well-suited for high-speed monitoring that requires maximum weed eradication, given its high recall. Finally, YOLOv12n offers the best balance for edge-based devices, given its size, thereby demonstrating the capability of the lightweight Yolo model to be deployed for weed identification and detection in maize farmlands.

title: A Tiered Multimodal Memory and Event-Graph Architecture for Real-Time Agentic AI Systems
authors: John Benito Jesudasan Peter

title: A Multi-Level Morphology and Rule-Guided Unsupervised Segmentation Method for Industrial Automotive Brake Calipers
authors: Ronghui Sun, Xiaohu Liu
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Aiming at the image segmentation challenges in the industrial visual inspection of automotive brake caliper X-ray images, this paper proposes an unsupervised caliper region extraction method based on multi-level morphology and rule guidance. The core innovation of this method lies in introducing a rule-based intelligent repair mechanism guided by contour analysis: by setting an area threshold, this mechanism can intelligently distinguish and fill small noise-induced holes while completely preserving the functional large holes inherent to the caliper (such as piston holes), thereby maintaining the geometric authenticity of key structures under the premise of ensuring mask integrity. Experimental results on a self-built dataset of real industrial production line caliper X-ray images show that the method can effectively overcome complex background interference, robustly and accurately segment the main caliper body region; it requires no annotated data whatsoever and features a highly interpretable process, providing an effective solution for few-shot, high-robustness segmentation tasks of specific components in industrial scenarios.

title: Sweat2Scroll: An Energy-Efficient Policy Enforcement Architecture for Mobile Edge Environments Using WebAssembly
authors: Jagadish Krishna Pilla
abstract: Implementing fine-grained Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) on consumer mobile devices presents a conflict between policy complexity and resource constraints. Traditional cloud-based Policy Decision Points (PDPs) introduce unacceptable latency and battery drain due to cellular Radio Resource Control (RRC) state transitions. Conversely, native mobile implementations lack the flexibility for dynamic “policy-as-code” updates. This paper introduces Sweat2Scroll, a novel edge-computing architecture that executes Open Policy Agent (OPA) policies compiled to WebAssembly (Wasm) directly on the mobile application processor. By decoupling decision logic from the application binary, our system enables verifiable, offline evaluation of cross-domain constraints. Microbenchmarks on Android and iOS demonstrate a 99th percentile evaluation latency of 0.1969 ms and a conservative energy efficiency improvement of over 3000 times compared to cloud-based alternatives. We further introduce a provenance-structured audit log based on the W3C PROV-DM standard to ensure decision transparency. The results confirm that Wasm-based execution at the edge provides a scalable, energy-efficient foundation for complex mobile access control.

title: Assessing the Impact of Cyberattacks Through the Integration of a Honeypot in a Controlled Environment
authors: Jorge Andrade Gómez, Christian Lazo Ramírez, Luis Veas-Castillo, Carlos Melo Paredes
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Cybersecurity is a critical concern for modern organizations due to the ongoing rise in cyberattacks worldwide. Companies are frequently targeted by malicious actors seeking to access sensitive information and exploit vulnerabilities for personal gain. In this context, Honeypots emerge as an effective defensive tool. These systems are designed to attract, divert, and analyze attacks, enabling the study of malicious behavior. This work focuses on the integration of low and medium-interaction Honeypots to assess the impact of various cyberattacks targeting commonly exposed services such as Secure Shell (SSH) and Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP). Additionally, public Internet Protocol (IP) addresses were deliberately shared on malicious forums within the Dark Web to observe offensive actors responses. The proposed methodology enabled the collection of statistics regarding attack types and techniques, as well as the analysis of the effects of deliberately exposing vulnerable assets. Results revealed a significant increase in access attempts following the forum postings, highlighting the direct influence of such platforms in spreading threats—reaching totals of nearly 900 unique connections to a single server. Moreover, recurring patterns were identified in the targeted paths, including repeated login attempts to specific endpoints, commonly used passwords, and the geographical origin of attacks. These findings reinforce the value of Honeypots as a proactive tool for research and threat monitoring.

title: Remote Access Interaction Models in Industrial Control Systems: A Threat Analysis of Control, Mirroring, and Proxy-Human Patterns
authors: Rakesh Reddy Panati
abstract: Remote access in Industrial Control Systems (ICS) is commonly treated as a connectivity problem, overlooking how interaction models determine execution of authority and attribution. This paper systematizes three prevalent interaction models—Interactive Control, View-Only Mirroring, and Authorized Proxy Execution—and demonstrates how each reshapes the threat landscape. While restrictive models such as View-Only Mirroring reduce direct technical attack surfaces, they frequently displace execution to trusted local operators, introducing attribution gaps and enabling humanmediated exploitation pathways. Using a Systematization of Knowledge grounded in MITRE ATT&CK for ICS, this study shows how adversarial techniques shift from direct execution toward trust exploitation. The paper further analyzes the misalignment between enterprise Zero Trust Network Access (ZTNA) and legacy OT protocols dependent on Layer-2 discovery, showing how this gap incentivizes Shadow Bridging workarounds. Finally, interactionaware control deltas mapped to ISA/IEC (International Society of Automation / International Electrotechnical Commission) 62443-3-3 are proposed to align security controls with operational realities of industrial maintenance.

title: TrustBench: Benchmarking Trustworthy Large Language Models With Cost and Stability Metrics
authors: Ritesh Ruparel, Srinivasa Maringanti, Aravinda Jatavallabha, Jen Agarwal, Praveen Kumar Reddy Vavilla, Suraj Vangala
abstract: Deployment trust in large language model (LLM) systems is shaped by behaviors that do not show up in single run accuracy numbers. In production, teams routinely need models to request missing information instead of guessing, behave consistently across repeated calls, refuse unsafe requests when required, and remain economical at scale. We present TrustBench, a reproducible benchmark designed around these practical requirements. For each model and prompt pair, TrustBench executes <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N_{r}=5$</tex> trials under fixed settings and reports: desired behavior rate (DBR) from a per item majority vote, run stability as agreement with that majority label, confidence dispersion across reruns, and cost effectiveness from token based pricing. We evaluate five proprietary models: OpenAI GPT-4.1, GPT-4o, GPT-5; Claude Sonnet 4.5; and xAI Grok 4.1 (Fast Reasoning). On the evaluated ambiguity set, requesting clarification is rarely the dominant outcome, and some models never reach a majority clarify label. Safety behavior is highly stable across reruns, yet pricing differences are large enough to change value rankings even when behavioral rates are close. For the multi step set, the main tables report determinism (repeatability) and confidence dispersion rather than verified correctness. Code, prompts, and per run logs are released to support reproducibility [1].

title: Review of Artificial Intelligence Solutions for the Wood Processing Industry
authors: Felipe S. Gutiérrez, Alejandro J. Rojas, Mario A. Ramos
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This work reviews artificial i ntelligence (AI) solutions applied to the plywood manufacturing industry. Our review proceeds in three stages. First, we characterize the industrial context, detailing typical process flows, c onstraints, a nd KPIs relevant to individual workstations. Second, we characterize the methodologies used across the literature-learning paradigms, model families, data requirements, and strategies to handle process nonlinearities and variability-at both workstation and system-wide levels. Third, we report the main results for each selected study and synthesize them via metric clustering to highlight common patterns, trade-offs, and gaps. The objective is to provide decision-makers with a clear framework to match adaptable AI solutions to the specific <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$r$</tex> equirements of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$t$</tex> heir <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p$</tex> roduction lines, balancing accuracy, robustness, and implementation cost.

title: DeepServe: Hierarchical Model Placement and Dynamic Batching for Cost-Efficient Multi-Tenant LLM Inference at Scale
authors: Tejas Pravinbhai Patel, Pragya Agarwal
abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) inference serving at scale presents critical challenges in multi-tenant cloud environments, where organizations must balance conflicting objectives of cost efficiency, response latency, and resource utilization across heterogeneous GPU infrastructure. Current serving systems employ static model placement strategies and homogeneous batching policies, resulting in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$40 - 60 \%$</tex> GPU underutilization and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3-5 \times$</tex> cost inefficiency for variable workloads. We present DeepServe, a hierarchical model placement framework with dynamic batching that optimizes cost per served token across five GPU tiers (A100-80GB, A100-40GB, V100, L4, T4) and four model scales (7B-175B parameters). Our system integrates three key innovations: (1) Memoryaware hierarchical placement algorithm achieving 82% GPU utilization through optimal model-to-GPU assignment based on memory footprint and throughput profiles, (2) Adaptive continuous batching with SLA-aware scheduling attaining <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.8 \times$</tex> throughput improvement over sequential processing while maintaining P95 latency under 2.5 seconds, and (3) Multi-objective cost optimizer reducing inference cost by 47% compared to static provisioning through heterogeneous GPU selection. Evaluation on production-representative workloads with 50,000 requests demonstrates: P50 latency of 890 ms, P95 latency of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2.31 ~\mathrm{s}, 79 \%$</tex> average GPU utilization, and $0.0012 cost per 1000 tokens-matching vLLM throughput benchmarks while achieving 47% cost reduction versus naive A100-only deployment.

title: Few-Shot Data Augmentation Image Anomaly Detection Based on Clip
authors: Zhaowei Zeng, Dan Li, Xujun Li
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: The Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (CLIP) model demonstrates remarkable generalization capabilities and exhibits substantial potential for few-shot anomaly detection in industrial applications. Therefore, further enhancing its performance is of significant importance. To address its low efficiency in utilizing text-image pair data, this paper proposes a semantic splicing module that converts normal text into anomalous text and combines it with the original image to form new negative sample pairs, thereby achieving data sample augmentation. Since CLIP inherently lacks the ability to distinguish between positive and negative sample pairs, we design an anomaly boundary module that projects CLIP features onto a single hypersphere, ensuring the distance between normal images and anomalous text is greater than that between normal images and normal text. This strengthens the model's discriminative capacity between normal and abnormal samples, thereby boosting detection accuracy. In anomaly detection experiments on MVTec and VisA dataset, our method achieves superior metrics compared to PatchCore, WinCLIP and RWDA.

title: RainbowCFR: Deep Counterfactual Regret Minimization with Rainbow DQN
authors: Jinquan Liu, Yuezhongyi Sun, Xiaodan Wang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: In recent years, incomplete information games (IIGs) have become a central research area in artificial intelligence and reinforcement learning. These games, where players lack full knowledge of their opponents' states, pose significant challenges to decision-making and policy learning. While Counterfactual Regret Minimization (CFR) and its deep extensions, like DeepCFR, have shown promise in solving IIGs by approximating regret values and strategies, they suffer from high variance in counterfactual value estimation, unstable policy updates, and low sample efficiency. To address these challenges, we propose RainbowCFR, a framework that integrates Rainbow DQN's stabilization mechanisms with CFR. Our method combines techniques such as Dueling network architecture, Double Qlearning, C51 (distributed value function estimation), NoisyNet, Prioritized Experience Replay (PER), and N-step returns. Additionally, we introduce a variance-driven Monte Carlo correction module to stabilize counterfactual value estimation and improve learning efficiency. We evaluated RainbowCFR in two poker environments: Leduc Hold'em and Heads-up No-limit Texas Hold'em (HNLH). Experimental results show that RainbowCFR outperforms DeepCFR, NFSP, and D2CFR in terms of convergence speed and policy stability, with significant advantages in Leduc Hold'em and robust performance in HNLH, demonstrating the potential of combining Rainbow DQN mechanisms with CFR in IIGs.

title: Design and Validation of an Optimized Data Transmission Scheme for High Orbit Satellites
authors: Wenjie Du, Tingting Shi
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: With the rapid growth of satellite communication d ata demand, limited frequency resources and low transmission ra tes have become bottlenecks in high-orbit satellite-ground end-to-end TCP transmission. This paper designs an innovative data tra nsmission scheme and verifies its effectiveness. The results show <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{t}$</tex> hat the lightweight TCP-QUIC PROXY scheme performs very we II in reducing connection delay, improving congestion/flow contro 1 efficiency, and supporting multiplexing and connection migratio n. Hardware-in-the-loop simulations based on a prototype system confirm its superiority: the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 5 0 M b p s}$</tex> downlink rate is increased by more than <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 0 0}$</tex> times, and the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 0 M b p s}$</tex> uplink rate by more tha n 7 times.

title: Spectrally Constrained VAE-WGAN-GP for EMG and Ultrasound RF Signal Synthesis in Hand Motion Estimation
authors: Sulav Bastola, Coskun Tekes
abstract: Data-driven models for continuous hand motion estimation rely on large, well-aligned multimodal datasets, yet collecting extensive surface electromyography (EMG) and ultrasound radiofrequency (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R F}$</tex>) data is time-consuming and physically demanding. Physiological variability and subject dependence further constrain data availability, motivating the use of synthetic data generation to support scalable learning. This paper presents a spectrally constrained Variational Autoencoder-Wasserstein Generative Adversarial Network with Gradient Penalty (VAE-WGAN-GP) for signal-domain synthesis of EMG and ultrasound RF data. The proposed framework integrates structured latent representation learning, adversarial distribution matching, and a multi-resolution time-frequency spectral loss to preserve physiologically meaningful temporal and spectral characteristics. Synthetic EMG and ultrasound RF signals are generated independently and used to augment training data for a deep learning-based regression model that estimates continuous hand flexion-extension. Experimental results on multimodal EMG and ultrasound RF recordings from multiple subjects demonstrate that incorporating synthetic data enables reliable continuous hand motion prediction while substantially reducing reliance on real training data. In particular, prediction performance and subject-level generalization are largely preserved even when up to 50 % of the training data are replaced with synthetic signals, highlighting the potential of physiologyaware generative modeling as an effective strategy for mitigating data scarcity in continuous neuromuscular modeling applications.

title: Social Network Node Embedding Optimization Algorithm Based on Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN) and its Application in Community Detection
authors: Xinrui Huang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: The widespread presence of structural noise and weak connections in social networks makes it difficult for standard graph convolution-based node embedding to characterize real community boundaries. This paper proposes a structure-consistency-constrained graph convolutional network (SCC-GCN) node embedding optimization method. A structure-consistency weight, jointly defined by local connectivity pattern similarity and Laplace spectral response, is introduced into the graph convolutional message passing to continuously modulate neighborhood information. Furthermore, topological variational regularization constrains the consistency between the embedding spatial distance distribution and the local spectral structure of the original graph, thereby suppressing noise propagation and over-smoothing of the structure. Under a unified optimization framework, a community-structure-oriented node representation is obtained. Experimental results show that the proposed method achieves a modularity of 0.712, a normalized mutual information (NMI) of 0.684, and an adjusted Rand index (ARI) of 0.769 in the social network community detection task, while maintaining a modularity of 0.679 and an ARI of 0.731 even under 20 % structural noise. These results validate the proposed method's effective ability to model fine-grained community structures in complex social networks.

title: A Wearable Multi-Modal Sensing Fusion Approach for Physical Fatigue Monitoring
authors: Fariha Alam, Razvan Voicu
abstract: Physical fatigue is a progressive physiological state characterized by reduced muscle efficiency, diminished motor performance, and altered autonomic responses, which can affect productivity, safety, and overall well-being. Beyond occupational settings, continuous monitoring of physical fatigue is essential for diverse populations as fatigue develops gradually and can transition from acute to chronic if not managed early. This study investigates how a wearable sensing system can be developed to effectively monitor physical fatigue and help users track it proactively. A multi-modal wrist-worn device integrating electromyography (EMG), electrodermal activity (EDA), and a tri-axial accelerometer was designed to capture complementary physiological signals and motion data associated with fatigue progression. Data were collected from five participants under varying physically fatiguing activities, and a novel preprocessing pipeline was introduced to reduce motion artifacts and noise while in real-world use. Features were extracted, scaled, fused, and selected by balancing model performance with system-level efficiency. Using a Random Forest classifier, the system achieved 97.30 % accuracy with 97.04 % macro F1 score and 97.18% macro recall in predicting the discrete physical activities that act as fatigue-inducing stressors, thereby enabling assessment of fatigue progression. The multi-modal, wrist-worn system delivers robust, context-aware monitoring in a lightweight and comfortable form factor, demonstrating a practical and scalable path toward continuous real-world fatigue management.

title: CSN-SPD: A Super Node Prioritized Dispatching Algorithm for Taming Communication Walls in Massive AI Clusters
authors: Liang Guo, Lina Xie, Jiayuan Li, Jie Li
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: To overcome the “communication wall” where Model FLOPs Utilization (MFU) drops below 30% in 4,096-node clusters, this paper proposes a Computing Star Network (CSN) architecture leveraging Super-nodes for architectural “logical folding”. We introduce an Interconnected Energy Level coefficient <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\zeta)$</tex> to quantify the scale-up advantages of high-speed bus matrices and unified memory addressing. Based on this framework, the CSN-SPD algorithm is designed to implement “gravity suppression,” proactively anchoring communication-intensive tasks within high-bandwidth super-node domains. Extensive simulations demonstrate that the proposed scheme sustains a 71.8 % MFU and achieves a 92.9 % reduction in synchronization latency compared to DCQCN-enabled baselines at a 4,096-node scale. Our results validate that energy-level quantization is critical for deterministic performance in hyperscale AI infrastructure.

title: Enhanced Divide-and-Conquer: A Hybrid Neural Network and Optimization Method for Inverse Source Problems
authors: Xiaotong Liu, Chuang Yi, Zhengyang Liu, Zheng Lei, Chaoye Chen, Haonan Wu, Shaomei Fang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Multi-source localization under limited-aperture and sparse-sensing conditions is a highly ill-posed inverse problem. Conventional direct methods are susceptible to instability under limited-aperture measurements, whereas deep-learning-based approaches are often constrained by permutation ambiguity in multi-source prediction. This paper aims to develop a dataefficient localization framework using sparse, single-frequency boundary measurements acquired on a semi-circular curve. To this end, we propose an enhanced Divide-and-Conquer framework. First, a lightweight network is employed to estimate the source count. Subsequently, we introduce a recursive coordinate prediction scheme coupled with polar coordinate parameterization, which mitigates permutation ambiguity at the architectural level and reduces both output dimensionality and training difficulty. Finally, the initial guesses are refined via physics-informed, gradient-based optimization to achieve highprecision results. Experiments on two-dimensional synthetic datasets demonstrate that the proposed hybrid method achieves accurate localization and high computational efficiency for various source counts. Overall, this paper presents a structured and reproducible approach to address the combined challenges of sparse-sensing, limited-aperture, and multi-source localization, thereby laying a foundation for engineering applications in scenarios with limited sampling, such as acoustic and electromagnetic imaging.

title: A Pseudo-Code Measurement Combined with Frequency-Hopping Automatic Traversal Method for Ultra-Wideband Group Delay Calibration
authors: Zhangjian Lu, Weiwei Wang, Guoyong Wang, Zongpan Zhang, Xin Ji, Lianjun Xu, Keyuan Yang, Pan Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: With the explosive growth of requirements for data rate and precision in wireless communications, radar, and other related fields, modern communication systems featuring spreadspectrum, frequency-hopping, and ultra-large bandwidth have experienced rapid development. However, the non-flat characteristic of signal group delay has become a critical bottleneck restricting their performance. To address the impact of group delay on system performance in ultra-large bandwidth communication systems, this paper proposes a joint correction method based on pseudo-code measurement combined with automatic frequency-hopping traversal. High-precision measurement of group delay at a single frequency point is achieved using pseudo-code signals. All operating frequency points within the GHz bandwidth are covered via automatic frequency-hopping traversal technology, and the phase compensation value is calculated according to the measurement results. Both simulation analysis and experimental verification demonstrate that the proposed method can accurately measure the system group delay and significantly improve the tracking performance of broadband spread-spectrum and frequency-hopping systems.

title: Heuristics for Ethereum Block Building With Semantic Interactions
authors: Jazmín María del Luján Gamarra Benitez
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Ethereum block building has traditionally been approached using greedy algorithms that prioritize transactions with the highest fee per unit of gas. This work proposes an alternative that considers semantic interactions among transactions and operational constraints to define the utility of transaction combinations. Through a utility model that assigns bonuses and penalties to pairs and triples of transactions, we design algorithms capable of constructing blocks more valuable than those obtained by traditional methods. In experiments with 1,000 real transactions extracted from the network, two algorithms were implemented and evaluated, both grounded in a formulation inspired by job scheduling theory: a classic greedy baseline and the proposed heuristic.
