abstract: To address the high missed-detection rate and severe identity switches in pedestrian multi-object tracking under crowded scenes with frequent occlusions, this paper proposes a pedestrian tracking method based on improved YOLOv8n and ByteTrack. On the detection side, the standard downsampling module is replaced with the ADown module to enhance feature preservation while reducing computational overhead, and a Dynamic Head (DyHead) is introduced to improve the discrimination capability for densely distributed targets. On the tracking side, a pedestrian occlusion filtering algorithm is integrated into ByteTrack to remove redundant or nested detections caused by occlusions, thereby reducing mismatches and identity switches and improving trajectory continuity and tracking robustness. Experimental results show that, compared with the baseline method, the proposed approach achieves a 4.7 percentage-point improvement in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{mAP} {@} 50$</tex> on the public MOT20 dataset, while improving tracking performance by 9.3 percentage points in MOTA and 8.8 percentage points in IDF1, and reducing IDSW by 90, with real-time performance maintained. Overall, the proposed method achieves a favorable balance among accuracy, robustness, and efficiency, providing an effective solution for pedestrian multi-object tracking in complex scenarios.

title: Customer Financial Fraud Detection and Risk Profiling Using a Cloud-Native Swarm of AI Agents on AWS
authors: Vimal Pradeep Venugopal
abstract: Financial institutions increasingly require real-time fraud detection and risk profiling systems that can operate at scale while satisfying regulatory requirements for explainability, auditability, and fault isolation. Traditional rule-based systems and monolithic machine learning pipelines struggle to adapt to rapidly changing market conditions and heterogeneous data sources. This paper proposes a cloud-native, agentic architecture for customer financial fraud detection and risk profiling using a swarm of autonomous AI agents orchestrated on AWS. The proposed system decomposes risk assessment into specialized agents responsible for customer profiling, transaction analysis, repayment behavior evaluation, market condition analysis, and decision summarization. These agents collaborate through an event-driven orchestration layer with shared contextual memory, enabling parallel execution, resilience, and adaptive reasoning. The architecture leverages AWS Strands Agents, Amazon Bedrock for foundation model inference, and managed cloud services such as Amazon S3, DynamoDB, and Simple Notification Service (SNS). Experimental results from a prototype implementation demonstrate improved modularity, scalability, and interpretability compared to centralized risk analysis approaches. The proposed design illustrates how agentic AI systems support regulated financial workflows while maintaining transparency and operational robustness.

title: Escaping Local Minima: A Finite-Time Markov Chain Analysis of Constant-Temperature Simulated Annealing
authors: Hansini Ramachandran, Bhaskar Krishnamachari
abstract: Simulated Annealing (SA) is a widely used stochastic optimization algorithm, yet much of its theoretical understanding is limited to asymptotic convergence guarantees or general spectral bounds. In this paper, we develop a finite-time analytical framework for constant-temperature SA by studying a piecewise linear cost function that permits exact characterization. We model SA as a discrete-state Markov chain and first derive a closed-form expression for the expected time to escape a single linear basin in a one-dimensional landscape. We show that this expression also accurately predicts the behavior of continuous-state search up to a constant scaling factor, which we analyze empirically and explain via variance matching, demonstrating convergence to a factor of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sqrt{3}$</tex> in certain regimes. We then extend the analysis to a two-basin landscape containing a local and a global optimum, obtaining exact expressions for the expected time to reach the global optimum starting from the local optimum, as a function of basin geometry, neighborhood radius, and temperature. Finally, we demonstrate how the predicted basin escape time can be used to guide the design of a simple two-temperature switching strategy.

title: Evaluating the errors of Oceansat-3 winds over the Indian Ocean_supp1-3685171.docx
authors: Krishna K K. Osuri
abstract: The Oceansat-3 scatterometer (O3SCAT) winds, operating in the Ku-band frequency, launched by the Indian Space Research Organization, are evaluated by collocating against OMNI, RAMA buoy, ship, ASCAT, and ERA5 reanalysis for different basins over the Indian Ocean (IO), viz., tropical-IO (TIO), north-IO (NIO), and south-IO (SIO), and different seasons during April 2023 to December 2024. O3SCAT shows an increasing bias in wind speed (WS) as wind strength increases, with overestimation at low WS and underestimation at medium and high WS. Whereas wind direction (WD) shows the opposite trend, with a decrease in bias as wind strengthens. Overall, the O3SCAT accuracies are comparable to the mission requirements of 1.8 ms<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup> and 20°. NIO exhibits the highest RMSD (up to 2.04 ms<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup> and 27.01°), followed by TIO (up to 1.99 ms<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup> and 27.01°), and SIO (up to 1.93 ms<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup> and 20.71°), with the Arabian Sea contributing to higher error than the Bay of Bengal. Seasonal verification over the TIO exhibits the highest error of WS (2.08 ms<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup>) during the monsoon season, and of WD during the post-monsoon season (41.88°) compared to OMNI buoy and ship data, with the highest seasonal variability. However, a consecutive triple collocation analysis shows the presence of inherent noise in OMNI buoy and ship datasets, though O3SCAT agrees well with RAMA buoy, ASCAT, and ERA5 datasets. Further analysis reveals a limitation of L2B 25 km O3SCAT data in capturing maximum winds of ocean vortices with a comparatively high RMSD of 6.51 ms<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup>. This study thus ensures the quality of O3SCAT-derived winds to the level required for assimilation in numerical models.

title: A Novel Data-Driven Prediction and Compensation Method for Robot Contour Errors Considering the Joint Dynamics
authors: Hongwei Sun, Shizhong Tan, Jixiang Yang, Han Ding
abstract: The prediction and compensation of contour errors are essential for improving the accuracy of robotic operations. However, accurate prediction of robot joint tracking errors is challenged by coupled factors, including pose-dependent dynamics and strong nonlinear friction during low-speed reversals. Moreover, unsmoothed compensation values will deteriorate robot kinematics, leading to undesirable effects, such as jitter and increased contour errors. To address these challenges, a data-driven robot tracking error prediction and contour error compensation method is proposed by considering robot dynamics. First, a history-future information embedded tracking error prediction framework is proposed, which leverages the long short-term memory network to extract kinematic, dynamic, and load features. In addition, a reversal feature, incorporating the nonlinear friction with robot motion states, is designed to improve prediction accuracy during low-speed reversals. Then, to address velocity fluctuations from nonsmooth compensation, a polynomial-based smoothing method is proposed to fit the compensation trajectory within contour error mutation intervals, effectively mitigating kinematic degradation and additional contour errors. Finally, experimental results show that the proposed tracking error prediction method achieves higher accuracy than existing methods, especially in low-speed reversal intervals. Moreover, the smoothing compensation method outperforms direct compensation strategies by mitigating kinematic fluctuations and improving contour accuracy.

title: Preserving Discrete Morse–Smale Complexes in Error-Bounded Lossy Compression
authors: Yuxiao Li, Mingze Xia, Xin Liang, Bei Wang, Hanqi Guo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Scientific applications are generating unprecedented volumes of data that overwhelm storage and transmission systems, posing significant challenges for the design of data management tools and scientific databases. Lossy compression has emerged as a promising strategy to address this problem, but most existing compressors fail to preserve the topology of scientific data, leading to inaccuracies in downstream analyses and potentially erroneous scientific conclusions. In this work, we present a methodology for fully preserving the topology, specifically, Morse-Smale complexes (MSCs), in lossy-compressed 2D and 3D scalar field data from scientific simulations. We generalize the edit-based strategy introduced in MSz [1] (a previous method that preserves only segmentations and cannot preserve saddles or separatrices) by extending the framework to the full MSCs, including all critical points and separatrices. Our approach corrects the MSCs in the decompressed output of any error-bounded lossy compressor (e.g., SZ3 or ZFP), referred to as the base compressor, using an iterative editing strategy that preserves all critical points and their connectivity via separatrices. During compression, we generate a sequence of quantized edits that are applied to the decompressed output, ensuring accurate preservation of topological features while maintaining the error within prescribed bounds. The strategy iteratively fixes critical points and separatrices in alternating steps until convergence is achieved in a finite number of iterations. To meet diverse application needs, our method offers flexible options (e.g., whether to preserve the geometry of separatrices) that balance compression efficiency with feature preservation. To reduce computation time, we leverage GPU parallelism to accelerate each component of the workflow. Experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate that our method achieves 100% preservation of Morse-Smale complexes.

title: M-GBT: Multi-region Short-term Electricity Load Forecasting Framework based on Graph Bayesian Transformer Network
authors: Shiqi Zhou, Zhekang Dong, Junfan Wang, Xiaoyue Ji, Yujie Yuan, Chun Sing Lai
abstract: Accurate forecasting of short-term electricity load is key to achieving efficient household energy management, optimizing resource allocation, and ensuring reliable grid operation. However, existing methods still suffer from inherent constraints in data distributional assumptions and inter-regional correlation modelling, which jointly compromise the forecasting accuracy and practical applicability. Based on this, a multi-region short-term electricity load forecasting framework that explicitly captures dynamic data distributions and inter-regional dependencies is developed. Two key components constitute the forecasting framework: a graph neural network and a Bayesian Transformer. The former is designed to explicitly construct graph structural features across multiple regions, facilitating inter-regional correlation modelling. The latter integrates a hybrid attention mechanism, allowing it to better cope with evolving load data profiles and deliver robust probabilistic forecasting. Correspondingly, an adaptive learning strategy with the ability to capture multi-region dependencies is designed. Furthermore, experimental validation on two real-world multi-region datasets (New England dataset, London household dataset) shows that the proposed forecasting framework surpasses established benchmarks, with average improvements of 21.2%, 25.6%, and 24.8% in RMSE, pinball loss, and CRPS, respectively.

title: Unifying Variables in Neural Scaling for General Audio Representations via Embedding Effective Rank
authors: Xuyao Deng, Yanjie Sun, Yong Dou, Kele Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: In neural scaling, scaling laws have profoundly shaped our understanding of model performance across easily scalable and expressible parameters. However, when scaling involves multiple parameters that present complex expressions or are difficult to express, it becomes challenging to formulate scaling functions that encompass such parameters. In this work, We found that in the audio domain, the embedding effective rank (RankMe) can serve as a unifying metric that enables label-free, information-theoretic quantification of audio embeddings, encapsulates the impact of diverse variables including masking rate, model size, data volume, computational budget, and architectural configurations on representation quality and demonstrates more significant effectiveness compared to traditional scaling laws. Our empirical findings reveal a consistent power-law relationship between RankMe and representation quality, suggesting that embedding effective rank serves as a reliable proxy for assessing model performance in audio representation learning. Based on RankMe as a label-free proxy metric, our work demonstrates its role as an early-stage monitoring tool. Furthermore, we provide an empirically validated framework for early screening of optimal models from multiple complex hyperparameter settings during pre-training, which saves computational resources.

title: Admittance Reshaping Method for Charging Current Ripple Suppression of the Battery Energy Storage System under Distorted Grid Conditions
authors: Xin Tang, Yuchen Yu, Kaixuan Tang, Peijun Zhong, Xiong Zhou
abstract: Grid voltage background harmonics can induce harmonic currents in the energy storage converter (ESC). Furthermore, the frequency-coupling characteristic of the battery energy storage system (BESS), which induces new harmonic current components, can result in current ripple during battery charging. This paper proposes an admittance-reshaping method to mitigate the influence of grid background harmonics on the battery charging current of the BESS. Firstly, the small-signal admittance model of the ESC is established. Then, a theoretical analysis and mathematical derivation of the charging current ripple are conducted. The general forms of the inner current-control loop and outer power-control loop admittance-reshaping factors are then developed to reshape the admittance model into a symmetrical system within the dq frame, as a result, the harmonic components are reduced. Furthermore, the admittance-reshaping factors' parameters are appropriately selected to reduce the magnitude of harmonics. By decoupling the system and lowering the admittance value of the ESC, the quality of the grid-connected current is improved, while simultaneously suppressing the charging current ripple. The feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed admittance-reshaping method are verified by the presented simulation and experimental results.

title: Parasitic PNP Punch-Through Suppression and BV Enhancement of 1200V Double-Thin SOI-LDMOS for Gate Driver HVIC
authors: Dingxiang Ma, Jingchuan Zhao, Feifan Yu, Chao Dai, Teng Liu, Shenghuai Liu, Guoliang Yao, Nailong He, Sen Zhang, Bo Zhang, Ming Qiao
abstract: This paper proposes a double-thin silicon on insulator LDMOS (DT SOI-LDMOS) characterized by thin buried oxide (BOX, 0.4 μm) and thin SOI (0.11 μm) for better heat-dissipation and reduction of parasitic capacitance in gate driver HVIC. Based on novel substrate N-Well (SN-Well) and multiple P-Well ring (MPWR) design, the DT SOI-LDMOS effectively suppresses parasitic PNP and optimize the electric field to achieve higher breakdown voltage (BV). Fabrication of the DT SOI-LDMOS can be easily realized by using standard recipe like high energy ion-implantation, LOCOS and contacts for different layers. According to simulations, design of SN-Well shows an improvement in early voltage from -212 V to -460 V and enables a 20 μm reduction in cell-pitch. Consequently, the suppressed PNP allows for the inclusion of more P-Well rings to realize high avalanche off-state BV (BVOFF). Besides, simulations were performed to show a 22% lattice temperature decrease for 0.4 μm BOX, demonstrating the merit of thin BOX. In experiment, the measured results confirm the SN-Well in suppressing the parasitic PNP and the MPWR in enhancing BV, achieving avalanche BVOFF of 1380 V and on-state breakdown voltage (BVON) of 1400 V.

title: Adaptive Spatial-Temporal Graph Learning-Enabled Short-Term Voltage Stability Assessment Against Time-Varying Topological Conditions
authors: Chao Deng, Lipeng Zhu, Chang Liu, Hefeng Zhai, Baoye Tian, Zexiang Zhu, Jiayong Li, Cong Zhang
abstract: The emerging deep learning (DL) technology has recently exhibited great potential in data-driven short-term voltage stability (SVS) assessment of complex power grids. However, without sufficient attention to the time-varying topological structures of today's power grids, the majority of existing DL-based SVS assessment schemes could experience severe performance degradation in practice. To address this drawback, this paper proposes an adaptive spatial-temporal graph learning-enabled SVS assessment approach that can adapt well to various topological changes. First, considering the time-varying topological conditions of a given power grid, an adaptive graph representation matrix is automatically learned to effectively capture the complicated spatial correlations between individual buses within the grid. Then, to help better capture regional SVS features for subsequent learning processes, the adaptive graph representation matrix is properly adjusted by introducing a spatial attention mechanism. Further, with post-fault system trajectory data linked together via attention-based graph representation, a residual spatiotemporal graph convolutional network is carefully built with Optuna-based optimization to deeply mine system-wide spatiotemporal features and thus achieve structure-adaptive SVS assessment. Numerical test results on two representative sub-systems of a realistic provincial power grid in South China demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed approach under various changing topological conditions.

title: A Vision-Driven End-User Robot Programming Method Based on the Alignment Between Body Language and Motion Commands
authors: Shipeng Lyu, Shenzeng Huo, Wanyu Ma, Guodong Guo, David Navarro-Alarcon
abstract: This work is motivated by the complexity of conventional robot programming methods, which require a deep understanding of both robotics and software development. This situation presents significant challenges for nontechnical end-users who need to program a new robotic task. To address this issue, we propose a new robot programming method that enables humans to specify robot motion tasks using simple and intuitive body-language commands. To this end, we propose a triadic task decomposition framework to decompose complex robot motion tasks into three basic task units, i.e., location, operation, and motion. These units are paired with a set of body-language cues that enable end-users to specify a variety of actions and motions, providing an intuitive alternative to traditional robot programming methods. To program these units, we develop a multimodal fusion-based instruction-parsing algorithm with a dual-phase visual architecture, enabling temporally aware body-language recognition and high-accuracy cross-modal object localization. Furthermore, a semantics-adaptive task execution system is proposed, which incorporates an intent reasoning module that dynamically handles end-users' nonstandard programming sequences and incomplete task logic, enhancing programming success rates and system usability. We evaluate the proposed method on a dual-arm robotic platform and a humanoid robot through various pick-and-place tasks, and compare its performance with that of several baselines from the literature.

title: CLAPC: A Hybrid CNN-LSTM Architecture for Automated Passenger Counting from Video Streams in Public Transport
authors: Sambu Seo, Klaus Obermayer
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Information on passenger occupancy in public transport is crucial for service providers to enable accurate revenue sharing within transportation networks. This requires reliable methods to count boarding and alighting passengers and to differentiate between passenger categories, such as those requiring a valid ticket. In previous work, a recurrent Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network (NAPC) was applied to depth videos captured by time-of-flight sensors mounted above train doors to perform passenger counting and height-based differentiation. In this work, we investigate whether this approach can be transferred to 2D gray-scale video data recorded from the same overhead perspective, where explicit depth information is unavailable. To this end, we extend the LSTM architecture by integrating a convolutional neural network, resulting in the CLAPC model, which improves the extraction of image features. Additionally, region-of-interest cropping and spatial down-sampling are explored to better address privacy concerns, and the training procedure is refined to reduce undercounting. Applying CLAPC to depth data improves counting accuracy compared to NAPC. Moreover, high accuracies are achieved on 2D video data despite the absence of explicit depth information. The highest performance is obtained using down-sampled door-path crops, yielding mean accuracies of 99.73% for boarding and 99.87% for alighting passengers. On depth data, accuracies of 99.79% and 99.97% are achieved for boarding and alighting, respectively. These results meet the ± 1% accuracy requirement of VDV Guideline 457. The 95% confidence intervals from an equivalence test lie within the ± 1% bounds, which confirms that CLAPC is statistically robust.

title: Ultrathin Dual Circularly Polarized Transmitarray Antennas Based on Polarization Conversion Huygens' Metasurface
authors: Haibo Wang, Chunhua Xue, Rui Wang, Teng Li, Xi Gao, Zhi Ning Chen
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This paper presents a Huygens' metasurface integrated with polarization conversion for designing dual circularly polarized (DCP) transmitarray antenna (TAA). With the proposed double-resonant unit cells, the metasurface provides full transmission phase coverage for two linear polarizations (LPs) and maintains a stable 90° phase difference across a broad bandwidth, enabling the conversion of ±45°-LP waves into DCP waves with precise phase control. When applied to a TAA, it supports DCP high-gain beams. Simulations and measurements validate the design. For left-handed CP (LHCP), aperture efficiencies of 32.28% is achieved, with 3-dB gain bandwidths of 17.5% and 3-dB axial ratio (AR) bandwidths of 16.8%. For right-handed CP (RHCP), aperture efficiencies of 32.43% is achieved, with 3-dB gain bandwidths of 17.1% and 3-dB AR bandwidths of 17.9%. These results offer valuable insights for future millimeter-wave communications.

title: POTR: Post-Training 3DGS Compression
authors: Bert Ramlot, Martijn Courteaux, Peter Lambert, Glenn Van Wallendael
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has recently emerged as a promising contender to Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) in 3D scene reconstruction and real-time novel view synthesis. 3DGS outperforms NeRF in training and inference speed but has substantially higher storage requirements. To remedy this downside, we propose POTR, a post-training 3DGS codec built on two novel techniques. First, POTR introduces a novel pruning approach that uses a modified 3DGS rasterizer to efficiently calculate every splat’s individual removal effect simultaneously. This technique results in 2-4× fewer splats than other post-training pruning techniques and as a result also significantly accelerates inference with experiments demonstrating 1.5-2× faster inference than other compressed models. Second, we propose a novel method to recompute lighting coefficients, significantly reducing their entropy without using any form of training. Our fast and highly parallel approach especially increases AC lighting coefficient sparsity, with experiments demonstrating increases from 70% to 97%, with minimal loss in quality. Finally, we extend POTR with a simple fine-tuning scheme to further enhance pruning, inference, and rate-distortion performance. Experiments demonstrate that POTR, even without fine-tuning, consistently outperforms all other post-training compression techniques in both rate-distortion performance and inference speed.

title: An application of the mean motion problem to time-optimal control
authors: Omri Dalin, Alexander Ovseevich, Michael Margaliot
abstract: We consider time-optimal controls of a controllable linear system. It is well-known (see, e.g., [1, Theorem 6-8]) that if all the eigenvalues of the matrix describing the linear system dynamics are real then any time-optimal control has a bounded number of switching points, where the bound does not depend on the length of the time interval. Here, we consider the case where the governing matrix has purely imaginary eigenvalues, and show that then, in the generic case, the number of switching points is bounded from below by a linear function of the length of the time interval. The proof is based on relating the switching function in the optimal control problem to the mean motion problem that dates back to Lagrange and was solved by Herman Weyl.

title: Automatic detection of suicidal ideation on social media using a hybrid CNNBiLSTM architecture
authors: Sadala Nagib Salame Neto, Kaique da Silva Pinto, Silvio Tadeu Teles da Silva, Miércio Cardoso de Alcântara Neto
abstract: Suicide is a serious public health problem, responsible for more than 700,000 deaths annually worldwide, according to the World Health Organization. Early identification of signs of suicidal ideation can support prevention initiatives and reduce fatal outcomes. This study investigated the application of Natural Language Processing (NLP) and deep learning techniques for the automatic detection of suicidal ideation in social media posts. A hybrid architecture based on Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM), combined with convolutional layers and regularization strategies, was proposed. The evaluation was conducted on a Reddit dataset processed in two versions: with and without stopword removal. The results showed that the proposed architecture achieved high performance in both versions, reaching an accuracy of 95.77% and an F1-score of 95.79% on the test set without stopword removal. Despite the higher computational cost, this approach outperformed previous studies reported in the literature. In summary, the proposed model demonstrates potential to support early monitoring strategies for suicidal ideation in digital environments, offering a relevant contribution to public health and suicide prevention.

title: Selective Active Zero-State Pulse-Width Modulation (AZSPWM) for Reduction of Common-Mode Voltage (CMV) of Three-Phase Inverter Considering Saturation of Filter Inductor
authors: Jaekeun Lee, Shanshan Zhao, Chaeyoung Jeong, Kiwoo Park, Shenghui Cui
abstract: Conventional active zero-state PWM (AZSPWM) techniques are effective in limiting common-mode voltage (CMV) but typically assume linear and balanced three-phase filter inductances. In practice, however, filter inductors are designed for high-power density and inevitably operate near magnetic saturation (e.g., using powder-core materials). This results in periodic unbalanced dynamic inductances among the three phases and thereby degrading the effectiveness of the well-established pulsewidth modulation (PWM) techniques for CMV reduction. This paper reveals the failure of existing PWM techniques on CMV reduction under such conditions and proposes a saturation-aware selective AZSPWM method that adaptively selects the optimal pair of opposite active vectors based on predicted CMV. A common-mode equivalent circuit incorporating unbalanced dynamic inductances is derived, and an online CMV estimation framework is developed using an approximated <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$B-H$</tex-math></inline-formula> curve of the inductor to enable optimal AZSPWM selection. Experimental results on a 220 kVA grid-connected industrial inverter demonstrate that the proposed method significantly reduces CMV compared with conventional AZSPWM method, particularly under the high load and high power factor conditions.

title: FedDiDy: Federated Class-Incremental Fault Diagnosis for Industrial IoT Rotating Machinery Under Dynamic Edge Participation
authors: Yanxin Hu, Yan Huang, Zhenzhen Xie, Junjie Pang, Chao Cheng, Zhipeng Cai
abstract: Federated learning (FL) is promising for privacy-sensitive fault diagnosis in the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT). However, real-world deployments must address the coexistence of class-incremental fault evolution and dynamic edge participation, which leads to fragmented class exposure, catastrophic forgetting, and aggregation bias. To address this issue, we propose FedDiDy, a unified framework for federated class-incremental fault diagnosis under dynamic participation. FedDiDy combines a multi-head classifier for task decoupling, a raw-data-free conditional generator for historical knowledge replay, and a perception-aware aggregation mechanism for bias mitigation. Experiments on four datasets under Bernoulli, Cyclic, and Markov participation patterns show that FedDiDy consistently outperforms the compared baselines, with up to 15.1% absolute improvement in average accuracy and 14.44% reduction in forgetting rate.

title: The Impact Analysis of Delays in Asynchronous Federated Learning with Data Heterogeneity for Edge Intelligence
authors: Ziruo Hao, Zhenhua Cui, Tao Yang, Xiaofeng Wu, Hui Feng, Bo Hu
abstract: Federated learning (FL) has provided a new methodology for coordinating a group of clients to train a machine learning model collaboratively, bringing an efficient paradigm in edge intelligence. Despite its promise, FL faces critical challenges in Internet of Things (IoT) networks, particularly the combined impact of data heterogeneity and communication delays. This paper examines the theoretical and empirical impact of these factors in Asynchronous Federated Learning (AFL). Initially, we categorize existing memoryless asynchronous strategies as Asynchronous Updates with Delayed Gradients (AUDG). Our theoretical analysis of AUDG reveals the coupling effect of delays exacerbate the adverse impact of data heterogeneity, causing the global model to drift towards frequently active clients. To address this, we propose a gradient reusing mechanism, termed Pseudo-Synchronous Updates by Reusing Delayed Gradients (PSURDG). By leveraging storage to reuse historical gradients, PSURDG effectively decouples the correlation between delay and data heterogeneity. Crucially, we conducted a comprehensive convergence analysis covering both convex and non-convex settings, confirming the algorithm’s effectiveness in diverse optimization landscapes. Finally, both schemes are validated through rigorous analysis and extensive simulations on multiple datasets. The results demonstrate a clear trade-off that AUDG remains efficient under low data heterogeneity, while PSURDG improves convergence in high-heterogeneity scenarios with moderate delays, thereby providing a theoretical guideline for aggregation strategy selection in different edge intelligence scenarios.

title: MFIR: Model-Free Intrusion Response for Partially Observable Industrial Control Systems
authors: Yingxu Lai, Peng Zhao, Ziqi Wang
abstract: The increasing number of multi-stage attacks has brought severe cybersecurity threats to industrial control system (ICS), making it crucial to develop autonomous and effective intrusion response systems (IRS). Most existing IRS approaches depend on the idealized assumption that the system state is perfectly known and directly available for intelligent control decisions. However, this assumption is rarely satisfied in practice, as state information is typically incomplete and only partially observable, which severely limits the applicability and effectiveness of these methods. To address this challenge, we propose a model-free intrusion response approach (MFIR) tailored to ICS with partially observable states. We formulate intrusion response as a partially observable Markov decision process and construct a simulated ICS with incomplete state observations. Then we use a recurrent neural network to infer the hidden complete states from historical observation sequences, thereby avoiding reliance on prior knowledge such as transition or observation probabilities. Based on the inferred states, we develop a deep reinforcement learning algorithm with a dual-branch neural network to make response decisions. Experiments on a Tennessee Eastman-based ICS testbed demonstrate that MFIR outperforms existing approaches, achieving up to 32.86% higher total reward, fewer compromised nodes, and lower defense cost under various observability levels. These results verify that the proposed approach is well-suited for intrusion response in partially observable ICS environments.

title: DIM-NEG: Dynamic Incentive Model for Federated Learning Based on Smart Contracts and Nested Evolutionary Games
authors: Wei Fan, Xiaohong Deng, Zhigang Chen, Ming Zhao, Guangfu Wu, Kangxu Qiu, Yuqin Hu
abstract: The effectiveness of federated learning (FL) systems relies on extensive client participation. However, the existing incentive mechanisms often overlook the intrinsic coupling between participation and privacy strategies. This oversight leads to mismatches between incentive distributions and clients’ privacy costs and contribution quality, limiting the effectiveness of incentives for enhancing contribution quality and participation scale. To address this, DIM-NEG, a dynamic incentive model based on smart contracts and nested evolutionary games, is proposed. First, we construct a nested evolutionary game framework linking external participation with internal privacy strategies. By utilizing the internal equilibrium payoff as a feedback parameter for the external game, we achieve unified modeling of these coupled decision processes. Second, on the basis of this structure, a functionally decoupled dual-incentive lever mechanism is proposed. The server employs the strategy incentive to guide the internal strategy portfolio and the participation incentive to regulate the participation scale, enabling separate optimization of the strategy composition and overall participation level of the system via hierarchical control. Finally, utilizing blockchain-based smart contracts, we design an automated mechanism that encodes rules on-chain to resolve trust issues associated with centralized servers. A theoretical analysis and simulation results demonstrate that the DIM-NEG model achieves superior global accuracy, training efficiency, and communication cost-effectiveness, while exhibiting strong robustness in non-IID environments. The model adequately motivates users to participate in high-quality data sharing tasks and maintains system stability, thereby maximizing the overall effectiveness of the federated learning system.

title: SafeHDC: Concurrent Uncertainty and Fault Detection in Hyperdimensional Computing
authors: Mahboobe Sadeghipour Roodsari, Vincent Meyers, Mehdi Tahoori
publish: IEEE Transactions on Device and Materials Reliability
abstract: HyperDimensional Computing (HDC) is a brain-inspired machine learning paradigm that encodes data into high-dimensional hypervectors and performs classification through similarity comparisons. Its computational efficiency, one-shot learning capability, and robustness to noise make it attractive for resource-constrained edge applications. However, HDC systems can still exhibit uncertainty when inputs deviate from the training distribution, such as with noisy or Out-of-Distribution (OOD) data, and when hardware faults occur within the encoding or classification stages, potentially compromising reliability in safety-critical scenarios. This work introduces SafeHDC, an ultra-lightweight, concurrent runtime mechanism that detects both input-induced uncertainty and hardware-induced anomalies without model retraining or added latency. SafeHDC identifies noisy and OOD inputs as well as internal faults with negligible hardware cost (2 LUTs, 1 register). Experimental results show that SafeHDC achieves competitive detection accuracy and reaches up to 100% fault-detection coverage in many situations, enabling real-time safety enhancement for HDC accelerators with minimal overhead.

title: Optimal-Feature Driven Adversarial Adaptive Cascade Forest method for Automatic Modulation Recognition
authors: Zhaoyu Xia, Yujie Lin
abstract: Automatic Modulation Recognition (AMR) plays a crucial role in tackling the challenges of spectrum scarcity within the Internet of Things (IoT), facilitating dynamic sensing and allocation of spectrum resources. However, traditional deep learning (DL) based AMR methods necessitate the addition of redundant layers and parameters to improve recognition accuracy, particularly under low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions. Furthermore, these methods heavily rely on features extracted from expert knowledge and high-quality data annotations, leading to significant reconstruction and full-process training costs when adapting to new samples. To tackle these issues, an optimal-feature driven adversarial adaptive cascade forest (OF-ACF) method for AMR is proposed. This method first constructs an optimal feature subset in the high-order domain feature space utilizing augmented recursive feature elimination (ARFE). Subsequently, it employs adversarial training (AT) of the adversarial adaptive cascade forest (AACF) algorithm driven by this subset, thereby enhancing recognition accuracy in low SNR environments and with unknown modulation schemes. The early stopping (ES) and adaptive growing (AG) mechanisms incorporated within the AACF method can effectively mitigate the resource consumption. Furthermore, the visualization of feature contributions through Shapley additive explanations (SHAP) and local interpretable model-agnostic explanations (LIME) significantly enhances the interpretability of the model. The OF-ACF method achieves a recognition accuracy of 70.9% at an SNR of 0 dB on the RadioML2018.01A dataset, representing a 10.96% improvement over the MADWN method. At SNR values exceeding 10dB, the accuracy stabilizes above 98.85%. Ablation experiments confirm the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed method, facilitating high-accuracy recognition speeds at the 1-ms level.

title: From Diversity to Academic Inclusion: Case Study of First Year Students in Software Engineering and Business Informatics (2023-2024)
authors: Kattia Rebeca Rodríguez Brenes, Karlinna Chaves González, Juan Diego Rodríguez Cambroneror
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: STEM programs (Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics) offered by Costa Rica's public universities are in high demand. However, some of these programs, particularly in computing, are perceived as accessible only to certain groups, which may discourage students from pursuing or completing their studies. This case study identifies the social, economic, academic, and technological factors that influence the perception, choice, and persistence of 81 first-year students in STEM careers, particularly in Software Engineering and Business Informatics, at the Universidad Técnica Nacional (UTN) and the Universidad de Costa Rica (UCR). The study was structured using the PICO model, adapted to the educational context, which helped organize the population, proposed interventions, comparison criteria, and expected strategies that promote academic success from the early stages, supported by differentiated methodologies and emerging technologies such as artificial intelligence. These a concrete proposal to guide the next stages of research and institutional action.

title: Comparative Analysis of Machine Learning Models for Misogynistic Hate Speech Detection in Brazilian Portuguese: a Study Using the Mina-Br Dataset
authors: Ana Rúbia Ramos Vicente, Jefferson Oliveira Andrade, Kelly Assis de Souza Gazolli, Karin Satie Komati
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Misogynistic hate speech targets women using language that promotes discrimination and hostility. In Brazil, the scarcity of annotated datasets in Portuguese adds further challenges to automated detection. This study evaluates supervised machine learning models for classifying misogynistic hate speech using the MINA-BR dataset, which contains over 2,000 manually labeled comments. Four experimental scenarios were considered: (1) Bag of Words, (2) TF-IDF (Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency) with unigrams, (3) TF-IDF with bigrams, and (4) BERTimbau. Text preprocessing included lemmatization and removal of stop words. Feature vectors were used as input to six classifiers: Naive Bayes, Support Vector Machines, Logistic Regression, Multilayer Perceptron, Random Forest, and XGBoost. Among the models tested, BERTimbau achieved the best performance, with 87 % accuracy and an F1-score of 0.58 on the test set. The model demonstrated high precision and recall for the majority class, but struggled to identify the minority class. These findings highlight the importance of text representation and balancing strategies for effective hate speech detection in Portuguese-language content.

title: Bio Lab Escape: An Educational Game for Teaching Cellular Biology
authors: Oscar Rojas, Benjamín Robles, Sebastián Jeria, Makarena Abarca, Silvana Roncagliolo, Claudio Cubillos, Verónica Rojas
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: The use of video games is proposed as an effective strategy to address the growing lack of student motivation by integrating the playful dynamics inherent to these resources with the teaching-learning process. In this study, perception and knowledge surveys were administered before and after the use of an educational video game focused on cell biology content. The sample consisted of 40 students who are currently enrolled in or have previously taken courses related to this discipline. The results showed an average increase of 0.07 points in the accuracy rate, suggesting that the video game has significant potential as a reinforcement tool. However, student feedback also revealed various limitations present in the pilot version of the game. These findings demonstrate that the use of video games is a didactic tool with high potential for addressing complex content, such as that found in the field of cell biology.

title: What the #*&% Is a Shader and How Is It Implemented in Unity?
authors: Dr. Carlos Astengo Noguez, Dra. Lorena Martínez Elizalde, Dra. María Raquel Landa Cavazos, Lic. Daniel Lãpez Salas
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Shaders are often perceived as esoteric and daunting, but their fundamental role in computer graphics and the video game industry is both accessible and essential. This article aims to demystify shaders, providing a comprehensive overview from their basic definition to practical implementation in Unity. We will explore the different types of shaders, such as vertex and fragment shaders, and their respective functions in transforming 3D models into 2D images. By breaking down the concepts and explaining the parallel processing advantages of GPUs, we will illustrate how shaders perform complex calculations swiftly and efficiently. Furthermore, the article will guide readers through the steps of creating and implementing shaders within Unity, one of the most popular game development platforms. We aim to equip developers with the knowledge and confidence to leverage shaders in their projects, enhancing visual effects and overall game quality. This holistic approach will make shaders more approachable, empowering both novice and experienced developers to harness their full potential in the video game industry.

title: Embedded Hardware-in-the-Loop Platform for Observer-Based Drug Delivery Control with Remote Monitoring
authors: Rodrigo F. de Souza, Cecília F. Morais, Jonathan M. Palma, Diana C. González
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper presents the design and implementation of a hardware-in-the-loop testbed to validate a drug delivery control strategy based on a discrete-time pharmacokinetic model. The proposed architecture uses two ESP32 microcontrollers: one emulates the physiological drug absorption and distribution process, while the other executes a proportional-integral controller combined with a discrete-time state observer. The controller regulates the drug concentration in a target organ using only blood measurements, reducing the need for invasive sensing. Real-time remote monitoring and reference adjustment are supported through a web-based dashboard built with the Blynk IoT platform. Simulation and experimental results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed system in tracking reference signals, accurately estimating internal states, and maintaining robust performance under embedded constraints. These findings highlight the potential of the system as a low-cost flexible platform for telemedicine-oriented drug delivery applications.

title: Responsible AI in Cloud Security: A Human-Centric Framework for Transparent Threat Detection and Identity Governance
authors: Meletius Mgbeodichimma Igbokwe, Divine Uzodinma, Ugochukwu Ibeawuchi, Uchenna Victor Moses
abstract: Cloud identity management systems process millions of authentication decisions daily through AI automation, yet this creates a trust paradox: users must depend on systems they cannot observe or control. When AI-driven conditional access policies deny authentication requests, users receive minimal explanation beyond generic errors, undermining both security effectiveness and user confidence. We address this through a human-centric framework integrating automated threat detection with explainable AI (XAI) interfaces and human oversight mechanisms. Security assessments of Azure Active Directory combined with user surveys <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(n=62)$</tex> support three design principles for responsible AI in enterprise security applications: graduated automation based on risk levels (achieving 87-100% detection accuracy for reconnaissance and credential attacks in controlled conditions), mandatory XAI interfaces exposing decision logic, and preserved user agency through override capabilities. Survey findings reveal 94% of users prioritize security infrastructure visibility over cost <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\chi^{2}= 14.3, p<0.001$</tex>), yet 71% expect providers to manage security—demonstrating demand for transparent automation. Organizations receiving clear AI explanations implemented 68% of security recommendations versus 42% without explanations <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\chi^{2}=8.7, p<0.01$</tex>). Our framework aligns with NIST AI RMF 1.0 and IEEE P7001 transparency standards, demonstrating that responsible AI deployment in cybersecurity systems requires deliberate architectural choices balancing automation efficiency with human-centric transparency.

title: Main Challenges of National Transmission Network Expansion: A Study on Energy Efficiency and Development
authors: Patricia Sebajos Vaz, Miguel Edgar Morales Udaeta, André Luiz da Veiga Gimenes, Viviane Tavares Nascimento, Vanessa Meloni Massara
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: The expansion of the electric transmission network is a strategic component to ensure the efficiency, reliability, and sustainability of the Brazilian energy system. This article examines the main challenges faced in this process, with emphasis on the Northeast Region, where the high penetration of renewable sources such as wind and solar imposes additional demands on the flow infrastructure. The research adopts a qualitative approach, combining literature review and documentary analysis of technical and regulatory data. As a case study, the situations of the states of Bahia, Rio Grande do Norte and Piauí are analyzed. The results reveal that, despite the advances in public policies and transmission auctions, there are still bottlenecks related to environmental licensing, the gap between generation and flow, and institutional fragmentation in energy planning. Based on this, guidelines are proposed for integrated and coordinated planning, which aligns the expansion of the electricity grid with the demands of the energy transition.

title: Beyond Centralized Control: Can Peer-to-Peer Systems Support Online Safety for Teens and Older Adults?
authors: Sabin Banjara, Utsav Singh, Arup Ghosh
abstract: Protecting online safety is a growing challenge for teenagers and older adults, who are increasingly exposed to cyberbullying, harmful content, online grooming, misinformation, and financial scams. Most existing safety tools are built on centralized client–server platforms. While these systems enable large-scale moderation and enforcement, they also introduce risks related to privacy, censorship, data misuse, and single points of failure. This paper investigates whether peer-to-peer (P2P) architectures could offer a meaningful alternative for supporting online safety. We present a systematic review of 39 studies published between 2000 and 2025, spanning research on online safety as well as client–server and peer-to-peer system design. Our analysis shows that P2P systems provide many key advantages—particularly when it comes to privacy, resilience, and community-based trust—but struggle to support critical safety functions such as coordinated moderation, identity verification, large-scale fraud, and abuse detection. Rather than viewing P2P and client–server systems as competing models, we argue that online safety is best supported through hybrid architectures that combine centralized oversight with decentralized peer-based support. Such hybrids offer a promising path toward more trustworthy, resilient, and user-centered online safety systems for vulnerable populations.

title: Design, Implementation, and Evaluation of a Cloud-Native Real-Time Distributed System Using Kubernetes and AWS
authors: Nitish Mane, Kapil Jadhav
abstract: The cloud-native platforms help to create scalable distributed systems but realtime predictable performance is still a daunting task to achieve, mainly because of the resource virtualization and dynamic scheduling systems. The given study outlines the design, implementation, and empirical evaluation of a cloud-native distributed system in realtime on Amazon Web Services (AWS) with the help of Kubernetes. There is the interpretation of latency-sensitive autoscaling and orchestration in the architecture to meet the strict realtime requirements. Empirical evidence shows that mean end-to-end latency increases between 45ms and 120ms with an increase of request rates between 100 and 500 requests/ second and indicates almost linear response to the addition of up to five service replicas. Moreover, the system has a well-defined autoscaling behavior and can recover immediately after failures have been deliberately injected, which highlights the practicability of achieving so-called realtime performance in cloud-native systems without compromising scalability or reliability.

title: Mining User Forums to Evaluate Quality-in-Use of Environmental Software
authors: Yvette D. Hastings, Jeffrey C. Carver, A. Redempta Manzi Muneza, Robert A. Payn, Stephanie A. Ewing, Stephan Warnat, Ann Marie Reinhold
abstract: Environmental research software applications are foundational tools for modeling and predicting Earth system dynamics. Among these, reactive transport models (RTMs) are particularly important because they simulate critical system processes that affect contaminant transport and water quality. Many RTMs are developed by domain scientists rather than software engineers, and have quality-in-use (QIU) issues that frustrate end users. Addressing these frustrations requires a clear understanding of the problems and challenges that users experience. We characterize the QIU frustrations most concerning to RTM users using a systematic approach. Specifically, we analyze RTM user forums and codify user-reported challenges by operationalizing the QIU characteristics and subcharacteristics defined in the ISO/IEC 25019:2023 standard. We extracted 3,941 forum threads from four widely used RTM user forums. Terms and phrases related to QIU barriers were identified using natural language processing (NLP) and mapped to QIU subcharacteristics to calculate their frequencies. The QIU characteristic of Beneficialness challenged users most frequently. Within Beneficialness, Usability caused the most frustration across the four forums, suggesting users struggle to achieve their goals effectively, efficiently, and with satisfaction. These findings highlight the importance of providing clear, accurate user resources, such as documentation and tutorials, to support research goals. By operationalizing the full scope of the QIU standard, we offer a transferable method for identifying end-user challenges across domains. By treating QIU as a first-class citizen, developers can improve user satisfaction and develop user-centric, trustworthy software systems across software domains.

title: A Hybrid Model for Missile Detection Using YOLOv8 and Machine Learning Enhancement
authors: Zainab A. Mezher, Kadim K. Mohsen
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 2nd International Conference on Computing and Emerging Sciences
abstract: None

title: Improving the Circuit Realization of Grover's Quantum Search Algorithm by Replacing Hadamard with √X Gates
authors: Ali Al-Bayaty, Ali Al-Shuwaili, Abbas AlZubaidi, Marek Perkowski
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 2nd International Conference on Computing and Emerging Sciences
abstract: None

title: AI-Augmented Technical Debt Management in Legacy Software Systems
authors: Sapan Pandya
abstract: Legacy software systems continue to support mission-critical enterprise operations, even as technical debt accumulates over time. As systems evolve, this debt often manifests as rising code complexity, architectural drift, and gaps in documentation that slow down change. In this work, an AI-augmented toolkit is introduced that embeds machine-learning analysis and large-language-model summarization directly into CI workflows. The solution combines three components: an AI-enhanced static analysis trigger, an intelligent debt tagging matrix, and an AI-assisted refactor log sheet. To assess its effectiveness, the toolkit was applied in a six-month pilot using anonymized software systems representative of enterprise environments and was evaluated against a static-analysis-only baseline. Across the pilot period, error rates per release were reduced by 60%, while the maintainability index improved by 18% and review-cycle time decreased by 18%. These results suggest that integrating AI-assisted technical-debt governance into CI pipelines can improve maintenance outcomes in long lived systems without forcing disruptive rewrites.

title: Quantifying Cross-System Curriculum Alignment: A Reproducible and Interpretable Method
authors: Zonglin Han, Kristian A. Stevens
abstract: Cross-system curriculum comparison remains a persistent challenge in higher education. Differences in how programs such as A-level, AP, IB, and university courses define and assess learning outcomes make it difficult to evaluate equivalence or design coherent learning progressions. This paper presents a reproducible and interpretable method for quantifying curriculum alignment across educational systems. The method models course modules and learning outcomes as structured data, computes similarity using semantic and difficulty attributes, and outputs quantitative metrics such as coverage, alignment accuracy, and gap ratio. A lightweight Python implementation demonstrates how this approach produces alignment matrices and visual summaries that reveal strengths and gaps between curricula. Experiments using A-level and university mathematics datasets verify that the method can detect misalignments and provide transparent evidence for cross-system comparability. The results show that quantitative alignment analysis can support evidence-based curriculum evaluation and reproducible educational data research.

title: Using Higher-Order Spectral Growth in Pant Pocketworn Accelerometers for Cough-Detection Apps
authors: Ryan Connelly, Rushi Vyas
abstract: Early detection of respiratory distress marked by coughing associated with somewhat common viruses such as COVID, SARS and Influenza have become important for managing public health safely. With most adults carrying smartphones, there are now methods to assist with this detection using methods such as contact-tracing and manual tracking. Enabling diagnostic features to detect coughing using existing apps and tools can be useful in tracking diseases in larger populations. However, detecting lower intensity coughing, which is a common symptom in Influenza and COVID, is harder to do in the presence of human activity, such as walking. In this work, we study power spectrum density (PSD) features of tri-axial accelerometer signals measured from the outer thigh pocket, where smartphones are commonly worn. In particular, we analyzed the spectral growth generated around and above the 12 Hz range due to the very low body surface movements around the thigh pockets during coughs and other activity such as standing up or sitting down to identify distinct cough-related spectrum features. A preliminary study based on test measurements from 4 test subjects of different sizes and genders shows acceleration above 12 Hz as having the most potential to discern mild or intense coughs with subjects having minimal movement. Tri-axial acceleration measured at the outer thigh for 4 sitting test subjects of varying ages shows this. The most visible measurements occurred along the front to back (Y-axis) and up-down (Z-axis) directions with respect to the test subject. Measurements across all test subjects show the acceleration power increase from close to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 2}$</tex> to 20G<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>/Hz in the Y-direction from close to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 0}$</tex> to 100G<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>/Hz in the Zdirection.

title: A Dual-Mode Receiver Combining APD and SPAD Operation for Direct ToF LiDAR
authors: Yukang Xie, Ziyun Yang, Shaolin Liao, Xianbo Li
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: This paper presents a dual-mode direct time-offlight (ToF) LiDAR receiver combining linear avalanche photodiode (APD) and single-photon avalanche diode (SPAD) operation. In the APD mode, an inverter-based transimpedance amplifier (TIA) is employed. Furthermore, a timing discrimination circuit is utilized to extract the timing information from the TIA output. In the SPAD mode, a hybrid quenching and active reset (HQAR) circuit is adopted to achieve fast quenching and reset. In particular, the HQAR circuit is reconfigured from the TIA, which reduces the chip area. Implemented in a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 8 0} \mathbf{~ n m}$</tex> CMOS process, the receiver in APD mode achieves a transimpedance gain of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$94.9 ~\text{dB} \Omega$</tex>, a −3dB bandwidth of 527 MHz, and an input-referred noise (IRN) of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.13 \mu ~\mathrm{A}_{\text{rms }}$</tex>. For an input photocurrent swing varying from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \mu \mathbf{A}_{\mathbf{p p}}$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$20 \mu \mathbf{A}_{\mathbf{p p}}$</tex>, the walk error of the timing discrimination circuit is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{6 7 4 ~ p s}$</tex>. In the SPAD mode, the HQAR circuit achieves a quenching time of 1.9 ns and a reset time of 0.37 ns, consequently yielding a dead time of 2.27 ns.

title: Unveiling the Secrets of Successful Game Design
authors: Dra. Lorena Martínez Elizalde, Carlos Astengo Noguez, Lic. Daniel López Salas
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: The MDA (Mechanics-Dynamics-Aesthetics) framework is a well-known but often misunderstood methodology in game development. Independent game developers frequently encounter challenges in game design due to unclear understanding of core mechanics and dynamics, and often neglect the emotional journey and aesthetic design of the game. This article introduces a successful methodology that has been taught over the past two years. By providing a structured approach to the MDA framework, we aim to address common pitfalls in indie game development and enhance the overall design process. The methodology emphasizes the importance of clearly defining game mechanics, dynamics, and aesthetics, and analyse how well-designed video games engage players through thoughtful and immersive experiences. By presenting detailed practical examples, this article provides valuable insights and strategies for indie developers. These insights will help refine their game design practices and create more compelling and cohesive gaming experiences.

title: Beyond the Server: Permissioned Blockchain Coordination for Robust Federated Learning in Smart Systems
authors: Promita Chakraborty, Priyanka Chakraborty, Debapriya Sengupta, Sayani Konar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: Federated Learning (FL) provides a model training to protect privacy by sharing data between different parties. Traditional FL is based on central integration and introduces single points of failure (SPOFs) and vulnerabilities in hostile environments. Blockchain-based FL (BFL) provides decentralisation, but existing solutions often suffer from high latency due to chain computations or do not handle non-identifiable and identifiable data (non-IDs). To address these limitations, we propose the approved hybrid framework for learning coordination based on blockchain PH-BFL-Coord. Adaptive mechanisms are important to introduce reputation-based dynamic robustness (RIDR) mechanisms against malicious attacks and benign data heterogeneity. Experimental validations under 30% Byzantine attack scenarios showed that PH-BFL-Coord detected anomalies at 92.4% accuracy, representing 18.7% more convergence stability than FedAvg, and confirms the framework's ability to provide critical IoT and cyberphysical infrastructures that are reliable, scalable, and coordinated.

title: Comparative Study on Aqueous Solubility Prediction of Organic Molecules Based on Multi-Feature Fusion and Machine Learning
authors: Jiarui Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Aqueous solubility of organic molecules is a key physicochemical property that underpins pharmaceutical development, environmental chemistry, and materials science. Traditional methods such as experimental measurements and quantum chemical calculations are accurate but time-consuming and computationally costly. Existing machine learning (ML) approaches typically rely on single-feature inputs, resulting in limited feature representation and interpretability. To address these issues, this study proposes a high-precision and interpretable solubility prediction model based on multi-feature fusion and tree-based ensemble learning. The framework integrates 2048-bit Morgan molecular fingerprints with six 2D physicochemical descriptors (e.g., LogP, molecular weight, and topological polar surface area [TPSA]), combining topological and physicochemical information. Among three ensemble ML models evaluated-LightGBM-Base, XGBoost-Base, and XGBoost-Improved-the LightGBM-Base model achieved the best performance on the Delaney ESOL dataset, with an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 9 0 3 8}$</tex>, mean absolute error (MAE) of 0.4759, and root mean squared error (RMSE) of 0.6623. SHAP (Shapley Additive Explanations) analysis identified LogP, molecular weight, and TPSA as the most influential features, revealing that moderate TPSA values (1-2) maximize aqueous solubility. The multi-feature fusion strategy improved <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> by 2.7 % and reduced MAE by 9.7 % compared with single-feature models. Requiring only SMILES strings as input, the proposed model delivers predictions within one second, offering an efficient and interpretable framework for high-throughput molecular design.

title: Topo-RAG: A Visual Topology Reconstruction and Constraint-Guided Reasoning RAG Framework for Pollutant Discharge Rights Determination
authors: Longlong Liu, Ying Yuan, Wei Liu, Fuliang Deng
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Accurate pollutant discharge rights determination is critical for environmental capacity control, yet the multi-source heterogeneity of Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) reports poses significant challenges for automation. Traditional Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) methods often struggle with spatial semantic loss in cross-page tables and are prone to numerical hallucinations in tasks involving complex constraints. To address these issues, this paper proposes Topo-RAG, an intelligent determination framework that integrates visual topology reconstruction with constraint-guided reasoning. Specifically, the framework employs the DeepDoc model to perform deep document understanding, reconstructing the 2D topological structure via a coordinate-guided serialization strategy to effectively fuse spatial cues into textual embedding. Subsequently, a Chain-of-Thought (CoT) strategy is introduced to decompose the determination task into explicit multi-step reasoning processes, serving as a semantic validator to enforce strict domain logic constraints and mitigate error propagation. Experiments on a real-world industrial dataset covering six major sectors demonstrate that Topo-RAG achieves an average accuracy of 90%, outperforming the Standard RAG and Long-Context baselines by 40% and 28%, respectively. Notably, the framework reduces the generative error rate from 20% to 2%, effectively eliminating numerical hallucinations. These results validate the effectiveness and engineering potential of the proposed framework for automated environmental auditing.

title: Deep Learning-Driven Adaptive Network Intrusion Detection: a Hybrid Framework Integrating Dualdiscriminator Wgan and Hierarchical Multi-Head Attention
authors: Yunfeng Huang, Mulin Qiao, Canxin Zhao, Jiang Zhou, Yue Chang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: As network attacks evolve towards automation and high-level encryption, existing Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) face dual challenges: the scarcity of attack samples (Data Imbalance) and the difficulty in capturing long-term dependencies in encrypted traffic. To address these issues, this paper proposes a Deep Learning-Driven Adaptive Network Intrusion Detection Framework (HD-WGAN-HMA). First, we construct a DualDiscriminator Wasserstein GAN (D-WGAN-GP) with gradient penalty. It employs two separate discriminators to constrain statistical distributions and temporal patterns respectively. This mechanism generates high-fidelity few-shot attack samples, mathematically resolving the mode collapse problem. Second, we design a Hierarchical Multi-Head Attention (H-MHA) mechanism to extract global dependency features in parallel at both the bytelevel and packet-level. Specifically, to prevent data leakage and overfitting, we implement a strict flow-based chronological splitting strategy and IP masking during preprocessing. Experiments on the CIC-IDS2018 benchmark show that the model achieves an overall accuracy of 99.3 % and a macro-averaged <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{F 1}$</tex> score of 0.987. Compared with the state-of-the-art E-GraphSAGE (2024) and RoBERTa-IDS, our method reduces the False Positive Rate (FPR) to 0.28 % while maintaining a low inference latency of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.2 ~\text{ms} /$</tex> flow, verifying its robustness and real-time capability in complex network environments.

title: A Compact 4×4 MIMO Antenna Array Optimization Design Using Meander-Slot Patches with Ground-Plane Decoupling Structures
authors: Sonu Kumar, Durbadal Mandal, Rajib Kar
