title: Proposed Design of an Immersive Simulation: Virtual Reality and Mathematical Modeling
authors: Francisco Guantecura, Elisabeth Ramos-Rodríguez, Felipe Muñoz La Rivera
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Mathematical modeling is recognized as a cornerstone of contemporary mathematics education because it enables students to connect real-world phenomena with mathematical formulations and solutions. However, its implementation in school settings poses significant didactic and logistical challenges. In parallel, Virtual Reality (VR) has emerged as an educational technology capable of generating immersive, contextualized experiences, although its application to mathematical modeling processes remains nascent. This article presents the technical and pedagogical aspects of designing an immersive educational simulation in VR, with the goal of exploring how such an environment influences the mathematical modeling process in secondary education students. The article examines its pedagogical and technical foundations and its potential to enrich the learning of mathematical modeling through immersive environments. The experience, titled “Painting a Room,” poses a contextualized task in which students must manage a budget to paint a room. The design aligns with the phases of a mathematical modeling cycle and is structured according to the stages of the Design Science Research Methodology (DSRM). The simulation is currently under development. As results, we present characteristic features of the simulation's design and main flow, and how these relate to a mathematical modeling process; moreover, we operationalize the phases of the modeling process within this immersive experience. We highlight the relevance of the technology for enabling the validation of students' mathematical models.

title:
Prediction and Optimization of S
                    <sub>11</sub>
                    (Reflection Coefficient) in Log-Periodic Toothed Antenna on Flexible Kapton Substrate Using Machine Learning
authors: Md Abdur Rashid, Anandi Dutta, Yihong Maggie Chen
abstract: Designing ultra-wideband log-periodic toothed antennas (LPTAs) on flexible substrates usually relies on fullwave electromagnetic simulations, which are accurate but computationally expensive for large design spaces and iterative optimization. Machine learning (ML) offers a practical way to speed up this process by replacing many of these simulations with fast surrogate models. This paper applies Random Forest and Gradient Boosting regressors to predict and optimize the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$S_{11}$</tex> reflection coefficient of LPTAs on flexible Kapton substrates. A large HFSS-generated dataset is used, in which element spacing, scaling factors, and operating frequency serve as input features and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$S_{11}$</tex> is the target. The Random Forest model achieves a test <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}$</tex> of 0.996 and a test mean squared error of 0.185, clearly outperforming Gradient Boosting. Comparison of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$S_{11}$</tex> versus frequency shows that the Random Forest predictions closely follow the HFSS curves for a representative LPTA design, indicating that the ML model can reproduce the reflection response very effectively across the operating band. Feature-importance analysis identifies frequency as the dominant factor, with the scaling parameters Tau <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\tau)$</tex> and Sigma <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\sigma)$</tex> also having a strong influence on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$S_{11}$</tex>, while residual analysis confirms that the prediction errors are small and largely unbiased. Overall, the results show that ML can significantly reduce the number of required electromagnetic simulations while maintaining high accuracy, helping to accelerate the design workflow for nextgeneration flexible and ultra-wideband antenna systems.

title: Research on Virtual Collaboration-Based Line Construction Simulation Drill Technology and Training Strategy
authors: Houyuan Li, Congying Wu, Jianjun Fang, Zhihong Xiao, Peili Yan
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Implementing an interactive, virtual-real integrated training model is an urgent task for the technical skill training of power transmission and transformation construction. Aiming at the limitations of current simulation training platforms - such as low pre-training theoretical assessment pass rate (<70%), poor interaction (average satisfaction 3.2/5), inflexible scene adjustment (72 minutes per 10 km segment), lack of automatic evaluation, and high training cost - this paper proposes a framework of virtual collaboration-based line construction simulation drill technology and training strategy. It conducts research on key technologies including 3D terrain data fusion, generative 3D scene technology, line construction algorithm and simulation training technology, as well as training strategy optimization. Based on simulation experiments and pilot applications conducted at three training bases of State Grid Corporation of China, the framework reduces terrain elevation error to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\leqslant 0.5$</tex> meters, shortens scene generation time by 75%, improves trainee operation pass rate by 54%, and shortens training cycle by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 0 \%}$</tex>. This study provides an innovative solution for efficient talent training, improving on-site safe and high-quality construction capabilities.

title: From Dataset to Deployment: A Full-Stack Autoencoder-Based Anomaly Detection Pipeline for Gas Turbine BLDC Fuel Pumps
authors: Viet Linh Nguyen, Duy Lanh Chu, van Huy Nguyen
abstract: Reliable health monitoring of fuel pumps is essential for maintaining the stability and safety of gas turbine engines. Brushless DC (BLDC) fuel pumps operate under tightly coupled electrical–mechanical–hydraulic dynamics, where fault progression is often subtle and real fault data are scarce. This article presents a full-stack anomaly detection pipeline spanning dataset construction, feature engineering, model development, evaluation, and embedded deployment, built on an unsupervised autoencoder trained exclusively on normal operating data. A real-world dataset was collected from BLDC fuel pumps integrated into gas turbine testbeds over multiple engines and years; however, the available corpus remains modest, comprising 18 log files and nearly three hours of cumulative operation. A 48-dimensional handcrafted feature set was designed to capture statistical, energetic, derivative, and autocorrelation characteristics of pump behavior. The autoencoder learns a compact representation of healthy operation, while anomalies are identified through reconstruction error using a fixed threshold and a persistence-based decision rule. In offline analysis, the proposed autoencoder achieves competitive discrimination relative to strong unsupervised baselines, while its primary practical advantage lies in supporting a deterministic end-to-end TinyML deployment pipeline rather than maximizing offline discriminative performance alone. Real-time on-device experiments on an STM32 platform demonstrate successful detection for the evaluated dry-run and aeration scenarios, while no alarms are produced under normal steady and transient tests after persistence filtering. Under the current feature set and calibration, the evaluated partial-blockage and gradual-restriction scenarios did not produce persistence-triggered alarms, indicating incomplete fault-mode coverage and motivating future work on broader fault characterization and adaptive calibration. These results support the practical feasibility of real-time, on-device anomaly detection for gas-turbine fuel-pump monitoring in a resource-constrained TinyML setting.

title: MHDash: An Online Platform for Benchmarking Mental Health–Aware AI Assistants
authors: Yihe Zhang, Finn Mohawk, Kaiying Han, Vijay Srinivas Tida, Manyu Li, Xiali Hei
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied in mental health support systems, where reliable recognition of high-risk states such as suicidal ideation and self-harm is safety-critical. However, existing evaluations primarily rely on aggregate performance metrics, which often obscure risk-specific failure modes and provide limited insight into model behavior in realistic, multi-turn interactions. We present MHDash, an opensource platform designed to support the development, evaluation, and auditing of AI systems for mental health applications. MHDash integrates data collection, structured annotation, multiturn dialogue generation, and baseline evaluation into a unified pipeline. The platform supports annotations across multiple dimensions, including Concern Type, Risk Level, and Dialogue Intent, enabling fine-grained and risk-aware analysis. Our results reveal several key findings: (i) simple baselines and advanced LLM APIs exhibit comparable overall accuracy yet diverge significantly on high-risk cases; (ii) some LLMs maintain consistent ordinal severity ranking while failing absolute risk classification, whereas others achieve reasonable aggregate scores but suffer from high false negative rates on severe categories; and (iii) performance gaps are amplified in multi-turn dialogues, where risk signals emerge gradually. These observations demonstrate that conventional benchmarks are insufficient for safety-critical mental health settings. By releasing MHDash as an open platform, we aim to promote reproducible research, transparent evaluation, and safety-aligned development of AI systems for mental health support.

title: Attack Detection in Internet of Medical Things Using Machine Learning
authors: Anand Choubey
abstract: The growing proliferation of Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) devices offers the potential for significantly improved patient care, but also creates new risks for cyberattacks that can compromise the privacy and even safety of patients. To prevent such attacks from occurring, intrusion detection systems can be utilized to detect any attempted attacks and take action accordingly. In this paper, we explore the use of machine learning models, which offer greater adaptability and ease of deployment compared to traditional approaches, for intrusion detection in the context of Bluetooth, which, despite its widespread use in IoMT devices, remains largely unexplored in terms of intrusion detection compared to other protocol stacks. Our results indicate that the four models tested are all promising candidates for securing IoMT systems, with logistic regression achieving a precision of 0.9875, a recall of 1, an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$F_{1}$</tex> score of 0.9934, and an AUC score of 0.999.

title: Flash Snap: An Efficient Snapshot Loading Architecture for Rapid FaaS Startup
authors: Wanfu Xiong, RuiBo Wang, Wenzhe Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Function-as-a-Service (FaaS) has emerged as a dominant serverless paradigm, offering fine-grained elasticity for bursty workloads such as IoT event processing and machine learning inference. While containers are the common deployment unit, their shared-kernel architecture often lacks stringent isolation, spurring the adoption of MicroVMs that provide hardwareenforced isolation. However, MicroVMs suffer from significant cold-start latency due to guest OS initialization overhead. Snapshot-based restoration is a promising mitigation, but existing solutions are hindered by inefficient working-set loading, coarsegrained page mapping management, failing to meet the demands of latency-sensitive applications. To address these challenges, this paper presents FlashSnap, a high-performance snapshot loading framework optimized for MicroVM restoration in FaaS environments. FlashSnap introduces three core components: a Parallel Queue-Aware Snapshot Loading (PQSL) engine that saturates NVMe bandwidth via CPU-NVMe affinity, a Fine-grained Page Mapping Management (FPMM) mechanism leveraging kprobes for precise page fault interception and proactive pre-mapping, and Adaptive Preprocessing Strategies (APS) that select optimal restoration schemes across diverse workloads. Evaluation results show that FlashSnap outperforms existing solutions (Vanilla Firecracker, REAP, FaaSnap) across synthetic and real-world workloads, reducing major page faults by up to 94.6% and total cold-start overhead by up to 42.2% compared to state-of-theart alternatives, enabling low-latency, strongly isolated serverless execution.

title: Hierarchical Control and Power Converter Topology for an Isolated DC Microgrid for EV Charging
authors: Diego Roman, Jose Aguilar, Enrique Espina
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Isolating the pulsed dynamics of electric vehicle charging from the main grid is a major challenge in the design of future energy systems. This work proposes an isolated DC microgrid architecture capable of autonomous operation over a complete daily cycle. The generation subsystem is based on a Proton Exchange Membrane fuel cell supported by both first-life and second-life battery energy storage systems. A hierarchical control strategy is implemented, combining primary, secondary, and tertiary levels to ensure stable dynamics, energy balancing, and optimal scheduling. The proposed solution integrates the design and sizing of DC-DC power converters, control loops with specified dynamic performance, and adaptive droop mechanisms for energy sharing. Key technical characteristics of the infrastructure are detailed, and its effectiveness is validated through simulation results. This architecture establishes a foundation for further research on intelligent, operator-assisted, and resilient EV charging infrastructures.

title: Semantic Search of AutoCAD Drawing Repositories Via Cost-Effective Multi-AI Specification Extraction
authors: Catherine Thuo, Steven C. Puckett
abstract: Engineering organizations increasingly rely on large repositories of AutoCAD drawings, yet locating relevant drawings remains difficult due to inconsistent or missing metadata. This paper presents a multi-AI pipeline that converts DWG, DXF, and PDF drawing formats into structured specifications using redundant artificial intelligence models to improve accuracy and reliability. Extracted technical data-including dimensions, materials, and annotations-is normalized into JSON and combined with natural-language descriptions. Processed drawings are stored in a vector database to enable semantic retrieval by engineering constraints rather than filenames or tags. In evaluation, the system achieved a 994 ms average query latency, with Precision@10 of 0.459, Recall of 0.593, and Mean Reciprocal Rank of 0.792 across 29 diverse engineering queries. Cost modeling indicates an estimated annual operating cost of approximately $ 480, representing a 97 % cost reduction compared to commercial CAD management systems and demonstrating a scalable, cost-effective alternative to traditional document management.

title: Hybrid LLM Architectures for On-Device and Cloud-Based Intelligence in Regulated Industries
authors: Sivarama Krishna Akhil Koduri
abstract: The deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs) in regulated sectors such as healthcare, finance, and legal services is currently hindered by a rigid dichotomy: the privacy risks of centralized cloud APIs (e.g., Azure OpenAI, AWS Bedrock) versus the computational limitations of local devices. This paper proposes a Hybrid LLM Architecture that bridges this gap by utilizing commoditized on-device runtimes like Ollama on consumer silicon (Apple M-Series, NVIDIA GeForce) as a “sovereign gatekeeper” for hyperscale cloud intelligence. We analyze the hardware physics of Unified Memory Architectures (UMA) versus discrete GPU VRAM, formally define privacy leakage using Fisher Information Loss (FIL), and present a mathematical framework for dynamic workload offloading. Benchmarks suggest that local Llama 3 70B models may exhibit hallucination rates competitive with GPT-4 (e.g., 4.1 % vs. 9.6 % in specific tasks), supporting the potential of “sovereign reasoning” for sensitive data processing.

title: Control System for the Formation of a Balanced Hybrid Single/Three-Phase Microgrid From Three Isolated Single-Phase Microgrids
authors: Bárbara Contreras Bustamante, Patricio A. Mendoza-Araya
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Access to electricity in isolated rural areas is challenging due to geographical, technical, and/or economic constraints. The use of distributed generation and the implementation of one or more single-phase microgrids can provide a solution to this problem, since residential loads are mostly single-phase. However, it would not be possible to connect a three-phase load if power is required. Therefore, a hybrid single/three-phase microgrid topology is proposed, consisting of three single-phase microgrids connected to a common three-phase busbar. Based on hierarchical control of the microgrid, with primary droop control and decentralized secondary control, the relationship between a power step in one phase and its angular difference with respect to another phase is calculated. A decentralized phase angle shift PI control system is proposed to regulate phase shift angles through power transfer between phases. Thus, a balanced threephase system is formed in a hybrid single/three-phase microgrid, allowing the connection of three-phase loads. Simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed control system, subject to the physical limitations of power generation and transfer.

title: Semantic Resonance: Distributed Backdoor Attacks via Semantic Trigger Families in Federated NLP
authors: Jian Wang, Hong Shen
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Federated learning (FL) is widely used in privacysensitive natural language processing applications, but its distributed nature also introduces serious security vulnerabilities, particularly backdoor attacks. Existing federated NLP backdoor methods typically rely on fixed token triggers, which suffer from gradient discordance under severe non-IID data and are easily exposed by robust aggregation defenses. In this paper, we propose Semantic Resonance, a novel federated NLP backdoor attack framework that integrates distributed attack strategies with semantic-level triggers. By instantiating an abstract semantic concept as a family of natural and diverse trigger variants and distributing them across malicious clients, the proposed method induces consistent gradient directions in a shared semantic subspace during federated aggregation, enabling robust backdoor implantation in the global model. Experiments on the IMDB dataset show that our method achieves a 92.8% attack success rate on BERT-base with only a 0.8% drop in clean accuracy and a malicious update detection rate of 12.5%, significantly outperforming four baselines. Under extreme non-IID settings, the attack success rate remains at 88.6%, and clear advantages are preserved under robust aggregation defenses such as Krum.

title: GenAITD Score: A Quantitative Framework for Measuring Enterprise Technical Debt and Generative AI Readiness
authors: Pankaj Kumar Jain, Jahnavi Thirumalasetty
abstract: The rapid adoption of Generative Artificial Intelligence (Gen-AI) in enterprise environments has outpaced the readiness of existing technical and governance infrastructures, exposing latent forms of technical debt across data pipelines, legacy workflows, and machine learning operations (MLOps) practices. While organizations are under increasing pressure to operationalize Gen-AI capabilities, existing maturity models lack the granularity required to identify the specific architectural and operational constraints that hinder reliable deployment. This paper introduces the Gen-AI Technical Debt (GenAITD) Score, a quantitative framework designed to measure enterprise readiness and Gen-AI-specific technical debt. The framework constructs a composite index on a normalized <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0-500$</tex> scale and evaluates enterprise readiness across five weighted dimensions: Data Debt & Governance, Process & Automation, AI Platform Architecture, Organizational Readiness, and Risk & Compliance. A total of 45 objectively defined Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) are aggregated to produce both a numerical score and a corresponding letter-grade classification (A+ to D), enabling a standardized, audit-able mechanism for stakeholders to benchmark maturity.

title: Temperature Multi-Step Prediction Algorithm for Coke Dry Quenching Waste Heat Power Generation System Based on LSTM
authors: Peixin Li, Rentao Zhao
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: This paper presents a multi-step prediction algorithm based on Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) neural networks, aimed at addressing the temperature regulation lag problem caused by system response delays in the operation of the coke dry quenching (CDQ) waste heat power generation system. By predicting the quenched coke temperature and the boiler inlet circulating gas temperature, the algorithm provides strong support for the safe operation of the system and the improvement of power generation efficiency. First, a gray relational analysis (GRA) method is used to analyze the correlation between all field-collected variables and the target variables. The feature variables for the LSTM model are then selected based on the system mechanism. Subsequently, a single-step multi-output LSTM model is constructed, and on this basis, a recursive multi-step prediction model is established.

title: Graph Spectra of Technical Documentation
authors: Rocker D'Antonio
abstract: Technical documentation serves as a critical interface between the design of technological systems and the users who implement, maintain, and operate them. As documentation is increasingly consumed not only by humans but also by automated tools, understanding its structural properties has become more important. While documentation corpora encode rich structural information through explicit cross-references, this structure is rarely examined quantitatively at the level of global link organization. In this work, we model technical documentation corpora as page-level hyperlink graphs derived from explicit inline links and analyze their structure using spectral graph methods. Directed graphs are constructed from multiple real-world documentation sources using a standardized, quality-assured extraction pipeline, and Laplacian spectral properties are computed for both the full graphs and their largest weakly connected components. The resulting measurements reveal substantial variation in graph size, connectivity, and fragmentation across corpora constructed under identical rules, illustrating observable differences in explicit cross-referencing structure.

title: The Variational Quantum Classifier from Qiskit Machine Learning Applied to the Classification of Pulsar Stars
authors: Anna B. M. Souza, Clebson Cruz, Marcelo A. Moret, Otto M. Pires
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Quantum Machine Learning is a new computational tool that combines the quantum properties from quantum computing with the pattern recognition from machine learning. In this paper, we apply the Variational Quantum Classifier algorithm to the problem of pulsar classification of candidates from the High Time Resolution Universe 2 dataset. We use Qiskit Machine Learning circuits to compare the performance of the model using different feature selection methods, various number of features and training data size. Comparisons on the model from changing the data encoding and ansatz options are also reported.

title: Signal-to-Noise Ratio Fundamental Limits for Cascade Networked Control Systems
authors: Alejandro J. Rojas, Héctor Ramírez
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: In this work we study the networked control system (NCS) configuration of a cascade control over additive white noise (AWN) channel models. We study first the case in which a single AWN channel model is located over the control path, and characterize its infimal channel input signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). For an inner plant model with one unstable pole <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\rho$</tex>, the infimal SNR recovers the value of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\rho^{2}-1$</tex>. We then study the presence of two AWN channel models over each of the two feedback paths, the one for the inner faster closed loop, and the one for the outer slower closed loop. The effect of the presence of the two AWN channels is expressed in terms of the channel input SNR of both channels. For an inner plant model with one unstable pole <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\rho$</tex>, the AWN channel infimal SNR over the inner feedback path is slightly bigger than the previous case, not considering the extra cost imposed by the AWN channel infimal SNR over the outer feedback path. The SNR fundamental limit study for the cascade NCS can result in greater operational efficiency, and reduced costs by minimizing energy consumption at each actuator signal or sensor measurements transmission.

title: Counting Characters: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Evaluating Character-Level Understanding in Large Language Models
authors: Yuyang Zeng, Yuqi Yi
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit systematic failures in character-level tasks despite strong performance on complex reasoning. The “strawberry problem” (incorrectly counting the letter ‘ <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$r$</tex> ’ in “strawberry”) exemplifies this limitation. We present a 5,000 -sample benchmark covering five task types to evaluate character counting as a fundamental proxy for character-level understanding. We assess eight open-weight 79-B parameter models deployed locally on a single NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU across four prompting strategies. Our findings reveal: (1) word length is the primary difficulty factor (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$r=-0.521, p<0.001$</tex>), with token count showing a small independent effect after controlling for word length (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$r_{\text{partial}}=-0.085, p<0.001$</tex>), (2) chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting yields the largest average improvement (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$+13.7 {\%}$</tex>), with Qwen2.5 gaining +36.8%, while character decomposition shows model-dependent effects, and (3) qualitative analysis of reasoning traces reveals that structured character enumeration is the key mechanism behind CoT effectiveness. These results provide a quantitative baseline for future character-level research.

title: A Novel Metric Based on Linear Probes to Analyze Learning Progression in Deep Neural Networks
authors: José Luis Vázquez Noguera, Carlos U. Valdez, Miguel García-Torres, Sebastian Alberto Grillo
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Deep neural networks achieve remarkable results but remain difficult to interpret due to their black–box nature. Understanding the learning progression within these models is critical for improving their design and reliability. Linear probes–simple classifiers trained on fixed intermediate representations–offer a way to assess feature separability without retraining. We propose a new metric based on multiple support vector machines to measure linear separability more realistically. Our methodology tracks the evolution of separability across layers and training epochs, providing insights into overfitting and generalization. Experiments on three architectures and seven datasets demonstrate the utility of this approach for analyzing deep network training dynamics.

title: Intelligent Information Systems for Precision Livestock Farming in Dairy and Poultry
authors: Mateus de Castro Duarte Cardoso, Julio Cesar Schendroski, Andre Sirikaku Miyashiro, Iran José Oliveira da Silva, Thiago Luís Lopes Siqueira
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Creating a world free of hunger requires decreasing food insecurity by means of developing food production systems that improve both sustainability and yield. However, animal diseases threaten production, economy, livelihood and health. Therefore, interdisciplinary research and development involving animal sciences and information technology play a key role to prevent diseases and mitigate their effects. The development of intelligent information systems for these purposes demands composing diversified teams, studying and assessing existing solutions, creating prototypes and evaluating their feasibility, and finally searching for collaborations with non-academic stakeholders to transfer technology and increase the societal impact. In this paper, we focus on precision livestock farming in dairy and poultry. First, we reevaluate solutions for diagnosing cow mastitis. Second, we describe a prototype for computer vision that detects heifers in images. Third, we detail a prototype for computer vision that detects objects inside a poultry barn. Fourth, we describe an application that maps and searches for farms, since farmers are potential partners. We also discuss our results considering: interdisciplinarity, data as the center of intelligent information systems, and technological debt and divide in rural areas.

title: DRL With Dual-Actor and Risk-Critic Coupling for Safe Navigation of USVs: Sim-to-Field Validation
authors: Mengmeng Lou, Xiaofei Yang, Zhengrong Xiang
abstract: Autonomous collision avoidance is essential for unmanned surface vehicles (USVs) in Maritime Internet of Things (MIoT) systems. Existing deep reinforcement learning (DRL) methods often suffer from poor exploration-exploitation balance and weak safety integration, which may lead to local optima and unsafe actions. To address these issues, a risk-aware role-differentiated dual-actor deep deterministic policy gradient framework (RADA-DDPG) is proposed. Abandoning homogeneous structures, a heterogeneous dual-actor design mitigates local optima by assigning distinct exploration and exploitation roles, dynamically balanced via a Q-value-based switching strategy. Additionally, an embedded risk-critic network directly shapes actions during end-to-end policy optimization, rather than serving merely as an external constraint. Extensive simulations demonstrate that RADA-DDPG outperforms baselines in learning efficiency, safety, and robustness. Furthermore, real-world field experiments with dynamic obstacles validate its feasibility.

title: Fluid-Antenna-aided AAV Secure Communications in Eavesdropper Uncertain Location
authors: Yingjie Wu, Junshan Luo, Weiyu Chen, Shilian Wang, Fanggang Wang, Haiyang Ding
abstract: For autonomous aerial vehicle (AAV) secure communications, traditional designs based on fixed position antenna (FPA) lack sufficient spatial degrees of freedom (DoF), which leaves the line-of-sight-dominated AAV links vulnerable to eavesdropping. To overcome this problem, this paper proposes a framework that effectively incorporates the fluid antenna (FA) and the artificial noise (AN) techniques. Specifically, the minimum secrecy rate (MSR) among multiple eavesdroppers is maximized by jointly optimizing AAV deployment, signal precoder, AN precoder, and FA positions. In particular, the worst-case MSR is considered by taking the channel uncertainties due to the uncertainty about eavesdropping locations into account. To tackle the highly coupled optimization variables and the channel uncertainties in the formulated problem, an efficient and robust algorithm is proposed. Particularly, the uncertain regions of eavesdroppers, whose shapes can be arbitrary, are disposed by constructing convex hull. In addition, two movement modes of FAs are considered, namely, free movement mode and zonal movement mode, for which different optimization techniques are applied, respectively. Also, both single-user and multi-user scenarios are considered. Numerical results show that, the proposed FA schemes boost security by exploiting additional spatial DoF rather than transmit power, while AN provides remarkable gains under high transmit power. The synergy between FA and AN results in a secure advantage that exceeds the sum of their individual contributions, achieving a balance between security and reliability under limited resources.

title: 2-T Embedded Flash Memory using Standard 28 nm Logic CMOS Technology
authors: Jonghyun Ko, Jeseung Jeong, Jiseong Im, Kyung Min Lee, Kangwook Choi, Gyuweon Jung, Jong-Ho Lee
abstract: A 2-transistor (2-T) embedded flash (eflash) memory fully implemented using a 28 nm logic CMOS technology (Samsung 28 nm LPP) is proposed. The proposed 2-T eflash requires no additional process steps. Compared with prior eflash memory employing three or more transistors and requiring program/erase voltages above 6.8 V, the proposed 2-T eflash reduces the device count and lowers the operating voltage to 4.5 V, substantially simplifying the peripheral circuit configuration. Owing to the low-voltage tunneling-based operation, the eflash exhibits high endurance exceeding 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">7</sup> cycles and retention characteristics are maintained for up to 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sup> s at an elevated temperature of 80°C. While preserving high-performance retention, the proposed eflash achieves endurance exceeding 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">7</sup> cycles, showing nearly a two orders of magnitude improvement, compared to conventional flash memory endurance. In addition, selective program and erase operations are demonstrated, enabling precise threshold-voltage tuning in a memory array. These results indicate that the proposed 2-T eflash is a promising candidate for scalable compute-in-memory applications.

title: Estimation of NaCl Concentration in Aqueous Solution Using Partial Least Square Regression and Raman Spectroscopy
authors: Edgardo Pérez Chambi, Daniel Sbárbaro Chambi, Rodrigo Fuentes, Fernando Castillo
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: The objective of this study is to quantify the concentration of NaCl in aqueous solutions using Raman spectroscopy and the Partial Least Square (PLS) model. Five NaCl solutions with concentrations of 1-5 g/100 mL were prepared at different temperatures (10-50 °C), and Raman spectra were recorded. Spectral analysis was performed using a multi-Gaussian fit of the O-H band, generating nine physical descriptors per spectrum. Two PLS approaches were compared: one with the full spectrum and one using only the physically informed descriptors. The results obtained show that the model with physical descriptors outperforms the model based on the full spectrum, with an R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> of 0.9939 and a significantly l o wer R M SECV. The proposed methodology provides an accurate and robust tool for the quantification o f N a Cl i n a q ueous solutions.

title: Analysis of Characteristics of Incoming Students to Informatics Programs at the Facultad PolitéCnica of the Universidad Nacional De AsuncióN
authors: Ellen L. Mendez Xavier, Rita Cantero, Christian von Lücken
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Choosing a university degree is always a great challenge for young people. In these times, Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics (STEM) fields are playing an important role as fundamental pillars for economic and social progress globally, and Paraguay is no exception. This paper presents an analysis of the characteristics in different dimensions of incoming students in the Computer Engineering and Computer Science programs at the Facultad Politécnica of the Universidad Nacional de Asunción in 2024. To carry out this study, a survey was conducted, revealing that the majority of incoming students are male, that they enter the university between the ages of 18 and 20 after completing a high school diploma generally related to the field of computer science, that most come from the capital city and nearby cities, and that their areas of interest focus on software development, cybersecurity, artificial intelligence, and machine learning. This study presents the first systematic characterization of students entering computer science programs at FPUNA, providing a baseline for future research and empirical information on academic, socioeconomic, and motivational variables of these students, contributing to documenting the situation in Paraguay and to the literature on the characteristics of students who choose a career in computer science.

title: Facilitating Open Data Discovery and Exploration: A Case Study on Uruguay's Open Data Catalog
authors: Nicolás Buero, Raúl Maglione, Agustina Simoncelli, Lorena Etcheverry, Adriana Marotta
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper examines the challenges of data discovery and exploration within open data repositories, with a focus on the Open Data Catalog of Uruguay as a case study. This catalog, which is built on the CKAN platform, allows users to search using filters or keywords. However, it lacks features for discovering related datasets or identifying datasets relevant to specific questions. To address these limitations, our approach combines advanced search methods, navigation, and semantic annotation. We utilize technologies such as D3L, Aurum, TableMiner+, language models (LLMs), and RDF graphs, along with an automatic metadata enrichment system. Our findings indicate that inferring relationships between columns, leveraging semantic annotations, and executing natural language queries on a generated RDF graph significantly enhance data exploration and retrieval.

title: A Lightweight DMA Controller for Edge AI in RISC-V Based SoC
authors: Zhenyou Kang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: With the progressive integration of the Internet of Things (IoT) and artificial intelligence (AI), IoT edge devices are placing higher demands on real-time performance, energy efficiency, and data processing capabilities. Traditional CPU-based data transfer methods struggle to meet the transmission requirements of large-scale matrix data in edge AI tasks. To address this, this paper designs and implements a specialized Direct Memory Access (DMA) controller for edge AI SoCs. It supports the AXI bus protocol and features automatic address increment, multi-mode transmission, and byte alignment capabilities, effectively enhancing data access efficiency and system energy efficiency. This paper also develops a RISC-V CPU-based SoC platform to functionally verify the DMA controller and implements a prototype system on a Xilinx Virtex FPGA. The implementation result shows that the DMA Controller occupies 11.36% of the LUTs and 11.02% of the Registers, demonstrating that the DMA controller exhibits favorable lightweight characteristics in resource utilization, providing an efficient and low-cost data transfer solution for edge AI devices.

title: Agentic Manufacturing: Enabling Autonomous, AI-Powered Industrial Robotics
authors: Ismael Lopez-Juarez
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper traces the evolution of learning-based autonomy in manufacturing robotics, from early neural-network driven assembly learning and procedural control strategies, through multimodal perception and incremental knowledge acquisition, to the emergence of Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) for adaptive skill formation and Sim2Real deployment. Building on this historical and experimental trajectory, we analyze the ongoing transition toward hybrid DRL-LLM embodied reasoning for agentic manufacturing systems, particularly in industrial activities such as manipulation, welding, and assembly. The work integrates our prior research on neural-based assembly perception, DRL visual path following, procedural simulation, and robot experimentation, and extends it toward edge-based multimodal autonomy, Sim2Real robustness, and humanoidready agentic architectures for future factory environments.

title: Adaptive Machine Learning for Intrusion Detection in IoT Environments Under Concept Drift: A Comparative Study Using IoT-23
authors: Jorge Andrés González-Quintana, Juan Ignacio Iturbe-Araya, Helena Rifà-Pous
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: The performance of machine learning-based intrusion detection systems (IDS) in smart home environments deteriorates over time due to concept drift, where the statistical properties of network traffic evolve. This paper presents a comparative evaluation of three drift adaptation techniques (ADWIN, HDDMW, DWM) applied to four classical classifiers (Random Forest, Decision Tree, Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression) using the IoT-23 dataset. The results indicate that adaptation significantly improves performance in key metrics, particularly those robust to class imbalance like the Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) and balanced accuracy. Among the techniques, HDDMW yields the most stable results, while ADWIN is more efficient in computational terms. The Random Forest model combined with HDDMW achieves the highest average accuracy (99.55 %) and MCC (0.98), confirming the benefit of adaptive strategies in maintaining detection capabilities over time.

title: Classification of Streak Contamination in Hubble Space Telescope Images via ResNet-Inception Transfer Learning
authors: Murtuza Lemonwala, Kayla D. Taylor, Naqiya Rangwala
abstract: The burgeoning commercial space industry and the proliferation of satellite mega-constellations are introducing unprecedented challenges to astronomy research by adding obtrusive light and noise pollution to astronomical images. Manually inspecting images for evidence of satellite streaks has become too labor-intensive to be sustained without data-driven modeling and computational approaches. To address this challenge, we introduce a dual-backbone ResNet-Inception ensemble that fuses the edge-preserving representations of ResNet-50 with the multi-scale feature sensitivity of Inception-v3 through logit-level averaging. The experiments on a 5-fold split of a labeled Hubble Space Telescope (HST) image repository (114,607 images) used the first fold for model development and evaluation (1,600 training images and 400 validation). Results show that ResNet-50 alone performs near chance level (~ 0.50 accuracy), while Inception-v3 and the ensemble achieve precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy above 0.875 at the default threshold of 0.50. A threshold-tuned ensemble (0.32) achieves performance metrics above 0.90. Collectively, these contributions provide a practical solution for automated streak/no-streak classification in astronomical imaging.

title: Space It Education: a CubeSat Approach
authors: Daniel J. Freburg, Will Lewis, Charles Edamala, Michael Lembeck, Jacob Oakley
publish: 2025 IEEE 11th International Conference on Space Mission Challenges for Information Technology (SMC-IT)
abstract: CubeSats have served as an excellent educational tool in the aerospace field and have helped facilitate STEM learning activities across various educational institutions. CubeSats have also become a valuable vehicle for space-related science missions and have created their own Low Earth Orbit small satellite industry. As the space industry continues expanding, the demand for spacerelated IT skills increases. Yet, IT disciplines have not been heavily involved in teaching and research in this area. This paper describes the need for, and the value of, a Space IT educational CubeSat, which will be used to introduce and teach information technology, computer science, and cybersecurity aspects of space tech to IT students.

title: Proactive Handoff Mechanisms in 6G Mobile Networks: AI-Driven Innovations for Seamless Mobility
authors: Sambit S. Mondal, Biplab Kumar Mondal, Khushi Banerjee, Bikas Mondal, Sujata Kundu, Sudip Das, Anirban Kundu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: The transformation in mobility management by shifting from traditional reactive handoff approaches to intelligent proactive strategies, the upcoming Sixth Generation (6G) wireless network will play a major role. These proactive systems using Advanced Artificial Intelligence & machine learning, will support mobility decision which are predictive, dynamic optimization and network control parameters will be adopted automatically. Hyun-Seo Park et al. (2024) and Abbas Ibrahim Mbulwa et al. (2024) suggested that, handover failures will be almost zero, with AI Driven prediction, if required in diverse terrestrial and non-terrestrial network setting also. this article tries to find Artificial intelligence powered proactive handoff unified framework which will incorporate time series prediction, deep reinforcement learning and tuning parameters automatically. Findings of this article include, performance comparisons, and simulated quality-of-service (QoS) which confirms that proactive handoff mechanisms will provide significant improvements over conventional reactive methods in terms of latency, reliability, and efficient resource utilization.

title: Autonomous Walking Trajectory Control of a Pin Replenishment Robot Considering Obstacles in the Surrounding Environment
authors: Liu Cao, Yuchen Zhu, Jiang Guo, Jianmin Hu
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Path planning based solely on preset simple rules can only achieve mechanical obstacle avoidance in a fixed pattern, resulting in poor trajectory control stability. Therefore, this study investigates the autonomous walking trajectory control of a pin replenishment robot considering surrounding environmental obstacles. Constructing a dynamic point cloud map of the robot's surrounding environment, an improved <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$A^{*}$</tex> algorithm is introduced to incorporate a dynamic obstacle avoidance cost function for global path planning, and a dynamic cost function is designed to generate the robot's optimal safe path. A PID controller is used to adjust the robot's linear velocity and angular velocity, and acceleration and angular velocity constraints are introduced to achieve autonomous walking trajectory control for the pin replenishment robot. Experiments show that the proposed method maintains linear velocity control deviations mostly within <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 0.1 ~\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$</tex> and angular velocity control deviations mostly within <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 0.2 \text{rad} / \mathrm{s}$</tex>. Compared to the comparative method, it can plan a smoother trajectory that is closer to the ideal path, achieving an obstacle avoidance success rate of 95.8 % with 50 obstacles, thus improving the stability of trajectory control.

title: PATS: Physics-Aware Transformer Scheduler for MPQUIC in LEO Satellite Networks
authors: Zhanglei Wu, Yidian Liu, Zhanjun Liu, Chengchao Liang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: This paper introduces PATS, a novel MPQUIC scheduler designed to address the high Bandwidth-Delay Product and dynamic link conditions of LEO satellite networks. To overcome the limitations of existing bandwidth estimation, PATS integrates a physics-guided hybrid estimator with Deep Reinforcement Learning (DDPG) and Deficit Round Robin (DRR) scheduling. Key innovations include a satellite-aware adaptive filter, a Fluid Congestion Index, and a Transformer-based temporal feature extractor. NS-3 simulations demonstrate that PATS significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, offering superior throughput stability and reducing flow completion time by approximately 40%.

title: Role Play as a First Approach to the Professional Role in Students of Electrical Engineering
authors: Felipe Alarcón, Matías Pavez, Alvaro Hoffer, Constanza Lazcano, Camilo Maury
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: The simulation of real-life cases in the initial training of electrical engineers remains a rarely used strategy, despite the benefits reported in previous research. This article analyzes the strengths and challenges experienced by electrical engineering students when confronted with their first real-world professional situation, through the Role-Playing methodology. The experience involved 14 students, and 2 external expert evaluators who employed a pre-designed rubric to assess the students' performance across the various tasks proposed in the simulation. In addition, a perception survey and a focus group interview were conducted to perform a data crossing analysis and evaluate the consistency of the findings. The results indicate that simulating real-world scenarios through Role-Playing, supports the application of theoretical knowledge acquired throughout the course, promotes its integration with content from other courses, strengthens collaborative work and fosters the development of engineering professional identity. Furthermore, it significantly enhances students' intrinsic motivation when addressing problems related to their field of development.

title: Unified Multi-Domain Framework With Outlier Exposure and Adversarial Learning for First-Shot Unsupervised Anomalous Sound Detection
authors: Shouzhi Liu, Hui Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Unsupervised anomalous sound detection for industrial machinery faces critical challenges including anomalous sample scarcity and domain shift across acoustic environments, particularly in first-shot scenarios requiring generalization to unseen machine types without hyperparameter tuning. This paper proposes a unified multi-domain framework that synergistically integrates Outlier Exposure, domain-adversarial learning, and self-supervised regularization. We introduce a novel strategy treating supplemental normal sounds as pseudo-outliers with margin-based deviation loss, hybrid domain adaptation combining gradient reversal with Maximum Mean Discrepancy constraints, and adaptive multi-task learning with rotation prediction for robust knowledge transfer. Extensive experiments on DCASE 2025 Task 2 development dataset demonstrate superior performance with average improvements of 9.1 % in source AUC, 15.2 % in target AUC, and 8.0 % in pAUC across seven machine types, validating the effectiveness of our approach for industrial predictive maintenance applications.

title: Video-Text Retrieval with Good Bias Exploitation
authors: Hua Lan, Chaohui Lv
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: As a core task in multimodality, video-text retrieval faces two major limitations: text bias in data annotations and spurious correlations learned due to video-text information asymmetry. Existing debiasing methods regard all biases as harmful information, overlooking the positive role of “good biases” (i.e., commonsense-based semantic extensions). To address this issue, this paper proposes a text-guided selective debiasing framework, which takes text semantics as the anchor to filter redundant frames and enhance the representation of key frames associated with “ good biases”. Subsequently, it constructs accurate semantic associations through cross-modal alignment optimization. Comprehensive experiments are conducted on the MSR-VTT and LSMDC datasets, comparing the proposed framework with state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods such as X-CLIP and DFLB. The results demonstrate that the framework can effectively leverage “good biases” and improve retrieval accuracy.

title: The Drone Tree Network: A High-Assurance Distributed Framework for BVLOS-Compliant UAV Logistics
authors: Praveen Manimangalam
abstract: The commercialization of distributed Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) logistics, particularly Beyond Visual Line of Sight (BVLOS) operations, requires addressing regulatory compliance, autonomous scalability, and system assurance. This paper presents the Drone Tree Network (DTN), a hierarchical cyberphysical framework designed to support performance-based BVLOS operations aligned with emerging Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) Part 108 requirements. The DTN integrates a cognitive decision stack combining a Weighted Sum Model for mission prioritization with an enforced emergency dispatch threshold and a hybrid Lazy Theta and D Lite path-planning architecture for real-time rerouting. Payload release safety is ensured through a dual-authorization protocol requiring both cryptographic validation using a FIPS 140-3-compliant hardware module and physical ground verification via a convolutional neural network and PointNet-based sensor fusion model, generating a ground-stability confidence score. Command-andcontrol resilience is strengthened through frequency-hopping spread spectrum and Unscented Kalman Filter-based positioning integrity. Simulation-based validation demonstrates measurable improvements in path efficiency, command-and-control robustness under jamming conditions, and fleet scalability, providing empirical evidence toward scalable BVLOS-compliant UAV logistics.
