title: MOS-RC Based Fractional Order Grounded Synthetic Inductor and its Application
authors: Satvik Ojha, Bhawna Aggarwal, Pradnya Moon
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: This paper introduces a new MOS-RC based fractional order grounded synthetic inductor(FOGSI). This FOGSI has been designed using a MOS-RC based grounded inductor alongside a fractional order capacitor (FOC). The FOC has been realized using a 5th order Foster-1 RC network. The results for FOGSI of fractional order 0.2, 0.5 and 0.9 have been discussed in detail to verify the operation of the proposed circuit. The proposed circuits, verified through SPICE simulations using 180nm TSMC CMOS model file with a ± 0.7 V supply voltage, exhibit stable operation over a wide frequency range of 10-100 MHz. Moreover, its application in fractional order bandpass filter has been presented.

title: A Sequential Model-Based Predictive Control for ANPC Shunt Active Power Filters for Power Quality Improvement in Three-Phase Four-Wire Systems
authors: Matias Aguilar, Juan Insfran, Alfredo Renault, Leonardo Comparatore, Julio Pacher, Osvaldo Gonzalez, Marco Rivera, Patrick Wheeler, Eduardo Espinosa
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper presents a Sequential Model-Based Predictive Control (SMBPC) for a three-level Active Neutral-Point Clamped (ANPC) Shunt Active Power Filter (SAPF) to mitigate harmonics, reactive power, and neutral current in four-wire systems. The control uses a synchronous reference frame (SRF) to generate compensation references. A key contribution is a two-stage sequential optimization that eliminates the need for weighting factors by hierarchically prioritizing current tracking and DC-link voltage balancing. Simulation results validate the strategy, demonstrating excellent dynamic response, robust voltage regulation, and significant power quality improvement, achieving unity power factor and a source current Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) well below standard limits.

title: A Federated Metadata Architecture for Scalable Governance in Lakehouse-Based AI Platforms
authors: Vipul Shyam Javeri, Ruiqing Zhu, Kok Li, Lanqing Hou
abstract: Modern enterprise AI and predictive analytics platforms increasingly rely on lakehouse architectures that unify data engineering, analytics, and machine learning workloads. However, governance and metadata management remain significant challenges when legacy Hive-based metastores coexist with modern unified catalog systems. This paper presents a federated metadata architecture that enables incremental migration from Hive Metastore (HMS) environments to a unified governance layer while maintaining operational continuity. The proposed design introduces a read-through federation model, centralized access control, and lineage-aware metadata management without immediate data relocation. We discuss architectural patterns, migration phases, and governance implications, and analyze how this approach enables scalable, secure, and compliant AI analytics in enterprise environments.

title: A Systematic Pipeline of ECG Classification Using CWT and ECOC Meta-Learning
authors: Gabrielle Villalba, Muhammad Ullah
abstract: This paper proposes a scalable, interpretable automated system for detecting arrhythmias in single-lead electrocardiogram (ECG) signals. The pipeline creates timefrequency representations of ECG signals through continuous wavelet transform (CWT) scalograms. Segment-level classification is performed using an adaptive residual pre-trained network (APRN) that utilizes ResNet-18 transfer learning. Temporal-frequency features are used to categorize segments, which are displayed using Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM), enabling transparency at each segment. Segment-based predicted classifications are aggregated to form meta-feature representations; these meta-features are classified with an AdaBoost-based error-correcting output code (ECOC) ensemble with an asymmetric cost function design to maximize sensitivity to congestive heart failure (CHF). As new ECG datasets become available, progressive learning updates enable continuous improvement in the system's performance. Permutation analysis can be used to identify the most influential meta-features for the final classification decision. Performance was validated on three external datasets: MIT-BIH Arrhythmia, BIDMC CHF, and PTB-XL, reaching a mean cross-validation accuracy greater than 97%. Outcomes revealed that the proposed system produces accurate, interpretable information for real-time clinical monitoring applications.

title: Robust Pilot Fatigue Estimation Via Adaptive Fusion of Eeg and Ocular Features
authors: FuKai Wang, Yanyu Lu
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Signal non-stationarity and intermittent sensor loss complicate pilot fatigue monitoring in operational environments. We introduce R-KFusion, a multimodal framework integrating geometry-aware EEG descriptors and ocular metrics via a quality-adaptive Kalman filter. By modeling EEG covariance on the Riemannian manifold, we derive a fatigue index robust to signal amplitude drifts while simultaneously extracting ocular indicators such as PERCLOS. To address varying sensor reliability, the fusion filter dynamically modulates measurement noise based on real-time signal quality indices, ensuring continuity even when one modality degrades. Validation in a Level-D A320 full-flight simulator demonstrates that the fused index correlates strongly with flight performance degradation (mean Spearman correlation coefficient approximately 0.65, best coefficient <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.71$</tex>), consistently outperforming single-modality baselines.

title: AI-Integrated Insurance Framework: Scenario Based Analytical Approach in Hybrid Environment
authors: Malar Mangai Kondappan
abstract: Insurance companies operate in a hybrid environment combining legacy systems with modern platforms. This leads to silo-ed technology stacks, disconnected data flows, and increased manual workloads for cross-system management [1], [2]. In this work, I propose an integrated AI Insurance framework that bridges these gaps introduced by the disparate systems through the unified AI service layer, both operationally and technically [4], [6], [7]. The AI layer integrates seamlessly with the modern platform without the need for legacy system replacement [5]. This unified AI-service layer comprises embedded, contextual, supportive, and primary AI technologies that automate the data extraction, cross-system workflows, risk-based rating, and volume predictive claims workload management [8], [10]. To assess the potential impact of this framework implementation, I have conducted scenario-based analytical evaluation from various research, industry case studies [3], [12], [15] and inputs from business experts to gather conservative estimates to quantify the proposed framework methodology's operational, financial, integration and customer experience metrics. The projections calibrated from these metrics suggests that claim processing could reduce by 45%, fraud detection scores could improve by 21%, and self-service adoption could increase by 32% under the proposed framework in the evaluated scenarios. For better visibility to business, I have also created a unified metric, AI Integration Effectiveness Index (AIEI) that provides a holistic overview of the integration performance and AI value realization [22]. Overall, the framework provides insurers with a practical road-map for modernizing legacy back-office operations through transformational abilities leveraged from the integrated AI technologies with measurable impacts.

title: AI-Driven Real-Time Data Synchronization in Distributed Financial Systems
authors: Karri Sairamakrishna BuchiReddy, Phaneendra Siddana, Sandeep Kumar Srivastava, Ramireddy Chilakala
abstract: With distributed financial microservices, the DualWrite problem frequently results in data discrepancy between payment gateways and in-house ledgers. Conventional reconciliation schemes are based on high-latency batch reconciliation or hard-coded rules, and cannot identify Soft Drifts, small corruptions in the data (e.g. 3% deviation) that resemble normal variance. The paper suggests a real-time reconciliation model that combines an Apache Kafka streaming high-throughput system and an Unsupervised Isolation Forest anomaly detector. The experimental outcomes have shown that although the application of static rules resulted in a Recall rate of only 52.1% (it does not detect soft drifts), the offered AI model attained 100% Recall in all types of drifts. Moreover, the system had a consistent latency of 3.76 ms which was found to be viable in high-frequency trading settings where low-latency and data integrity are of utmost importance.

title: Marginal Costs in the Chilean Electricity Market with Inter-Hourly Linkage
authors: David Sanhueza, Rodrigo Rozas, Leonardo Gacitua
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Chile forecasts that variable renewable energy will account for more than 60% of its installed capacity by 2043, demanding price signals that adequately compensate for operational flexibility. Temporal Locational Marginal Pricing (TLMP) enhance conventional nodal pricing by embedding, into the energy price, the dual variables associated with generation ramp-rate and storage state-of-charge constraints of the Optimal Power Flow problem (OPF). Using the LGPLAN tool, this study solves the OPF for the Chilean National Electric System projected to 2043, and benchmarks net revenues under the current LMP scheme against the proposed TLMP. The results show that technologies with low ramping capability and 4-hour storage systems experience significant revenue reductions, while 12-hour storage systems remain virtually unaffected. These changes increase the tariff income for the system operator and demonstrate that intertemporal constraints decisively influence price formation under the TLMP scheme. The findings underscore the advantage of explicitly valuing operational flexibility when designing future tariff structures for the Chilean National Electric System.

title: A Scalable Framework for Multidimensional Quality Assessment and Benchmarking of EEG Datasets
authors: Mario Quiles Pérez, Sergio López Bernal, Eduardo Horna Prat, Luis Montesano del Campo, Lorenzo Fernández Maimó, Alberto Huertas Celdrán
abstract: Ensuring the quality of datasets containing electroencephalography signals remains challenging due to heterogeneous acquisition protocols, diverse preprocessing practices, and inconsistent annotation standards. Existing toolboxes mainly focus on signal cleaning and pipeline checks, while generic data quality frameworks lack domain specificity and do not provide an interpretable dataset-level score for electroencephalography datasets used in tasks such as affect recognition and event-related paradigms. This work introduces a platform for dataset-level quality assessment of electroencephalography collections tailored to these downstream tasks. The platform computes a set of indicators that capture session completeness, consistency of session length, class balance, annotation timing, signal-to-noise ratio, outlier prevalence, feature information content, and class separability. It then combines these indicators into a normalized global Quality Score ranging from 0 to 100. The aggregation relies on order-preserving mappings and subject-wise summarization to enable robust comparisons across datasets and to prevent longer recordings from dominating the score. The evaluation uses six public datasets from affect recognition, event-related paradigm and motor imagery, all processed under identical protocols. Classification performance is reported using Balanced Accuracy and Area Under the ROC Curve. The global score reflects differences in data integrity, temporal consistency, and separability while remaining interpretable across heterogeneous collections. In addition, correlation analyses indicate positive associations between separability-oriented indicators and downstream performance, and negative associations with a higher prevalence of outliers. Targeted training adjustments guided by these indicators, including the removal of highly contaminated windows and loss reweighting based on class priors, consistently improve performance. Implementation and configuration files are publicly available to support reproducibility and adoption.

title: Performance Analysis of Dynamically Truncated Approx Multiplier for Low Power VLSI Applications
authors: Sai Rohith Thumapala, Manisha Bharti
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: The design provided in this paper is of a pipelined-based approximate multiplier, which uses pipelining mechanisms and is inspired by high-accuracy approximate compressors. Approximate multipliers provide a practical solution in situation where accuracy is not as important as speed, power, area, and accuracy. This piece of work aims at creating a scalable and low-energy performance-sensitive solution to systems, e.g. Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALUs), using dynamic truncation and synthesis-based clock-gating. The technique consists of pipelining to increase throughput and reduce latency and the use of synthesis-based clock gating to selectively gate the clock signal when the state is idled, so as to reduce dynamic power use. Xilinx tools are used to streamline the design and simulation and verification processes. This strategy enables trade-offs among computational performance, energy usage and precision needed.

title: Fuel Cell and Intelligent Power Processing Using Nonlinear Control and Quantum Computing with the Hadoop Ecosystem
authors: Wil McClay, Loni Taylor
abstract: This article is a detailed scientific study concerning a proton exchange membrane fuel cell [1], which is coupled to a DC-to-DC converter as the power processor, serving as a power source. The novel aspect of the article is the use of a new controller or nonlinear observer [5], [6] to predict parameter estimation of the fuel cell and the DC-to-DC converter as the load potential changes for the automated control system. Nonlinear control algorithms, which include nonlinear observers, were developed for such systems [10]. Additionally, the utilization of Quantum Computing with MATLAB, IBM Qiskit Ecosystem [11], open-source Python libraries were used to design, analyze, and simulate the automated control system utilizing quantum machine learning algorithms to prototype and uniquely optimize nonlinear sliding mode observers to predict parameter estimation(s) with data ingestion, timestamping and analysis integrating the Hadoop Ecosystem with Apache Spark, Apache Cassandra and Apache HBase NoSQL databases [7]. Preliminary outcomes indicate the potential for improved estimation accuracy, faster transient response, and heightened resilience to nonlinear behaviors inherent in MEMS-scale fuel-cell systems. The combined methodology demonstrates significant potential for next-generation smart energy systems where advanced control, quantum-accelerated computation, and distributed data architectures work in concert to optimize performance.

title: A Key Node Identification Method by Combining Attribute-Based Random Walk and Graph Neural Networks
authors: Yi Chen, Fan Yang, Jian Xie, Ziwei Tao
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Key node Identification in complex networks is one of the critical methods for controlling network propagation process. Effectively combining network topology with node attributes to identify key node remains to be explored. This paper proposes an Attributes-based Random Walk (ABRWalk) method. Firstly, by using existing centrality algorithms as node attributes, an attribute based random walks is proposed. Then, a comprehensive feature matrix is proposed by combing network topology and attribute based random walks. Secondly, ABRWalk employs Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) to learn hierarchical latent representations of nodes and implement key node identification. Comparative experiments against eight baseline methods are conducted on 9 synthetic networks and 12 real-world networks. Experimental results demonstrate that ABRWalk outperforms other baselines in accurately identifying key nodes.

title: PYXIS: A Highly Predictable Toolchain for FPGA Circuit Production of Advanced GNC Algorithm
authors: Inès Winandy, Arnaud Dion, Pierre-Loïc Garoche, Florent Manni
publish: 2025 IEEE 11th International Conference on Space Mission Challenges for Information Technology (SMC-IT)
abstract: The configuration of Field-Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs) within the context of command law implementation for aerospace systems is a complex process. In a previous work, we proposed the use of dataflow programming languages as the input for the synthesis process and as an alternative to traditional hardware programming languages. The proposed translation flow has been demonstrated to be faster than current state-of-the-art tools while simultaneously offering greater predictability in the inferred hardware component of the design. This paper extends this previous work, addressing identified challenges. A first one is related to the use of a generated circuit to perform Guidance, Navigation and Control (GNC) functions: the generated netlist requires synchronization to be used within a closed-loop system. A second one concerns the generation of circuits for custom datatypes: target descriptions must be automatically generated to facilitate technology mapping. As a consequence, a novel toolchain is proposed, providing the capability to generate synchronised circuits with arbitrary precision for computation.

title: Optimization of Bacteriophage Production in Batch Bioreactors
authors: Gabriel Castro Sahli, Marina Contreras Lafaury, Fernanda Santos Astudillo, Jairo Pinto Sánchez, José Ricardo Pérez Correa
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This study presents a dynamic modeling and optimization framework for bacteriophage production in batch bioreactors. A mechanistic model is developed to describe interactions among susceptible, infected, and resistant bacterial subpopulations, as well as free phages. The model explicitly incorporates infection timing via a smooth pulse function and accounts for key biological processes, including spontaneous mutation, phage adsorption, bacterial lysis, and resistance emergence. Model calibration was performed using synthetic datasets with varying temporal resolutions and added noise. Sensitivity analysis, the Fisher Information Matrix, and t-value criteria were used to identify and fix non-informative parameters, reducing the initial eight-parameter model to a more parsimonious four-parameter version. The reduced model was then used to optimize phage inoculum size and injection timing using the Grey Wolf Optimizer, aiming to maximize peak phage concentration. The optimal strategy-injecting <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10^{7} \text{PFU} / \text{mL}$</tex> at 1.52 hours-led to a 329% increase in final phage yield compared to the unoptimized baseline. Monte Carlo simulations based on parameter uncertainty yielded a mean phage titer of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.08 \times 10^{9} \text{PFU/mL} (\pm 0.22 \times 10^{9})$</tex>. These results demonstrate that optimized infection protocols can significantly enhance phage yields while remaining robust to biological variability, thus supporting the design of scalable and efficient production processes.

title: CoR-MoLE: Multi-Disciplinary Team Decision Support via Specialist-Collaborative Reasoning with Mixture-of-LoRA-Experts
authors: Wentao Chen, Siting Lin, Wenzhao Liang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Multi-Disciplinary Team (MDT) consultation represents the gold standard for managing complex clinical cases with multi-system comorbidities. However, identifying candidates for MDT from extensive Electronic Health Records (EHRs) remains challenging, as neither traditional rule-based systems with limited coverage nor generic Large Language Models (LLMs) prone to the “lost-in-the-middle” phenomenon can sufficiently meet this clinical demand. We propose CoR-MoLE (CoReasoning Mixture-of-LoRA-Experts), a framework emulating multi-specialty consultations via three synergistic components: (1) a Dynamic Clinical Memory Bank that transforms lengthy records into retrievable facts to mitigate long-context limitations; (2) a Specialist MoLE Module employing department-specific LoRA experts to replicate diverse clinical reasoning; and (3) a Conflict-Aware Decision Mechanism that derives MDT probability from expert activation entropy while providing evidence-based explanations via attention back-tracing. Experiments on the MDT-Predict benchmark demonstrate that CoR-MoLE achieves an F1-score of 0.893 and AUC of 0.912, significantly outperforming Qwen3-235B <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(+15.2 \% \mathrm{F} 1)$</tex> and standard LoRA finetuning (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$+9.8 \%$</tex> F1). Notably, the Entropy Mechanism provides a 2.1 % improvement, supporting our central hypothesis that the expert activation entropy serves as a reliable indicator of MDT necessity. These results validate that structured clinical mimicry and entropy-based conflict detection effectively transform expert disagreement into a predictive asset for automated MDT triage.

title: Research on Intelligent Big Data Processing and Performance Optimization Technology for Cloud Computing Environments
authors: Jinyi Jiao
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Cloud platforms have become the default execution environment for large-scale analytics, yet practical deployments still suffer from imbalanced resource scheduling, high end-to-end latency, and limited throughput when workloads mix batch, streaming, and iterative computation. This paper presents an intelligent processing and optimization workflow for cloud big data systems that couples (i) resource awareness, (ii) task scheduling, and (iii) data shunting into a unified control loop. First, an online resource-perception module continuously collects telemetry from cloud nodes and predicts short-horizon compute and bandwidth states using a lightweight learning model. Second, a dynamic task-sharding strategy splits jobs into elastic shards and applies SLA-aware priority scheduling with data-locality constraints to reduce queueing and straggler effects. Third, a data-shunting layer performs hot/cold separation and distributed caching with adaptive prefetch and write coalescing to cut I/O stalls. Experiments on a Kubernetes-based cluster with representative batch and streaming workloads show that the proposed workflow reduces average execution latency by 40%, improves cluster resource utilization to 89.6%, and increases sustained throughput by 32% compared with commonly used cloud scheduling baselines. The results indicate that joint optimization across sensing, scheduling, and data movement is necessary to unlock predictable performance for cloud big data processing.

title: PenAgent: A Retrieval-Augmented Multi-Agent Framework for Automated Penetration Testing
authors: Qihui Liu, Chen Feng, Kexin Zhu, Zhide Chen
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Penetration testing is critical for identifying system vulnerabilities. Traditional manual methods, however, are laborintensive and rely heavily on scarce expert knowledge. Existing automated tools often require complete network visibility or specific pre-trained models, limiting their applicability in blackbox scenarios. To address these limitations, we propose PenAgent, a MetaGPT-based multi-agent framework that automates the entire penetration testing lifecycle. The system coordinates four specialized agents to perform reconnaissance, scanning, exploitation, and logic optimization via Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). We evaluated PenAgent against ten N-day vulnerabilities, demonstrating that GPT-4-based agents achieved an exploitation success rate exceeding 80%. Furthermore, RAG integration significantly boosted the performance of smaller models (e.g., GPT-3.5-turbo) to levels comparable with GPT-4. These findings underscore the potential of collaborative LLM agents in autonomous security assessments.

title: Assessing Planning Algorithms for Structuring LLM-Derived Workflows in Agentic AI Systems
authors: Ganapathi Yeleswarapu, Chakra Pavan Kumar Kota, Ankur Vora, Sridhar Kumar Irujolla
abstract: Workflow decomposition is an important capability in agentic artificial intelligence (AI) systems, enabling autonomous agents to transform high-level goals into structured, executable task graphs. As agentic systems increasingly integrate model-based reasoning, tool invocation, memory, and dynamic planning, decomposition quality can affect reliability, interpretability, and safety. A variety of planning and reasoning algorithms have been used for related tasks, including Breadth-First Search (BFS), Depth-First Search (DFS), A* search, Hierarchical Task Networks (HTN), and Genetic Algorithms (GA), but their comparative behavior in agentic workflows remains unclear. This paper empirically compares these five algorithm families for workflow decomposition in agent-like settings. We evaluate them on (i) a synthetic benchmark of 1,000 procedurally generated hierarchical task trees and (ii) realistic agent-oriented case studies such as troubleshooting, room preparation, and small assembly tasks. We implement all methods in a common Python framework and compare them in terms of decomposition quality, runtime, scalability, interpretability, and sensitivity to structural noise. In our experiments, A* tends to offer a good overall trade-off between optimality and efficiency, HTN produces the most interpretable decompositions, BFS and DFS provide useful baselines with different resource profiles, and GA supports flexible but computationally costly solutions. These observations offer practical guidance for selecting planning modules in agentic AI architectures.

title: Evaluating the Impact of Data Anonymization on AI Model Performance Across Sensitive Domains
authors: Mahesh Shivnatri, Kuber Jain, Raghavender Puchhakayala, Willison Lopes
abstract: Privacy protection has become a critical requirement for data sharing in healthcare and financial environments, where the exposure of quasi-identifiers or sensitive attributes can lead to re-identification, attribute disclosure, and regulatory violations. At the same time, organizations increasingly rely on machine learning and AI-driven analytics built on such data, creating tension between model performance and privacy guarantees. This paper presents a comparative study of three classical anonymization techniques—k-anonymity, I-diversity, and t-closeness—applied to two distinct domains with differing privacy risks and utility constraints. Experiments are conducted using the UCI Heart Disease dataset and the Credit Card Fraud Detection dataset, representing medical diagnosis and fraud detection contexts, respectively. Privacy risk is quantified through equivalence class size, sensitive attribute entropy, and distributional divergence, while utility is measured using the degradation of downstream classification performance. Results (reported as mean ± standard deviation over repeated runs) indicate that healthcare data experiences more pronounced loss of model accuracy as anonymization strength increases, whereas the large-scale fraud dataset exhibits lower sensitivity to distribution-preserving anonymization. The cross-domain comparison highlights that no single technique offers universally optimal trade-offs, and it emphasizes the need for domain-aware anonymization strategies to support privacy-preserving AI workflows in practice.

title: Hardware-in-the-Loop Validation of a Transformerless Electric Vehicle Charger
authors: Daniel Velasquez, Alexander Rojas, Matias Diaz, Luis Pua, Luca Tarisciotti, Yeiner Arias-Esquivel
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Transformerless topologies offer a promising solution for high-power, efficient Electric Vehicle (EV) c hargers, but their practical implementation requires rigorous pre-prototyping validation using risk-mitigation tools such as real-time simulation and Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) testing. Therefore, this paper presents a HIL validation of a transformerless EV charger regulated by a cascaded control structure based on proportional-integral (PI) controllers. Using an HIL platform with a real-time power stage emulator and a dedicated digital controller, the performance of the proposed EV charger is evaluated under various dynamic conditions. The results confirm the effectiveness of the control strategy, which achieves precise output voltage regulation, stable operation during load transients, and low-distortion grid currents.

title: PoseDesign: Optimizing Human Pose Estimation with Deep Learning
authors: Congcong Ruan
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)

title: Integration of Augmented Reality for Spatially-Guided Respiratory Auscultation in Clinical Training
authors: Antonio Rienzo, Togo Arredondo, Omar Acevedo, Álvaro Huirimilla, Daniel Ciudad, María Rubio, José Lorant, Ignacio Carvajal
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This work presents the development of an interactive educational system for clinical auscultation training, centered on augmented reality (AR) to enhance student autonomy and spatial accuracy during practical sessions. The system integrates a wireless stethoscope with a mobile application that uses computer vision and AR overlays to display real–time anatomical landmarks and auscultation points on the subject's body. This visual guidance allows students to perform maneuvers independently, reducing the need for direct supervision. Preliminary validation with prototype tests demonstrated improvements in user orientation, precision in device placement, and potential to standardize skill acquisition. The paper describes the AR architecture, key implementation challenges, and results from controlled practice sessions. Conclusions and future improvements toward broader clinical integration are discussed. This approach complements existing frameworks for simulation–based education and aligns with current trends in digital medical learning.

title: Technical Program Committee
authors: N/A

title: Improving Macromodeling Accuracy for Power Distribution Networks at Both Low and High Frequencies Using Complex Z ref
authors: Chiu-Chih Chou, Tzu-Yuan Fang

title: Revisiting Negative Capacitance Effect for 1T-DRAM Application
authors: Md Moqueet Ahmad, Alok Kumar Kamal, Harshit Agarwal, Avinash Lahgere
abstract: In this article, we present a highly scaled one-transistor dynamic random access memory (1T-DRAM) cell using the ferroelectric (FE) material exploiting the negative capacitance (NC) effect. The Landau–Khalatnikov (L–K) model parameters have been accurately calibrated to fit the simulated S-curve to the experimentally reported hysteresis characteristics. The proposed 1T-DRAM device utilizes the voltage amplification due to the NC effect for better memory performance on less supply voltage and shorter gate lengths as compared to existing 1T-DRAMs. At 300 K, the proposed 1T-DRAM provides a reliable sense margin (SM) ~<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0.7~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>A/<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m, which is sufficient for embedded DRAM applications, exhibits high write speed due to steep switching caused by the NC effect, a larger retention time (RT) of ~50 ms that is ~<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> larger than the baseline 1T-DRAM, and also higher read current ratio (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${I}_{{R}{1}}/{I}_{{R}{0}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>). Moreover, at 358 K, the proposed 1T-DRAM shows the SM of ~<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0.65~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>A/<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m. The retention of proposed 1T-DRAM at 358 K is 20 ms, which is ~<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$40\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> higher than the baseline 1T-DRAM. Furthermore, the performance analysis of the proposed 1T-DRAM has been carried out through TCAD simulations on <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${2}\times {2}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> crossbar memory array level at 300 and 358 K.

title: Global Cooperative Lane-Change Decision for Multi-Conflict Scenarios
authors: Fang Zong, Shuo Jiang, Qianwei Dong, Sheng Yue, Huiyong Zhang
abstract: In a V2X environment, connected and automated vehicles (CAVs) aim to enhance traffic efficiency and reduce energy consumption. However, on road segments between intersections, heterogeneous decision-making processes and varying destinations often result in disorderly lane changes, giving rise to trajectory and role conflicts. To address these issues, this study analyzes two types of multi-vehicle lane-change (LC) conflicts—trajectory and role conflicts—as well as complex scenarios arising from their interaction, and proposes a cooperative multi-CAV lane-change (MCLC) decision model that integrates global optimization with local conflict resolution. Simulation results show that, compared with the MOBIL, MPC, and SG models, the proposed model reduces average travel time by 8.2%, 6.5%, and 3.5%, respectively, and decreases the mean absolute deviation (MAD) of acceleration by 1.8% and 16.7% compared with the MOBIL and MPC models, respectively. Compared with the SG model, the MAD increases by 3.1%. Although the MAD is slightly higher than that of the SG model, the proposed model improves overall traffic efficiency with only a marginal reduction in stability. Results confirm the model's effectiveness and adaptability in multi-conflict LC scenarios. The model ensures efficient LC decisions for CAVs under high LC ratios and heavy traffic volumes.

title: Fed-KAN: Federated Learning With Kolmogorov–Arnold Networks for Traffic Prediction: KAN-Based Learning
authors: Engin Zeydan, Cristian J. Vaca-Rubio, Luis Blanco Botana, Roberto Pereira, Màrius Caus López, Kapal Dev
abstract: Nonterrestrial networks (NTNs) are becoming a critical component of modern communication infrastructures, especially with the advent of low Earth orbit (LEO) satellite systems. Traditional centralized learning approaches face major challenges in such networks due to high latency, intermittent connectivity, and limited bandwidth. Federated learning (FL) is a promising alternative as it enables decentralized training while maintaining data privacy. However, existing FL models, such as FL with multilayer perceptrons (Fed-MLP), can struggle with high computational complexity and poor adaptability to dynamic NTN environments. This article provides a detailed analysis for FL with Kolmogorov–Arnold networks (Fed-KAN), its implementation, and performance improvements over traditional FL models in NTN environments for traffic forecasting. The proposed Fed-KAN is a novel approach that utilizes the functional approximation capabilities of KANs in an FL framework. We evaluate Fed-KAN compared to Fed-MLP on a traffic dataset of a real satellite operator and show a significant reduction in training and test loss. Our results show that Fed-KAN can achieve a 49% reduction in average test loss compared to Fed-MLP, highlighting its improved performance and better generalization ability. At the end of the article, we also discuss some potential applications of Fed-KAN within open radio access network (O-RAN) and Fed-KAN usage for split functionalities in NTN architecture.

title: OphFusionNet: Uncertainty-driven multi-scale multimodal feature fusion network for ophthalmic diseases classification
authors: Bo Wu, Weifang Zhu, Dehui Xiang, Xinjian Chen, Tao Peng, Chenwei Gui, Qing Peng, Fei Shi
abstract: Multimodal imaging has become an essential tool in clinical ophthalmology, offering complementary perspectives for disease diagnosis. However, current automated diagnostic approaches often fail to fully exploit the rich, complementary information provided by different imaging modalities. In this paper, to advance automated ophthalmic disease diagnosis through effective multimodal data integration, we propose the OphFusionNet, a novel multimodal learning framework based on uncertainty-driven multi-scale multimodal feature fusion. Drawing inspiration from clinical observations that ophthalmic lesions often appear at multiple spatial scales, we design a multi-scale feature fusion module with sparse self-attention (MSFF-SSA). This module captures hierarchical representations while suppressing redundancy, thereby enhancing both the expressiveness and efficiency of the extracted features. To further improve multimodal fusion, we introduce an uncertainty-aware multimodal fusion module with a game-theoretic selection strategy (UMF-GTSS). This component estimates the uncertainty associated with different features and adaptively weights them based on their relative reliability, yielding more robust and trustworthy diagnostic outcomes. To mitigate the tendency to over-rely on dominant modalities and underutilize the informative potential of subordinate ones, we propose a modality distillation strategy (MDS), which leverages multimodal features to guide and refine the learning of single-modal representations. This strategy enhances generalization and boosts the discriminative capacity of each individual modality. The OphFusionNet was evaluated on four publicly available ophthalmic datasets. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves superior multimodal integration, resulting in state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in multi-modal ophthalmic disease diagnosis. The code will be available at: https://github.com/wb66715/OphFusionNet.

title: FedScope – Federated Host Embeddings from Telescope Traffic: Design and Implementation
authors: Kai Huang, Andrea Sordello, Rodolfo Valentim, Luca Vassio, Idilio Drago, Marco Mellia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: network telescope is a range of IP addresses that host no services. Millions of bots and scanners contact it to look for vulnerable systems, and the traffic it exposes is fundamental to understanding malicious activities. The visibility a telescope offers depends on its size and geolocation, and merging the information from multiple telescopes could help increase visibility and uncover more malicious activities. However, sharing raw telescope data is complicated, calling for solutions that allow one to directly share the knowledge rather than the data obtained from multiple deployments. In this paper, we explore the application of Federated Learning (FL) to create and share such global knowledge from the malicious activities seen in distributed telescopes. For that, we introduce FedScope, an FL-based solution for generating host embeddings in a distributed way. We compare FedScope to local and distributed alternatives in downstream tasks, such as sender classification or coordinated activities detection. We show that FedScope (i) produces embeddings of equal or higher quality than those of a single telescope; (ii) increases coverage, allowing the global model to monitor more malicious actors; (iii) avoids the sharing of the raw data, limiting exchanged data.

title: Enhancing Attitude Availability in Star-Depleted Cases: An Inertial/Star Sensor Fusion Method
authors: Zeyuan Xu, Guoxing Yi, Xiaohua Zhang, Yangguang Xie, Okyay Kaynak
abstract: This article presents a robust fusion method to address stellar observation scarcity in satellite attitude determination. Conventional systems fail with fewer than three navigation stars, jeopardizing missions requiring precise pointing. The proposed tightly coupled architecture embeds a hemispherical resonator gyroscope-based inertial reference within the star sensor's processing chain, which enables predictive star tracking during scarcity events. By reformulating an observation model and implementing an inertial-aided matching algorithm, the tightly coupled system maintains resilient performance across varying stellar visibility. A tailor-made Kalman filter explicitly formulated around the novel observation model is designed to reliably achieve the process of state prediction, update, and implementation. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method not only achieves accuracy better than one arc-minute under optimal conditions but sustains reliable solutions with just one or two stars, which eliminates the traditional three-star threshold. This capability significantly enhances mission reliability in star-depleted regions.

title: Emotion Similarity and Shift: Modeling Temporal Dynamic Interactions for Emotion Prediction in Conversation
authors: Xiaohan Shi, Xingfeng Li, Tomoki Toda
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: Understanding a speaker's emotional states in conversation is essential for dialogue systems. Emotion prediction in conversation (EPC) aims to predict a speaker's future emotional states based on context. Most existing studies focus on modeling dynamic interactions in the historical context but often overlook the effect of temporal dynamic interactions on the EPC task. To address this limitation, we propose the emotion interaction prediction (EIP) model, which explicitly captures the temporal dynamic interactions between the speaker and interlocutors. Specifically, EIP characterizes emotional dynamics by integrating emotion similarity to model long-term changes and emotion shifts to model short-term changes. In this study, we systematically investigate the efficacy of temporal dynamic interaction by comparing the EIP model with mainstream EPC models and emotion recognition in conversation (ERC)-based models. Then, we conduct a detailed analysis of the impact of long-term and short-term temporal dynamic interactions. Next, we assess the effectiveness of key architectural components integrated into the EIP model. Subsequently, we evaluate the contributions of different modality-specific representations and their combinations to EIP performance. After that, we also explore the correlation between context length and EPC performance. Finally, we analyze the robustness of mainstream EPC models across different conversation scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that 1) our proposed EIP model outperforms established EPC and ERC-based methods, and 2) the inclusion of emotion similarity and emotion shifts effectively enhances temporal interaction modeling. Furthermore, 3) key components such as the individual and interaction layer contribute to performance. Moreover, 4) the integration of speech and text representations further improves EIP performance. Additionally, 5) context length has effects on EPC performance. Notably, 6) the EIP model exhibits improved robustness compared with established EPC methods across different conversation scenarios.

title: A Comprehensive Modeling and Benchmarking Framework for Two-Terminal In Plane Magnetic Anisotropy SOT-MRAM
authors: Md Nahid Haque Shazon, Daniel C. Ralph, Azad Naeemi
abstract: In this study, a modeling and benchmarking framework is presented to assess compact two-terminal in-plane magnetic anisotropy spin-orbit torque magnetic random-access memory (IMA SOT-MRAM) cells. Although a previously proposed one-transistor one-magnetic tunnel junction configuration, with its two-terminal (2T) configuration, offers a more compact cell design that improves the cell density compared to a three-terminal (3T) design, it suffers from significant current crowding at the edge of the device and does not provide any notable advantage in terms of write time or energy. Here, we analyze a 2T variant incorporating a fixed layer with a length half of the free layer; this increases the effective path electrons pass through the SOT layer and provides a measurable benefit in terms of write energy and conduction current density. Two materials for the spin-orbit torque channel—AuPt and β-W—are investigated. With the β-W SOT channel, the IMA 2T SOT-MRAM cell featuring a half-fixed layer achieves a 25% decrease in maximum conduction current density and a 16% decrease in write energy compared to the conventional 3T IMA SOT-MRAM. Also, the maximum conduction current densities for the IMA cases are significantly lower compared to those of the field assisted perpendicular magnetic anisotropy (PMA) case. These results indicate that the half-fixed 2T architecture—when paired with CMOS-compatible β-W—offers a practical path to denser, lower-energy in-plane SOT-MRAMs.

title: DIFEX: A One-to-Many Forward-Style Exchange for Cross-Chain Electricity Trading
authors: Fuyang Deng, Qianhong Wu, Qiyuan Gao, Xiaopeng Dai, Yizhong Liu, Bo Qin, Willy Susilo, Robert H. Deng
abstract: In IoT-enabled electricity markets, trading is often conducted in a forward-style manner, where parties agree on prices and quantities in advance and settle upon future delivery. However, deploying such forward-style exchanges across chains is incompatible with execution-restricted blockchains and typically incurs significant cross-chain gas overhead. Moreover, existing low-overhead cross-chain mechanisms, particularly HTLC-based schemes, inherently rely on pairwise locking and thus cannot efficiently support one-to-many settlement. We propose an inter-chain forward-style exchange framework based on a dual-track validation architecture. The framework integrates a double-spend fraud-proof mechanism to ensure correctness and a proof-of-acceptance (PoAc) mechanism to reduce cross-chain overhead for successful settlements. We further introduce a novel k-directional hash lock enabling one-to-many settlement, allowing a single buy order to be split across multiple sellers. Our analysis shows that the framework resists griefing attacks, prevents adversarial losses, and preserves cross-chain atomicity. Experiments demonstrate support for execution-restricted blockchains such as Bitcoin, scalability to 862,000 sellers per order, and a 78.25% reduction in settlement cost compared to light-client approaches.

title: A Data-Based Approach for Aging Estimation of Railway Vehicles Propulsion Batteries
authors: Do-Hun Kwon, Seok-Jin Na, Beom-Jun Kim, In-Ho Cho
abstract: The risk of fires and malfunctions in batteries supplying propulsion power to railway vehicles increases with age, making accurate aging estimation crucial for safe operation. However, conventional aging estimation methods face significant limitations: experimental methods require complex battery removal processes unsuitable for short maintenance cycles, and model-based approaches involve substantial computational costs for large-scale battery systems with high voltage and large capacity. These operational constraints indicate the need for intelligent data-driven approaches that can leverage periodically collected operational data without physical battery testing. However, conventional data-driven methods experience accuracy deterioration due to insufficient training data in the early commercialization stage of eco-friendly railway vehicles. This paper proposes a Combined Learning (CL) methodology that integrates transfer learning and continual learning to address these challenges. The proposed three-stage algorithm begins with pre-training using abundant EV battery data to establish initial feature extraction capabilities, followed by initial model adaptation with limited railway vehicle data, and continuous model updates leveraging periodic maintenance cycles. A time-series-aware rehearsal mechanism prevents catastrophic forgetting during continual learning. The appropriate AI model for the proposed CL method was selected through comparison, and battery degradation was measured from operational data including voltage, current, and temperature. Experimental validation demonstrates that the proposed CL method achieves Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of 2.21% and Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 1.77%, representing approximately 13% error reduction compared to conventional learning methods.

title: PolarDepth: Polarization-Guided Monocular Depth for Visual Odometry
authors: Naitri Rajyaguru, Tianfu Wang, Aryan Tajne, Botao He, Jiayi Wu, Cornellia Fermuller, Christopher Metzler, Yiannis Aloimonos
abstract: Glass surfaces remain challenging for indoor robot perception. Depth sensors and RGB-only monocular depth estimation often fail because of reflections, refractions, and low-texture regions. To this end, we present PolarDepth, a polarization-enhanced monocular depth framework for glass-dominant environments. We utilize a single polarization sensor to obtain a standard RGB image and a three-channel encoding of polarization cues, designed to be compatible with RGB-trained foundation depth models, and which we call Polar-RGB. This representation enables recovery of structure in transparent and specular regions where RGB cues are unreliable. We predict depth from both the RGB and Polar-RGB representations and fuse the predictions using a learned per-pixel reliability gate for mid-level fusion, highlighting RGB in diffuse regions and polarization on reflective surfaces. We demonstrate improvements in depth estimation and visual odometry performance over RGB-only baselines in glass-walled corridors on both real-world and synthetic data.

title: A Data-Observation Hybrid Compensation Method for Precise Force Control of Cable-Driven Wrist Exoskeletons in Teleoperation
authors: Fengyi Shi, Jiawei Tan, Dong Wang, Xingfang Wang, Hui Li, Xiao Huang, Zhihong Jiang
abstract: High-precision force control of wearable exoskeletons enables highly transparent force interaction operations, effectively improving the feasibility of teleoperation tasks. We adopted a cable-driven spherical parallel wrist exoskeleton (SPWE) which enables it to reduce the volume and enhance operational flexibility; however, significant hysteresis effects and nonlinear friction lead to insufficient force control accuracy. This paper proposes a Data-Observation Hybrid Compensation (DOHC) method, which constructs a Gaussian process-based hysteresis-aware model using physical characteristics. With critical velocity as the judgment criterion, a friction observer is introduced under quasi-static conditions to achieve high-precision compensation across both quasi-static and motion states. Experimental results demonstrate that DOHC significantly improves friction modeling accuracy and joint torque tracking performance in SPWE, and exhibits effective force transparency in haptic rendering experiments, providing reliable technical support for immersive teleoperation tasks.

title: Chaos-Modulated Spiking Neural Networks for Seizure Detection: A Dynamical Systems Approach to Clinical EEG_supp1-3683071.pdf
authors: KONGUVEL ELANGO

title: Real-Time Safe Production Scheduling via Causal Inference and Hybrid-Event Control
authors: Yong Lin, Yadi Lin, Wendi Lin
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Industrial Electronics Society
abstract: This paper proposes a real-time and certifiably safe scheduling framework for cyber-physical manufacturing systems, designed to operate under hybrid discrete–continuous dynamics and deployment-level latency constraints. The approach integrates two core components: (i) a wavelet-invariant causal modeling module that uncovers multiscale dependencies in sensor data while preserving conditional-independence structure under orthonormal wavelet projections, and (ii) a hybrid-event barrier certificate (HRCBC) filter that enforces hard safety constraints across both continuous flows and discrete event triggers such as tool changeovers and emergency halts. Departing from traditional methods that assume smooth dynamics or fixed planning horizons, the architecture fuses offline causal discovery with fast, online, safety-filtered control. Causal graphs are learned via orthonormal wavelet projections and refreshed per shift or upon concept-drift detection. Real-time safety is maintained by solving a convex quadratic program with warm starts and p99 latency under 10 ms. The method is validated on two industrial lines (Site-A: EV-module assembly; Site-B: appliance assembly) and on public synthetic benchmarks (SynTwin-Bench), with ablations and multi-site simulations. Across the reported testbeds, WaIST+HRCBC improves cycle time by up to 28% and operating cost by 20% relative to the incumbent dispatcher; throughout the reported evaluation horizon, no safety violations were recorded by the independent PLC safety layer, while maintaining PLC-compatible latency. The system is deployable on standard industrial gateways and interoperable with MES/PLC via RESTful and OPC UA adapters.

title: Fine-Tuning Open-Source Large Language Models for Cybersecurity and IT Support: A Comparative Study on LLM Fine-Tuning Techniques
authors: Chaithanya Vamshi Sai, Nouh Sabri Elmitwally, Iain Rice, Haitham Mahmoud, Ian Vickers, Xavier Schmoor
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Computer Society
abstract: The focus of Artificial Intelligence (AI) has progressed from exploring whether Large Language Models (LLMs) can transform domain-specific tasks to understanding how to optimise their impact. Advances in Natural Language Processing (NLP) have enabled LLMs to address complex tasks such as question answering, text classification, and translation with notable accuracy and efficiency. Hence, this study evaluates the effectiveness of fine-tuning open-source LLMs, which range from 2 to 9 billion parameters, for cybersecurity and IT support using three advanced techniques: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), and Quantised Low-Rank Adaptation (QLoRA). The results indicate that SFT achieves superior convergence in loss metrics for both tasks, while LoRA and QLoRA significantly reduce GPU memory and computational costs by 99% and 60%, respectively, with comparable performance. Models such as Llama, Mistral, and Phi consistently demonstrated high generalisation across all methods, making them particularly suitable for deployment in resource-constrained environments.

title: Time-Optimal Iterative Learning Planning for Lagrangian Systems and Its Application to Quadcopters
authors: Shuli Lv, Pengda Mao, Chen Min, Li Hong, Yanjun Liu, Quan Quan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Autonomous navigation requires real-time trajectory optimization with limited onboard computational resources, where traditional optimization-based methods often impose heavy computational burdens and require extensive parameter tuning. To achieve transparent navigation results and minimize computational burden, this paper introduces an iterative learning planning (ILP) approach for navigation. By dynamically integrating the control layer into the planning layer, ILP can achieve efficient trajectory optimization through functional iterative learning, providing transparent results that enhance navigation efficiency. This approach significantly improves real-time performance, achieving a time complexity of O (k*n), where k* represents the number of iterations and n represents the number of waypoints. Simulations and quadcopter experiments are conducted to verify the proposed framework. Results show that ILP significantly improves computational efficiency, ensures safe navigation, and maintains robustness against disturbances, demonstrating its potential as a practical solution for real-world autonomous systems.

title: Averaging Control for Time-Varying Energy Management of Lights and HVAC Units
authors: Xiaoting Chen, Simone Baldi, Meijuan Zheng, Ran Tian, Xiao Wang, Da Yan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: One of the major challenges in deploying demand-responsive energy management programs is the design of control-oriented models that explain how the demand is affected by shifting commands (e.g., shifts in the set points of lights, heating, ventilation and air conditioning (HVAC) units). This work proposes an averaging technique for control-oriented demand modeling: the key aspect resides in determining the average thermostatic phases emerging from coupled continuous-discrete demand dynamics. We analytically study stability, optimality, and robustness of averaging-based energy management, and we benchmark its effectiveness against state-of-the-art learning-based energy management. The proposed approach exhibits 3-4 times improved demand-responsive tracking, without compromising user’s flexibility in terms of set points and temperature deviations. Notably, the thermostatic phases corrupt the effectiveness of learning-based energy management, but not of the proposed averaging-based energy management.

title: No-Reference Quality Assessment of 3D Models Represented in Neural Radiance Fields and 3D Gaussian Splattings
authors: Zhengyu Zhang, Yuhang Zhang, Tiantian Zeng, Shishun Tian, Lu Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: The continuous breakthroughs in 3D reconstruction and rendering technologies have enabled synthesized 3D models to achieve exceptional realism. In particular, Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) and 3D Gaussian Splattings (3DGS) have gained significant attention due to their impressive ability to deliver high-quality 3D models. However, research on the quality assessment of NeRF/3DGS models remains an underexplored area, hindering the development of relevant generation, compression, and transmission algorithms. To fill this gap, in this paper, we propose the first no-reference quality assessment metric for NeRF/3DGS models rendered in Processed Video Sequences (PVS). Considering the uniqueness and diversity of distortions introduced in NeRF/3DGS models, the core idea behind the proposed metric is to extract universal quality-aware features that are generalizable across various distortions, rather than targeting a specific one. Specifically, inspired by the fact that spatial distortions typically alter the statistical distributions, we first measure the spatial fidelity of the rendered PVS by analyzing the spatial explicit statistics of textural variation and naturalness. Then, motivated by the ability of implicit energy composition changes to reflect temporal distortions, we propose to evaluate the temporal consistency of the rendered PVS through inter-frame discrepancy energy and multi-frame motion energy in the Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) domain. Finally, the spatial explicit statistics and temporal implicit energy are combined as perceptual features to evaluate the quality of NeRF/3DGS models via Support Vector Regression (SVR). Extensive experimental results on three representative databases demonstrate the superiority of the proposed metric in various aspects, such as predictive accuracy, performance stability, and cross-database generalizability. The source code will be publicly available at https://github.com/ZhengyuZhang96/PVS-3DMQA.

title: Where Prior Learning Can and Can’t Work in Unsupervised Inverse Problems
authors: Benoît Malézieux, Florent Michel, Thomas Moreau, Matthieu Kowalski
abstract:
Linear inverse problems consist of recovering a signal from noisy, incomplete (or compressed) observations in a lower-dimensional space. Many recent methods approach this by learning a prior from data to compensate for the loss of information, typically using paired clean and degraded examples. However, such supervised approaches are not applicable in unsupervised settings, where no ground truth signals are available.

This work focuses on the case where the prior takes the form of a learned dictionary and sparse codes. We provide a theoretical and empirical analysis of the limitations of such unsupervised prior learning. We first show that learning a dictionary is fundamentally unfeasible when observations are obtained through a single measurement operator. Multiple operators make recovery possible, provided they are diverse enough to span the whole signal space.

We further study the role of weak structural priors, either introduced via optimization constraints or deep learning architectures, and empirically demonstrate that they outperform handcrafted priors only when they are well adapted to the inverse problem at hand.

title: Parallel Mechanism-Type Skill-Assist Arm Using a Passive-State Actuator to Aid Movement of Limbs
authors: Kengo Tanaka, Hiroaki Kozuka, Hiroshi Tachiya
abstract: Robotic arms equipped with passive-state actuators to assist beginners in performing upper-limb motions have been developed for tasks such as welding requiring precise positioning. The arms are equipped with position-controlled actuators, one of which can be switched to a passive-state actuator by turning off its excitation. When the passive-state actuator is active, the operator can directly input motion, and the robot assists in positioning along a target trajectory by coordinating with the operator's motion and selectively switching actuator excitations. A three-degree-of-freedom (3-DOF) serial-type arm using the above method has been reported in prior work. However, it exhibited issues such as motion discontinuities caused by inertial effects during excitation switching. To address these problems, this study proposes a lightweight arm based on a parallel mechanism, incorporating torque control at the passive-state actuator to achieve stable assistance. A prototype was fabricated, and experiments were conducted in which the arm assisted subjects—assumed to be beginners—in upper-limb positioning tasks. The experimental results confirmed that the proposed arm effectively assists in positioning and confirms practical feasibility.

title: TmsNet: An Efficient Transformer Network Based on Multi-Scale Shift-Invariance Mechanism for Side-Channel Analysis
authors: Shengtao Tang, Lang Li, Lianrui Deng, Xingqi Yue
abstract: Transformer architectures have shown strong potential for profiling side-channel analysis (SCA), but their high computational complexity and substantial parameter overhead remain major obstacles to practical deployment in resource-constrained scenarios, especially under severe leakage-trace desynchronization. To address this challenge, we propose TmsNet, an efficient Transformer-based architecture tailored for desynchronized side-channel leakage analysis. TmsNet revisits the Transformer backbone by coupling multi-scale leakage representation with shift-aware modeling, thereby reducing model complexity while maintaining strong key-recovery capability under pronounced temporal misalignment. TmsNet is characterized by three key design components. First, it employs a multi-scale feature extraction module with temporal convolution kernels of different receptive fields to jointly capture fine-grained local leakage patterns and longer-range temporal dependencies, thereby reducing the sensitivity of vanilla Transformers to local misalignment. Second, it introduces a residual fusion mechanism to align and aggregate cross-scale features, yielding more robust feature representations under temporal perturbations. Third, it integrates relative positional encoding with Softmax-based normalization, replacing conventional LayerNorm to better preserve subtle leakage contrasts that may be weakened by mean-variance normalization, while mitigating the impact of countermeasure-induced random delays and clock jitter. Extensive evaluations on the desynchronized ASCADf, ASCADr, and TinyPower benchmark datasets show that TmsNet reduces parameter count by up to 78.5% relative to baseline models while maintaining competitive attack performance. Notably, it recovers the secret key using only 48, 42, and 28 attack traces on the three datasets, respectively, demonstrating an effective balance between architectural efficiency and practical profiling SCA capability.

title: LogRMC: Robust Log Anomaly Detection in IoT Systems via Multi-Field Fusion, Contrastive Learning, and Pseudo-Label Refinement
authors: Jun Ma, Qingjun Xiao, Yongchao Zhang, Liukun He, Hang Chen
abstract: In large-scale Internet of Things (IoT) environments, the reliability and security of complex cloud-edge ecosystems critically depend on accurate anomaly detection from system logs. However, most existing methods rely primarily on log templates, often ignoring the diagnostic signals embedded in heterogeneous fields (e.g., timestamps, components, and severity levels). Furthermore, they struggle to learn discriminative representations for rare anomalies due to severe class imbalance, causing models to become biased toward normal patterns. Finally, these approaches often fail to capture the latent structure of unlabeled data, limiting their ability to generalize to unseen anomalies. These limitations render them unreliable in practice. To address the above challenges, we propose LogRMC, a semi-supervised framework for robust log anomaly detection through multi-field log feature fusion, contrastive representation learning, and pseudo-label refinement. First, LogRMC extracts features from diverse log fields and fuses them into unified contextual embeddings that encode inter-field dependencies. Next, it applies contrastive learning on normal samples to learn discriminative and stable representations, thereby improving the separation of normal and anomalous sequence embeddings. To exploit unlabeled data, LogRMC further introduces a cluster-refined pseudo-labeling strategy: it performs density-based clustering on contrastively learned embeddings and applies density ratio estimation within each cluster to generate confidence-aware pseudo-labels. Finally, an attention-based GRU network is trained jointly on labeled and pseudo-labeled log sequences. Extensive experiments on public log datasets demonstrate that LogRMC outperforms state-of-the-art unsupervised and semi-supervised methods, and achieves performance comparable to fully supervised approaches.

title: Environment Division Multiple Access (EDMA): A Feasibility Study via Pinching Antennas
authors: Zhiguo Ding, Robert Schober, H. Vincent Poor
abstract: This paper exploits the dynamic features of wireless propagation environments as the basis for a new multiple access technique, termed environment division multiple access (EDMA). In particular, with the proposed pinching-antenna-assisted EDMA, the multi-user propagation environment is intelligently reconfigured to improve the signal strength at intended receivers and simultaneously suppress multiple-access interference, without requiring complex signal processing, e.g., precoding, beamforming, or multi-user detection. The key to creating a favorable propagation environment is to utilize the capability of pinching antennas to reconfigure line-of-sight (LoS) links, e.g., pinching antennas are placed at specific locations, such that interference links are blocked on purpose. Based on a straightforward choice of the pinching-antenna locations, the ergodic sum-rate gain of EDMA over conventional multiple access and the probability that EDMA achieves a larger instantaneous sum rate than the considered benchmarking scheme are derived in closed form. The obtained analytical results demonstrate the significant potential of EDMA for supporting multi-user communications. Furthermore, pinching antenna location optimization is also investigated, since the locations of the pinching antennas are critical for reconfiguring LoS links and large-scale path losses. Two low-complexity algorithms are developed for uplink and downlink transmission, respectively, and simulation results are provided to show their optimality in comparison to exhaustive searches.
