title: An Improved Attention Gate for U-Net Based Semantic Segmentation of Breast Cancer
authors: Md. Bipul Hossain, Mohamed Abouhashem Salama, Madeline Potter, Mohamed Shaban
abstract: Breast cancer is the most common cancer in the United States where breast cells grow abnormally. Early detection of breast cancer via regular mammography or ultrasound screening may improve the efficacy of the targeted therapeutic treatments and increase the survival rates. In this paper, we introduced an improved attention gate that when integrated in the U-Net, a low-complexity highly accurate semantic segmentation of breast lesions in breast ultrasound was realized with a dice score up to 83.5 % and an Intersection over Union (IoU) up to 85.3 % on the limited size BUSI and BUS-UCLM datasets. The proposed method outperformed standard techniques including the regular U-Net, the SegNet, the DeepLabV3+ and the U-Net with a classical attention gate as well as the-state-of-the-art semantic segmentation techniques for breast cancer in ultrasonography.

title: Finite Element Validation of MCSA-Based Algorithm for Rotor Fault Diagnosis in Low-Power Induction Machines
authors: Gonzalo Fonseca-Yanez, Carlos Madariaga-Cifuentes, Cesar Gallardo, Carlos Galvez-Araya, Juan A. Tapia
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper presents a finite element-based validation framework for a rotor fault detection algorithm using motor current signature analysis (MCSA) in low-power squirrel-cage induction motors. The method focuses on identifying broken rotor bars (BRBs) through FFT-based spectral analysis of stator current signals, combined with a diagnostic table that classifies fault severity based on sideband amplitudes. A calibrated 2D finite element model is used to simulate various fault scenarios. Results at multiple load levels highlighted the limitations of MCSA at low-load conditions due to reduced slip and spectral overlap. Nonetheless, the algorithm demonstrated to accurately detect rotor faults under moderate and high loads. The proposed FEM-based workflow enables robust evaluation of diagnostic algorithms prior to experimental validation and provides insight into the performance boundaries of MCSA in realistic operational settings.

title: An Improved Half-Wavelength Inversion Method Based on Frequency-Dependent Phase-Velocity Sensitivity Kernels
authors: Han Wu, Xiao Zhou, Hongyue Liu
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Surface-wave dispersion inversion is an effective technique for estimating shallow subsurface shear-wave velocity structures at engineering sites. Half-wavelength-based inversion methods can rapidly obtain preliminary models; however, they oversimplify the relationship between Rayleigh-wave phase velocity and shear-wave velocity and fail to capture the frequency-dependent sensitivity of phase velocity to subsurface media, leading to reduced accuracy under complex conditions such as high- or low-velocity interlayers. To address this limitation, frequency-dependent Rayleigh-wave phase velocity sensitivity kernels are introduced to construct weighting coefficients based on kernel integrals, which are incorporated into the half-wavelength inversion framework. Numerical simulations show that the proposed method more accurately recovers shear-wave velocity distributions in complex layered models, significantly improving the reconstruction of anomalous interlayers and overall inversion accuracy, and providing more reliable initial models for subsequent refined inversion.

title: Impact of Ordering on Sparse Tensors
authors: Shae Bolt, Joshua Dennis Booth
abstract: Tensor operations are important to many big data and AI applications. As problem size grows, operations for sparse tensors (e.g., those with relatively few nonzero values) will become important to reduce their memory footprint. Historically, nonzero element reorderings are very important to sparse matrix operations in influencing performance. This paper examines if the sparse tensor operation of MTTKRP is as sensitive by considering three reorderings. These three reorderings are a state-of-the art reordering of Lexi, a good sparse matrix reordering named band<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k$</tex>, and the natural ordering. We demonstrate with real world tensors and randomly generated tensors that reordering does not have a strong impact on performance of sparse MTTKRP. In particular, reorderings seem to matter less with the higher dimension and fewer nonzeros in the sparse tensor.

title: Optimization of the Polyisocyanurate (PIR) Panel Manufacturing Process Using a LiDAR-Based Monitoring System Integrated with Six Sigma Methodology
authors: Rodrigo Azcurra, Fabio Eduardo Maidana, Diego Stalder, Andrea Insfran-Rivarola, Ana Pamela Arevalos
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Highly sensitive industrial processes require precise control to ensure product quality and to reduce operating costs. In the continuous manufacturing of polyisocyanurate (PIR) core sandwich insulation panels, the current manual monitoring of the critical foam expansion time presents variability and generates structural defects, resulting in a loss of up to 9 percent. This study integrated the Six Sigma statistical approach with an advanced technological improvement based on a laser distance sensor and angular control by servomechanism, and developed an automated system for the accurate determination of the contact time during the expansion of the material. Preliminary tests showed that the proposed technology significantly reduces the variability of this critical time, enabling the process to meet demanding specifications and projecting a reduction in material waste to less than 4 percent. These results highlight the technical feasibility and high implementation potential of the developed system in real industrial production lines.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Network Regression for Gate Capacitance Modeling in InGaAs Double-Gate MOSFETs
authors: Longcheng Ye, Hongyu Li, Peigang Li
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Accurate compact modeling of III-V MOSFETs is crucial for next-generation low-power electronics, yet conventional physics-based approaches struggle with the quantum-mechanical complexities of InGaAs gate capacitance. This paper presents a novel neural network-based regression framework for modeling gate capacitance eigenvalues in InGaAs double-gate MOSFETs. We integrate physics-informed feature engineering with multi-layer perceptron networks (MLPNNs), introducing a physics-constrained loss function that eliminates non-physical negative gradients while maintaining high prediction accuracy. TCAD-generated training data encompassing 2,000 device configurations are used to train six MLPNN architectures, with systematic evaluation on eight independent test sets. The optimal Medium (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{2} \times \mathrm{8}$</tex>) architecture achieves RMSE of 0.0549 for the dominant capacitance mode eo, with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{R}^{\mathrm{2}}$</tex> exceeding 0.9996 across all test cases. Comprehensive C-V characteristic comparisons validate the model's capability to capture saturation behavior and voltage-dependent transitions. The proposed framework reduces modeling time from weeks to seconds while ensuring SPICE-compatible differentiability, offering a practical solution for III-V MOSFET compact model development in design technology co-optimization workflows.

title: ROS 2 Hardware Interface for the PincOpen Gripper
authors: Darryle K. Logan, Ian G. Conner, David C. Conner
abstract: Many robotic grippers exist, each designed to meet specific criteria. For research applications, the PincOpen gripper provides an open-source, cost-effective option. This paper presents the assembly, performance testing, and validation of the PincOpen gripper, along with the development of a ROS 2 package, pinc_open_driver, which provides both a ros2_control-based hardware interface and a URDF model that enables seamless integration of the gripper with any ROS-based robotic system. The gripper's functionality is demonstrated on two robotic platforms: a HEBI Robotics X-Series SCARA arm and a Universal Robots UR-3e arm. The pinc_open_driver package is released as open source project.

title: A Review of Research on Multimodal Data Fusion and Fine-Tuning Techniques for Large Models
authors: Shaohua Wang, Jiangfeng Su, Jing Tian, Xiaoqin Peng
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: With the rapid advancement of artificial intelligence technology, single-modal data has become insufficient to meet the demands of intelligent decision-making in complex scenarios, making multimodal data fusion a research hotspot in the AI field. The emergence of large models provides a robust infrastructure for multimodal data processing, while fine-tuning techniques have become the core method for adapting models to specific tasks and enhancing their performance. This paper systematically reviews the fundamental theories and fusion methods of multimodal data, focusing on the development history, architectural innovations, and application scenarios of multimodal large models. It delves into the key technologies, optimization strategies, and evaluation frameworks for fine-tuning large models, while identifying challenges and future trends in multimodal data fusion and large model fine-tuning based on current research status. By synthesizing relevant research outcomes from the past five years (2020-2025), this paper aims to provide a comprehensive reference framework for subsequent studies in this field, thereby advancing the efficient implementation of multimodal large models in practical scenarios.

title: Energy Consumption Minimization via Adaptive Sensing Interval and Beamforming Design in RIS-Assisted ISAC Systems
authors: Haoran Kong, Hao Mei, Haixia Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Focusing on integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems, this research examines how reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RIS) can be employed to enhance system performance. The RIS assists the ISAC BS in serving multiple communication users while detecting multiple sensing targets in which the direct links are blocked. However, existing designs in such scenarios often adopt a uniform sensing interval for all targets, overlooking their heterogeneous sensing-frequency requirements and wasting spectrum, BS energy, and RIS reflection degrees of freedom (DoFs). Consequently, our objective is to minimize the BS's power consumption by simultaneously optimizing the sensing intervals, the beamforming strategy of BS, and the phase-shift configuration of RIS, all while satisfying quality of service (QoS) constraints in both communication and sensing. The ensuing optimization problem is non-convex. We tackle it with a customized algorithm built upon an alternating optimization (AO) procedure, where semidefinite relaxation (SDR) and Gaussian randomization are integrated to recover feasible points. Numerical results verify the superior energy efficiency of the presented method relative to existing benchmarks.

title: SIGMA: Smart Infrastructure Grid Monitoring AI
authors: Ryan J Monemi, Sean Monemi
abstract: This paper presents SIGMA (Smart Infrastructure Grid Monitoring AI), a physics-informed artificial intelligence framework for predicting incipient electrical faults in microgrids. The model computes a real-time fault probability <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p(t)$</tex> from power-quality features: voltage sag (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{S}_{\boldsymbol{v}}$</tex>), total harmonic distortion <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(T_{h}\right)$</tex>, power factor deviation <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(P_{f}\right)$</tex>, and the rates of change of voltage (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$V$</tex>) and current (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$I$</tex>). Using logistic regression core and sliding time windows, SIGMA continuously estimates fault likelihood in a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 2 ~ V}$</tex> DC microgrid testbed. An optional Kalman filter improves noise rejection and residual extraction, and ensemble extensions are discussed for future work. Results from simulated and measured data show that SIGMA predicts faults <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx \mathbf{1 2}$</tex> s before onset with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\geq 92 \%$</tex> detection accuracy and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<0.1$</tex> false alarms / h. The approach bridges classical reliability analysis and modern AI-based predictive maintenance for resilient microgrids.

title: Deep Learning Based Threshing Detection for Rice Combine Harvesters
authors: Jing Xu, Meng Tan
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: With the rapid development of precision agriculture and intelligent agricultural machinery, online evaluation of threshing quality has become a key link in the intelligent upgrading of rice combine harvesters. Aiming at the problems of small grain targets, dense distribution, heavy dust, and strong interference from metal reflections in the threshing chamber, this study proposes an efficient threshing visual recognition model based on M-YOLOv8-EB. By introducing the MPDIoU loss function to improve the accuracy of bounding box regression and embedding BiFormer dynamic sparse attention and EfficientDet-BiFPN to achieve lightweight multi-scale fusion, the number of parameters is significantly reduced. Experimental results show that the Precision, Recall, F1 score, and mAP of M-YOLOv8-EB reach <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$96.71 \%, 93.82 \%, 95.24 \%$</tex>, and 98.23 %, respectively, which are improvements of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5.66 \%, 5.50 \%, 5.58 \%$</tex>, and 3.28 % over the original YOLOv8. This study proposes an M-YOLOv8-EB model suitable for the extreme environment of the threshing chamber, which addresses the problem of insufficient detection accuracy for small grain targets under heavy dust and metal reflection interference. Experiments verify the superior performance of the model under real threshing working conditions, and the method provides a feasible technical solution for online evaluation of threshing quality and intelligent control of the drum in rice combine harvesters.

title: CNN-Based Detection of Bearing Race Defects from Variable Reluctance Sensor Signals
authors: Collin Daly, Rami J. Haddad
abstract: This paper presents a non-intrusive framework for detecting and classifying bearing outer-race defects using the existing inductive magnetic reluctance (MR) gear-tooth sensor signal available in equipment such as positive-displacement flowmeters operating in hazardous locations. Rather than installing accelerometers or other added instrumentation, often impractical in HazLoc due to certification and retrofit constraints, we exploit fault-induced amplitude and phase disturbances in the near-sinusoidal tooth-passage waveform. We transform 20 sec recordings into time–frequency spectrogram images and classify bearing conditions using transfer-learned CNNs (GoogLeNet and ResNet-50). The novelty is the repurposing of an already-installed MR speed/flow sensor for bearing diagnostics in HazLoc settings, coupled with a spectrogram-to-CNN transfer-learning pipeline tailored to MR tooth-passage signals. Results demonstrate high classification accuracy across multiple defect conditions and operating speeds.

title: A Layer-by-Layer Backpropagation Approach for Image Classification
authors: José Luis Vázquez Noguera, Carlos U. Valdez, Julio César Mello-Román, Marvin M. Aguero, José Colbes, Sebastián A. Grillo
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper proposes a methodology to apply backpropagation to individual layers of a neural network rather than to the entire network simultaneously. This approach seeks to avoid overfitting by preventing all network parameters from being adjusted simultaneously. We evaluated the proposed method on 10 binary classification datasets with LeNet, AlexNet, and VGG-16 architectures, conducting tests for the first 10 epochs. The results show that the proposed method tends to converge faster than the original backpropagation. In addition, the proposed method tends to avoid local minima of the error, whereas the original backpropagation fails despite the use of a standard optimizer. Crucially, the proposed technique demonstrates significant performance gains in some datasets, with accuracy differences reaching up to 25 % superior compared to the best results for the original backpropagation.

title: A Unified Testing Framework for ADAS and Automated Vehicles: Synthesis of Methodologies and Regulatory Landscapes
authors: Hayleyesus Alemayehu, Arman Sargolzaei
abstract: Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) and automated vehicles (AVs) are increasingly deployed in safetycritical environments, yet current testing and verification (T&V) practices remain fragmented across safety, performance, and compliance requirements. This paper proposes a unified T&V framework that connects scenario-based evaluation with regulatory and standards-driven benchmarks. The framework represents tests using scene vectors and scene matrices, selects evaluation parameters through an assertion structure, and verifies each selected parameter against three assertion values (lower bound, upper bound, and reference target) derived from regulations, standards, and design specifications. To quantify verification outcomes beyond binary (i.e., pass/fail), a normalized verification cost function based on a Program Evaluation and Review Technique (PERT) probability density formulation is introduced to produce continuous scores in [0, 1], where zero indicates requirement violation and values closer to one indicate stronger compliance and performance relative to the reference target. The proposed framework is demonstrated using an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) case study based on real-world driving data, illustrating how safety constraints, such as minimum safe gap and speed limits, and performance objectives, like desired velocity tracking and desired spacing, can be evaluated simultaneously within a single mathematical and regulatory-aligned workflow.

title: Adaptive Load Balancing for Multi-Tier LLM Inference with Dynamic Model Routing
authors: Tejas Pravinbhai Patel, Chaitanya Kulkarni, Sandeep Shivam, Amit Kumar Padhy, Viswanathan Ranganathan, Siva Rama Krishna Varma Bayyavarapu
abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) inference systems face critical challenges in balancing latency, cost, and quality across heterogeneous GPU infrastructure. Existing approaches rely on static routing policies or simple load balancing, resulting in suboptimal resource utilization, high tail latencies, and excessive operational costs. This paper presents an adaptive load balancing framework for multi-tier LLM inference that dynamically routes requests based on real-time system state, request characteristics, and learned performance patterns. Our approach employs reinforcement learning to jointly optimize latency, cost, and Service Level Objective (SLO) compliance across edge, mid-tier, and cloud GPU resources. Experimental evaluation on a realistic workload of 5,000 requests demonstrates that our adaptive router reduces average latency by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 1. 1 8 \%}$</tex> and total operational cost by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 0. 0 8 \%}$</tex> compared to round-robin baseline, while achieving 12.02 percentage points fewer SLO violations. The system achieves these improvements through intelligent tier assignment that matches request complexity to appropriate model capacity.

title: Dynamic Anchors for High Precision and Coverage Explanations - Preliminary Results
authors: Supreeth Suresh, Suresh Muknahallipatna
abstract: Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) provides insights into the decisions of black-box models. Anchors offer highprecision human-interpretable rules in the form of simple if-then conditions over input features. Classical anchors compute discrete instance-wise rules via bandit-guided beam search without learning across instances, making them local interpretation algorithms. We propose Dynamic Anchors, a reinforcement learning (RL) approach that refines anchor-generated rules to optimize precision and coverage. Dynamic Anchors generate instance- and class-level rules that provide local interpretations and global summaries. Preliminary results on explaining black-box model classifiers with tabular datasets showed that Dynamic Anchors yield instance explanations on par with classical anchors and also offer globally relevant explanations.

title: Triple Band Graphene Based Terahertz Antenna With Triangular Slot for Biomedical Applications
authors: Naman Prasad, Rohan Prasad, Poonam Namdeo, Biswajeet Mukherjee, Mridula Gupta
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: A triple band Graphene as patch radiator is proposed for Terahertz frequency range which can be used for communication and biosensing applications. Graphene is used as conducting patch due to its high conductivity and supports Surface Plasmon Polaritons (SPPs). Proposed Antenna incorporates a triangular slot on radiating graphene patch excited through line feeding. Silicon Dioxide is deployed as substrate due to its efficient and retrospective dielectric properties at Terahertz frequencies which often offers low loss. The proposed antenna operates in triple bands from 1.12<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.46 \text{THz}, 2.5-3.1 \text{THz}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.7-4 \text{THz}$</tex>. The simulated radiation efficiency is 93 %. The proposed antenna is used for detection of skin cancer cell deploying Second Order Debye model. The reflection coefficient of proposed structure is simulated for healthy and cancerous cells to determine the impedance mismatch due to change in effective permittivity. This indicates its potential in non-invasive early-stage cancer cell detection.

title: Simulation and Experimental Validation of AlGaN/GaN HFET Electrical Characteristics
authors: Jeshua Stam, Md Ataul Mamun, Vitaliy Avrutin
abstract: AlGaN/GaN heterojunction field-effect transistors (HFETs) have emerged as a key technology for high-power, high-frequency applications due to their wide bandgap, high electron mobility, and large breakdown voltage. In this work, a simulation model of a GaN HFET was developed, and a corresponding transistor was fabricated. Measurements including Hall effect characterization, contact resistivity extraction, and currentvoltage (I-V) tests were performed and compared with simulation results to validate model accuracy. 2DEG simulation agreed with rated wafer characteristics, yielding <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\leq 5 \%$</tex> error for carrier concentration, electron mobility, and sheet resistance. The simulation model features variable parameters including gate length, work function, contact resistivity, and surface passivation to optimize future devices.

title: Proposal of an Intelligent Irrigation Model Based on Machine Learning Applied to Smart Farming 5.0
authors: Marco Fernández B., Héctor Kaschel C.
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: The following paper proposes an intelligent irrigation model based on machine learning for agriculture 5.0. It reviews relevant articles in this area and proposes an irrigation model based on linear regression to predict crop moisture as a function of temperature, soil <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{p H}$</tex> and irrigation time. Based on the proposed model, an algorithm is developed to generate graphs of the relevant variables and to predict soil moisture based on the proposed linear regression model.

title: Research on Sea Surface Wind Fields Based on Generative Adversarial Networks
authors: Dingqi Zhang, Qifeng Jiang, Weibin Zhang, Licheng Gu, Junlin Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Sea surface wind speed serves as the primary driver of ocean circulation and a key regulator of air-sea fluxes. Highresolution mapping of sea surface winds is crucial for understanding mesoscale and sub-mesoscale ocean processes and supports a wide range of offshore engineering applications. However, existing mainstream sea surface wind products often fail to meet the practical needs of small-scale or localized areas. To address this gap, this study employs a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) to conduct downscaling reconstruction of satellite-based fused sea surface wind products. This model successfully outputs a high resolution wind field with a spatial resolution of 12.5 km by processing the global HY sea surface wind speed product with a spatial resolution of 25 km as input. This achieves a doubling of spatial resolution and a quadrupling of the number of corresponding pixels. The model demonstrates strong performance in refining spatial details of wind fields, with generated data showing high consistency with actual observations in both spatial patterns and statistical similarity metrics. Validation against buoy data yields a root mean square error (RMSE) of 1.10, indicating a significant improvement over the original product. This work provides a valuable reference for highresolution sea surface wind estimation and supports further advances in regional marine studies and applications.

title: Evaluation of Modulation Techniques in MIMO-VLC Systems: Mitigating Ambient Light Interference for Enhanced Performance
authors: Javier Guaña-Moya, Milton Román Cañizares, Laura Romero, Pablo Palacios Játiva, Cesar A. Azurdia-Meza, David Zabala-Blanco, Ismael Sotoll, Maria Camila Reyes
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Visible Light Communication (VLC) can be substantially improved by using <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \times 2$</tex> Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO) links, which increase both throughput and coverage; however, the presence of ambient illumination remains a major performance limiter. In this work, we analyze three modulation options-Non-Return-to-Zero (NRZ), Return-to-Zero (RZ), and Quadrature Phase-Shift Keying (QPSK)-for a MIMO-VLC transmission and benchmark them against a Single-Input SingleOutput (SISO) baseline. Trace-based OptiSystem simulations indicate that MIMO increases the noise-free reach from 1.12 km (SISO) to 1.45 km when using QPSK, and it reduces the Bit Error Rate (BER) at short ranges by roughly a factor of two. When two ambient interferers are present (fluorescent lamp and white LED), the MIMO reach decreases by 13.6 % but still remains 150 m longer than the SISO link. Overall, NRZ and QPSK show the highest robustness, achieving BER <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<10^{-3}$</tex> with the smallest power penalty. The results highlight that compact MIMO combined with spectrally efficient modulation enables long-reach VLC under practical lighting conditions.

title: How Much Attention is Enough?
authors: Betelihem Wereta, Ravanasamudram N. Uma
abstract: In image classification applications where false positive rates and false negative rates do not determine profit margins, or life or death outcomes, it may be beneficial to trade accuracy for time. This is especially useful in resource-constrained environments such as in edge computing. One such application is classifying images taken by the Mars Science Laboratory (MSL) Curiosity Rover into one of 19 science and engineering-relevant classes. In most of these images taken by the Curiosity Rover, the object/region of interest, for example “sun” or “light-toned veins” occupies only a small portion of the image. We posit that hard attention strategies are suitable for this purpose and show that, surprisingly, focusing on very few regions of the image is sufficient to evoke a comparable performance in standard convolutional neural network (CNN) models such as ResNet50.

title: $K_{5}$-Factor in Random Graphs*
authors: Weiwei Zhang, Huiping Qi
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: For a graph <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$G$</tex> with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$n$</tex> vertices, where 5 divides <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$n$</tex>, a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$K_{5}$</tex>-factor is a subgraph of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$G$</tex>. consisting of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$n / 5$</tex> vertex disjoint <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$K_{5}$</tex>. We consider the minimal probability <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p=p(n)$</tex>, for which a random graph <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$G=G(n, p)$</tex> almost surely contains a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$K_{5}$</tex>-factor. In this paper, we prove that for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p=O\left(n^{-0.35}\right)$</tex>, the random graph <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$G(n, p)$</tex> almost surely contains a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$K_{5}$</tex>-factor.

title: Network Covert Channel Detection Algorithm Based on Convolutional Neural Network
authors: Liu Lin, Chen Gang
publish: 2025 2nd World Conference on Computer and Information Security (WCCIS)
abstract: Network covert channels, as a major challenge in the field of network security, achieve unauthorized information transmission through legitimate system resources or protocol mechanisms, and have high concealment and harmfulness. Traditional detection methods are inadequate when dealing with new and complex covert channels. Therefore, this study aims to utilize the deep learning ability of CNN to enhance the detection efficiency and generalization ability of covert channels. This paper first proposes a color image transformation method for network traffic data based on three-dimensional feature mining. On the basis of preprocessing the network traffic data, this method selects features by calculating information gain, Pearson correlation coefficient, and Kendall correlation coefficient, deletes invalid features, and reduces the dimension of the data. Then, a network covert channel detection method based on convolutional neural networks was proposed. This method designs convolutional neural networks, incorporates the CBAM attention mechanism, integrates the two dimensions of spatial attention and channel attention to extract image features, classifies the network traffic data converted into color images, and realizes the detection of network covert channels. The experimental results show that the proposed model can effectively detect the covert channels of the network. The average accuracy rate, precision rate and recall rate are 96.42%, 97.82% and 96.16% respectively, which are superior to the traditional methods.

title: Personalized Real-Time Financial Expense Categorization AI Using OCR and Retrieval-Augmented Generation
authors: Dhivya Guru, Senthil raj Subramaniam
abstract: This paper presents a personalized, AI-powered expense categorization system designed to automatically classify individual expenditures in real time, based on scanned receipts and secure user profile data. The system integrates Optical Character Recognition (OCR) for accurate extraction of merchant, date, and transaction amounts from both physical and digital receipts, and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to perform context-aware categorization using a user's historical spending patterns, preferred categories, and financial goals. A multilingual, voice-enabled interface ensures accessibility across diverse demographics, while an adaptive learning mechanism refines accuracy over time through user feedback. The personalized dashboard delivers instant visualizations of categorized expenses, budget adherence, and spending trends, enabling proactive financial decision-making. This work demonstrates the potential of combining OCR, RAG, and adaptive learning to deliver a scalable, user-centric solution for transparent and efficient personal financial management.

title: Real-Time Monitoring Technology for Sensitive Data Leakage Risk in Microservice APIs
authors: Jinhe Wang, Shenqiang Xiao, Jiaquan Li
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: This study addresses the challenge of real-time monitoring and protection of sensitive data in the complex API call chains of microservice architectures. It proposes a real-time monitoring technology for sensitive data leakage risk targeting microservice APIs, aiming to establish a complete closed loop from fine-grained data perception to intelligent risk decision-making. The approach designs a lightweight tracing probe based on Java Agent bytecode enhancement, which constructs a real-time dynamic data flow spectrum through dynamic taint marking and cross-service label propagation. Furthermore, a Bi-directional Long Short-Term Memory (Bi-LSTM) model, integrated with an attention mechanism, is proposed to model and quantify the risk of data flow sequences. Finally, an adaptive strategy response engine based on Deep Q-Learning (DQN) is introduced to dynamically optimize the alerting strategy. Experimental results show that the proposed system achieves precision, recall, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{F 1}$</tex> scores of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$88.7 \%, 91.3 \%$</tex>, and 89.9 %, respectively, and an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$F 1$</tex> score of 85.2% for detecting unknown anomalous traffic, significantly outperforming traditional rule-based and log-scanning methods. The system incurs low performance overhead with a resource consumption increase of less than 5 %, and an end-to-end processing delay of 210 milliseconds, demonstrating good stability and scalability. The proposed framework enables accurate, realtime, and adaptive monitoring of sensitive data leakage risks in microservice environments, with high production readiness.

title: TopoSizing: An LLM-aided Framework of Topology-based Understanding and Sizing for AMS Circuits
authors: Ziming Wei, Zichen Kong, Yuan Wang, David Z. Pan, Xiyuan Tang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Analog and mixed-signal (AMS) circuit design remains challenging due to the shortage of high-quality data and the difficulty of embedding domain knowledge into automated flows. Traditional black-box optimization achieves sampling efficiency but lacks circuit understanding, which often causes evaluations to be wasted in low-value regions of the design space. In contrast, learning-based methods embed structural knowledge but are case-specific and costly to retrain. Recent attempts with large language models (LLMs) show potential, yet they often rely on manual intervention, limiting generality and transparency. We propose TopoSizing, an end-to-end framework that performs robust circuit understanding directly from raw netlists and translates this knowledge into optimization gains. Our approach first applies graph algorithms to organize circuits into a hierarchical device–module–stage representation. LLM agents then execute an iterative hypothesis–verification–refinement loop with built-in consistency checks, producing explicit annotations. Verified insights are integrated into Bayesian optimization (BO) through LLM-guided initial sampling and stagnation-triggered trust-region updates, improving efficiency while preserving feasibility. On four real-world AMS circuits (OTA, FC-OTA, SACMP, LDO) in a 55-nm CMOS process, TopoSizing achieves 100% correctness in circuit understanding and parameter assignment. In constraint-satisfaction tasks, it delivers 1.4×–4.8× higher sample efficiency and 1.2×–3.5× faster runtime compared with strong baselines, while requiring 2–4× fewer LLM calls than prior LLM-based frameworks. Ablation studies confirm that reliable circuit understanding is essential for these gains.

title: Data-Driven Identification and Cooperative Parameters Optimization for Negative Damping Hydropower Units in Ultra-Low Frequency Oscillation Suppression
authors: Kai Liao, Hongxun Li, Jian Sun, Lin Li, Jianwei Yang, Zhengyou He
abstract: This paper proposes a data-driven identification method and a cooperative parameters optimization strategy for hydropower units with negative damping to mitigate excessive loss of primary frequency regulation capability during classical ultra-low frequency oscillation (ULFO) suppression. A data-driven joint time-frequency analysis method is employed to extract damping characteristics from ULFO dissipated energy, enabling the precise identification of units with negative damping without relying on detailed models and parameters. Subsequently, the effects of variations in the water hammer time constant and governor PID parameters on damping enhancement and primary frequency regulation capability loss are analyzed. Accordingly, a multi-unit damping enhancement target allocation mechanism is developed to facilitate the cooperative optimization of PID parameters for units with negative damping, mitigating excessive primary frequency regulation capability loss. The Pareto-optimal PID parameters solution is explored by heuristic algorithm. Finally, numerical simulations are performed on the 10-machine 39-bus system and a practical hydropower-dominated CJL power system. The results verify that the proposed method accurately identifies negative-damping units. At the same time, it effectively suppresses oscillations while retaining improved primary frequency regulation performance.

title: Novel Financial, Economic, and Management Framework for Sustainable Energy Supply Chain Transformation: Consumer Behavior Anomaly Detection and Risk Analytics
authors: Lu Liu, Yixuan Cheng, Aliasghar Baziar, Shakila Basheer
abstract: The transformation of sustainable energy supply chains requires integrated financial, economic, and management frameworks capable of addressing operational complexity, market risks, and evolving consumer behaviors. The increasing deployment of interconnected microgrids (MGs) enhances efficiency, resilience, and sustainability; however, it introduces significant coordination challenges across decentralized energy systems. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a comprehensive framework that combines blockchain-enabled energy management with advanced consumer behavior anomaly detection and risk analytics. The proposed approach leverages blockchain technology to facilitate secure, transparent, and decentralized communication among MGs, reducing operational inefficiencies and mitigating financial misconduct risks. A priority-based energy exchange mechanism is developed to optimize power transactions across interconnected MGs, while an incentive-driven pricing contract encourages economically efficient energy consumption patterns. To further strengthen the financial and operational robustness of the system, anomaly detection techniques are incorporated to identify irregular consumer behavior, enabling proactive risk management and fraud prevention within the energy supply chain. In addition, uncertainties associated with renewable generation and demand variability are modeled using a stochastic framework based on differential equations, supporting more informed financial and operational decision-making. The resulting problem is formulated as a mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) model and solved through a blockchain-integrated optimization algorithm. A multi-sector test system, including residential, commercial, and critical infrastructure MGs, is used to validate the framework.

title: System-Level Design of an End-to-End Neural Framework for Human Activity Recognition Using FMCW Radar
authors: Pung Hok, Runjin Liu, Yiqun Peng, YiPeng Ding
abstract: The growing demand for non-invasive sensing technologies has spurred increasing interest in radar-based gesture and activity recognition for applications in smart homes, healthcare, and human–computer interaction. Most existing approaches adopt a two-stage pipeline, combining traditional Fourier transform-based preprocessing with deep neural classifiers. In this work, we propose a complex-valued end-to-end neural architecture that directly processes raw frequency-modulated continuous wave (FMCW) radar signals, eliminating the need for handcrafted preprocessing. The proposed framework integrates a Fourier Multilayer Perceptron (Fourier MLP) with Fourier-based weight initialization to jointly learn multidimensional range, Doppler, and angle-of-arrival (AoA) representations while compressing redundant information through a dimensionality contraction layer. A Feature Enhancement Module based on complex convolutions further refines spectral–spatial representations by enhancing both high-and low-frequency components, and a Complex-Valued Self-Attention Encoder captures spatial–temporal dependencies across frames. Experimental results show that the proposed model achieves recognition accuracies of 98.54%, 99.58%, and 99.72% on hand gesture (HGR), arm gesture (AGR), and human activity (HAR) recognition tasks, respectively. Beyond its high accuracy and computational efficiency, the framework exhibits strong generalization to unseen users, thereby demonstrating superior robustness for real-world consumer applications.

title: ThyroDiff-X: A Diffusion-Driven Explainable AI Framework for Early Thyroid Cancer Detection Using Smart Health Biomarkers
authors: Jing Yang, Prakhar Consul, Zaffar Ahmed Shaikh, Hela Elmannai, Lip Yee Por, Hao Lv, Longquan Lou
abstract: Smart health devices are playing an increasingly vital role in early diagnosis and personalized care, especially for complex conditions like thyroid cancer. Yet, making sense of subtle, time-dependent patterns in patient data remains challenging— particularly when diagnostic accuracy, transparency, and real-time responsiveness all matter. To tackle this, we introduce the Attention-driven Quantum-Explainable Unified Block with integrated Diffusion Modeling (ThyroDiff-X). ThyroDiff-X brings together three complementary components to model evolving clinical trends. This integration enables the model to capture longitudinal biomarker evolution and suppress noise through diffusion-based temporal reconstruction, explaining its strong performance gains over static and single-visit diagnostic approaches. Specifically, quantum-inspired sinusoidal encoding enriches feature representation, a context-aware diffusion module (Enhanced-DDIM) reconstructs temporal biomarker trajectories using visit-level memory and SHAP-informed clinical context, and multi-head attention adaptively focuses on the most relevant visits. A built-in SHAP layer adds interpretability by highlighting the key features behind each prediction. To optimize performance, the model leverages MSAMO-VCS—a hybrid tuning approach that combines wide-ranging parameter exploration with precise local refinement. The model was rigorously tested across four diverse datasets—SmartThyro-300, UCI Thyroid, Sick-Euthyroid, and THYROID-MVTS—where it consistently outperformed leading baselines, achieving up to 99.5% accuracy, a log loss as low as 0.04, and the highest Quantum Explainability Confidence Rate (QXCR). Its successful deployment on a Jetson Nano board confirmed its real-time readiness and efficiency under resource-constrained settings.

title: Analysis and Improvement of Torque Characteristics in Dual-Airgap Permanent Magnet Motor for Aircraft Propulsion
authors: Han Xue, Zhuoran Zhang, Qiuyu Lin, Nicola Bianchi, Huamin Gao
abstract: In this article, the torque characteristics of a dual-airgap yokeless-stator permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) for aircraft propulsion are analyzed and improved. The motor topology and mechanical structure are first introduced, and both the dual-airgap PMSM (DM) and a conventional outer-rotor PMSM (OM) are optimized. Through theoretical analysis and finite-element analysis (FEA), the flux density distributions and torque characteristics of the DM and OM are comparatively investigated considering dual-airgap electromagnetic effects. The results indicate that the DM achieves higher torque density than the OM; however, its torque ripple may increase due to its specialized topology and mechanical structure. To mitigate torque ripple in dual-airgap PMSMs, two approaches are proposed and analyzed. First, the dual-rotor phase displacement method effectively suppresses torque ripple by properly adjusting the phase displacement angle. Second, an alternative stator mechanical structure employing stator holders at the slot openings is introduced, which provides higher output torque and lower torque ripple, albeit at the expense of increased eddy current losses in the stator holders. Finally, a prototype dual-airgap yokeless-stator PMSM is fabricated and experimentally tested. The measured results show good agreement with the simulation results, thereby validating the analysis and the proposed methodology.

title: Two Fast Erasure Decoding Algorithms for Reed–Solomon Codes Based on LCH-FFT
authors: Chao Chen, Sian-Jheng Lin, Nianqi Tang, Yunghsiang S. Han, Suihua Cai, Leilei Yu, Zhongwei Li, Baoming Bai, Bo Bai
abstract: Based on a recently proposed fast Fourier transform by Lin, Chung, and Han, this paper presents two fast erasure decoding algorithms for Reed–Solomon (RS) codes over binary extension fields of length N and dimension K. The first algorithm applies to low-rate RS codes (i.e., K/N ≤ 0:5) and achieves a complexity of O(N log K). The second algorithm applies to high-rate RS codes (i.e., K/N ≥ 0:5) and achieves a complexity of O(N log(N – K)). Compared to recent state-of-the-art algorithms, both proposed algorithms achieve the best complexity, resulting in significant throughput improvements in Single Instruction Multiple Data (SIMD) based simulations. Besides yielding new fast algorithms for RS codes, this paper also presents a new interpolation formula, as well as related results, which may be of independent interest.

title: An Experimental Characterization of Atmospheric Turbulence Effects on Millimeter Wave Propagation in a Controlled Environment
authors: Shawn Sheng, Omkar Pradhan, Roni Goldshmid, Simone Tanelli, Ken Cooper, Azita Emami
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This paper introduces a novel method for experimentally characterizing atmospheric turbulence effects on millimeter-wave propagation in a controlled laboratory environment. A large fan array wind tunnel with 36x36 individually programmable dual-fan units is used to create turbulent flows over a controlled temperature gradient. A W-band (95.4GHz) radar is used to propagate electromagnetic energy through this turbulent flow, while meteorological parameters such as temperature, humidity, and wind speed are measured by co-located sensors. Phase noise self-cancellation and vibration isolation techniques are used to observe scintillation down to millimeter length scales. The effect of temperature gradients and different mean wind speeds are shown to increase and shift the measured power spectrum of the radar signal within the inertial subrange and dissipation regions of turbulence. Results from this experimental setup are shown to be consistent and repeatable across multiple days. This setup can be used to (i) investigate novel techniques for remote sensing of atmospheric turbulence, (ii) develop statistical channel models for millimeter-wave communication systems, and (iii) expand on existing scintillation theory.

title: Fully Printed Electrically Small Antenna-on-Package for Wireless Electrocardiogram System
authors: Jiajun Li, Sakandar Rauf, Rana M. Bilal, Atif Shamim
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Low-cost and easy-to-wear wireless electrocardiogram (ECG) systems are in demand for personal health monitoring. An antenna is an integral part of such wireless ECG systems, requiring careful design to demonstrate decent bandwidth, gain, and radiation pattern performance on a lossy human body, while maintaining a compact size and low cost. In this paper, we present a fully printed and electrically small (ES) Antenna-on-Package (AoP) for a wearable and compact wireless ECG system. The proposed AoP is a modified form of an inverted-F antenna (IFA), which has been designed on a compact cylinder with both the radius and the height equal to 12 mm (ka=0.67). In addition to being the antenna substrate, the 3D printed cylinder also acts as the package for the ECG printed circuit board (PCB). By fully exploiting the space with AoP design and carefully bending the radiator around the package, the fully printed ES antenna achieved a maximum gain of 0.3 dBi and an -10 dB impedance bandwidth of 230 MHz for the Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) band in the presence of a lossy human body. On-body measurements of the proposed ECG system have been conducted with both the proposed antenna as well as a commercial chip antenna. The measured results indicate that with the proposed antenna, the read range is enhanced by 10 times in the forward direction (Theta=0°), as compared to the commercial chip antenna. The maximum read range of 150 m has been obtained at an angle of theta=-67.5°. The proposed antenna demonstrates excellent performance as compared to similar electrically small antennas that report on-body performances, despite its realization through low-cost printing technologies. The antenna has been integrated with the compact ECG system and it has been tested on a volunteer’s chest. The results have been compared with a commercial ECG system and an excellent agreement has been observed between the acquired ECG results from both systems, indicating that the developed low-cost and compact system is suitable for practical applications.

title: Towards Robust Optimization-Based Autonomous Dynamic Soaring With a Fixed-Wing UAV
authors: Marvin Harms, Jaeyoung Lim, David Rohr, Friedrich Rockenbauer, Nicholas Lawrance, Roland Siegwart
abstract: Dynamic soaring is a flying technique to exploit the energy available in wind shear layers, enabling potentially un limited flight without the need for internal energy sources. We propose a framework for autonomous dynamic soaring with a fixed-wing UAV. The framework makes use of an explicit representation of the wind field and a classical approach for guidance and control of the UAV. Robustness to wind field estimation error is achieved by constructing point-wise robust reference paths for dynamic soaring and the development of a robust path following controller for the fixed-wing UAV. Wind estimation and path tracking performance are validated with real flight tests to demonstrate robust path-following in real wind conditions. In simulation, we demonstrate robust dynamic soaring flight subject to varied wind conditions, estimation errors and disturbances. Together, our results strongly indicate the ability of the proposed framework to achieve autonomous dynamic soaring flight in wind shear.

title: Attack Path Planning in 5G-ICPS Penetration Testing: Leveraging TGNN and DRL for Large-scale Network
authors: Feiyang Li, Hao Hu, Yingchang Jiang, Yixiao Peng, Yongwei Wang, Yuchen Zhang, Yuling Liu
abstract: Path planning constitutes a critical component of penetration testing for 5G-ICPS networks. The diversity of interfaces and protocols necessitates deep analysis of vulnerability exploitation methods and cross-protocol combination strategies, significantly increasing attack path planning complexity. Furthermore, dynamic network slice configurations and physical-information coupling effects drive continuous topological evolution, causing state-space explosion and challenging path planning under uncertainty with incomplete information. To address these issues, we construct a temporal attack graph modeling 5G-ICPS attack processes, and design a GraphSAGE-based environment representation encoder. This encoder undergoes multi-tiered self-supervised pre-training, employing node-level and graph-level training to encode diverse reinforcement learning environments across attack scenarios into fixed-dimensional vector representations. This achieves decoupling from underlying topology, vulnerability specifics, and security configurations, effectively mitigating state-space explosion in large-scale networks. Subsequently, we cluster highly similar vulnerabilities and filter invalid attack actions using three typical 5G-ICPS attack constraints, compressing the agent’s exploration space. Especially, we design a customized reward function that dynamically incentivizes/penalizes actions based on compromised assets. Experimental results demonstrate significant improvements: penetration testing invalid action rates decrease from 22.3% to 7.5%, while average steps to achieve attack targets reduce by >54%. These advancements effectively reduce penetration testing costs and increase attack success rates.

title: RIS-Assisted Cascaded AF-DF Relaying for IoV: Performance Analysis and PSO-Based Power Allocation
authors: Baofeng Ji, Wenjuan Chai, Kaipeng Sun, Boyuan Li, Zijian Li, Tony Q.S. Quek
abstract: Driven by the escalating demand for extensive coverage and ultra-reliable communication in the Internet of Vehicles (IoV), conventional single-transmission technologies frequently encounter a severe ”bottleneck effect” in long-distance and heavily obstructed environments. To break through this limitation, this paper investigates a Reconfigurable Intelligent Surface (RIS)-assisted cascaded amplify-and-forward (AF) and decode-andforward (DF) relay system over independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) Nakagami-m fading channels. The proposed architecture innovatively exploits the synergistic advantages of cascaded active relays (providing initial power amplification via AF and terminal signal regeneration via DF) alongside the ”passive array gains” from the RIS, thereby effectively overcoming the severe ”double path loss” inherent in passive links while suppressing accumulated noise. Addressing the challenges of prohibitive pilot overhead and latency associated with acquiring instantaneous channel state information (CSI) in highly dynamic IoV environments, we propose a statistical CSI-based adaptive power allocation algorithm utilizing particle swarm optimization (PSO). By jointly optimizing the transmit power allocation among the source node, the AF relay, and the DF relay, the proposed algorithm efficiently tackles the intricate non-convex optimization problem subject to a total power constraint. Theoretically, we derive exact closed-form expressions for the system outage probability (OP) and average channel capacity, establishing a rigorous theoretical evaluation framework. Extensive simulation results not only validate the accuracy of the theoretical analysis but also demonstrate that the proposed PSO strategy effectively exploits spatial diversity gains and RIS passive beamforming gains. The findings reveal that the scheme significantly enhances transmission reliability and system robustness in complex propagation environments, substantiating its application potential for Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS).

title: RoQUEST : A Robust QUESTion Classification Framework in Consumer Electronics Domain
authors: Sovan Kumar Sahoo, Asif Ekbal
abstract: Question-answering (QA) has been a crucial NLP (Natural Language Processing) task for a long time. As an umbrella term, QA has multiple sub-tasks under its hood. Question classification (QC) is one of the most important sub-tasks of any QA pipeline. By providing hints about the classes or types of incoming questions and/or potential answers, a QC module enables the QA system to implement appropriate approaches/strategies to handle different types of question to produce better answers. Although QA systems have evolved significantly over time in the last few decades and witnessed a paradigm shift from earlier IR (Information Retrieval) based systems to more recent RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) based systems, there are very few QC frameworks available in the research community that can match and support such wider range of QA systems. Moreover, a robust QA pipeline must be able to answer any type of question, which is essential in a QA system, regardless of the paradigm. However, most of the research works and datasets available in the literature have been proposed considering QA systems consisting of only factoid questions and the QC frameworks for those systems supposed to help in performance improvement by predicting its subtypes (also referred as answer types in the literature). In our present work, we propose a QC framework to bridge these gaps. Our proposed QC framework consists of three types of questions, viz. factoid, confirmation, and descriptive questions in the consumer electronics domain. Consumer electronics is an important domain where the users have plenty of questions that could be addressed by a robust automated QA system. However, a suitable QC framework is not available in the literature, which could help to build a robust QA pipeline in this domain. In our current research, we address this issue by annotating our proposed QC dataset in the above mentioned domain. Moreover, the questions in our proposed dataset have two novel features, namely factoid/descriptive questions without “wh-words” and use of “polite phrases” in the questions. The inclusion of these two features makes our proposed dataset more robust and realistic, but it also makes the question classification task more challenging, since none of the existing datasets support these features. To address this challenge, we suggest a novel data augmentation technique to train our models. Finally, we propose a novel question classifier that works as a benchmark setup for the proposed dataset.We compare the performance of our proposed architecture with previously proposed models for the QC task and demonstrate the superiority of our proposed architecture.

title: Humidity Sensing in a Flexible SnO/MWCNT/PVP Nanocomposite for Wearable Devices_supp1-3683083.docx
authors: Shengchun Yang
abstract: Accurate humidity monitoring is crucial for diagnosing skin and respiratory conditions. However, developing portable, high-sensitivity sensors for dynamic human humidity detection remains a significant challenge. This study presents a flexible wearable humidity monitoring system utilizing a high-performance SnO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/MWCNT/PVP sensor. Material characterization confirmed that SnO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> incorporation significantly enhanced hydrophilicity, reducing the contact angle from 85.4° to 51°. Thus, it demonstrated excellent sensitivity and stable dynamic humidity-response behavior across 25–90% RH under controlled conditions, featuring rapid response/recovery times (327 s/89 s at 75% RH) and low hysteresis (3.4% RH). The integrated system successfully tracked human respiration in real-time, distinguishing slow, normal, and rapid breathing patterns. It further identified distinct spoken letters (A, B, C, and D) through humidity-induced resistance changes. Implementation of a hybrid LSTM-CNN model with attention mechanisms significantly enhanced breathing pattern classification accuracy by enabling high-precision temporal humidity data analysis. This work establishes an effective strategy for next-generation wearable health monitoring devices.

title: Channel Characterization and Emulation for Nonterrestrial Network Communications: Challenges and Solutions
authors: Hao Sun, Xiang Zhang, Yani Chi, Wei Fan
abstract: Nonterrestrial network (NTN) communication plays a crucial role in providing ubiquitous coverage and seamless connectivity in remote areas where terrestrial network (TN) infrastructure is unavailable, which has been regarded as a key solution in the 6G network. Different from the communications in a TN, NTN communication suffers from huge transmission loss, large propagation delay and highly dynamic Doppler shift. Therefore, it is of great significance to study and characterize the radio propagation channel, providing guidance for design, optimization, and performance evaluation of NTN systems. This work discusses the state-of-the-art works and challenges for channel modeling, sounding, and emulation of NTN systems. Furthermore, a real antenna array (RAA)-based passive channel sounder, which is capable of measuring dynamic spatial wideband modulated signals transmitted from in-orbit satellites, is presented in this work. Exemplary channel measurement results for the Tiantong geostationary Earth orbit (GEO) satellite under urban scenarios, containing multipath channel parameters in spatial and polarization domains, experimentally demonstrate the capabilities of the developed NTN channel sounder.

title: Efficient Privacy-Preserving Jaccard Similarity Evaluation over Multisets for Secure Collaborative Data Analysis
authors: Linming Gong, Weihu Cao, Jun Wang, Zhou Su, Jiliang Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: With the rapidly growing demand for collaborative data analysis, Jaccard Coefficient (JC) computation over multisets has been widely adopted in data deduplication to enhance large-scale data processing efficiency, but meanwhile incurs some security issues such as the leakage of input data sets. Thus, Secure Jaccard Coefficient Computation over Multisets (SCJM) schemes have been proposed. However, existing solutions that indirectly compute JC fail to protect the privacy of intersection and union cardinalities, incur high computational overhead, and rely on approximation techniques that cannot support high-precision analysis or simulation-based security proofs. To address these problems, we propose a secure and efficient protocol to accurately compute JC over multisets. Specifically, the protocol computes JC for small-scale data domains using secure oblivious ratio computation, ensuring that under the semi-honest adversarial model, the intersection and union cardinalities remain concealed from all parties, including the decryption key holder, during and after the computation. And it achieves a linear computational cost of O(em-1) without accuracy loss, where em-1 denotes the maximum number of repetitions of an element in the multiset. The protocol can also be extended to compute the cardinalities of intersection and union. To further enhance efficiency, we introduce a cloud-assisted encryption scheme, which improves computational efficiency by 25.5% to 30.4% compared with the non-cloud-assisted scheme. Additionally, we provide a secure proof of the proposed protocol in the ideal-real paradigm. Experimental results show efficiency advantages of our protocol over the state-of-the-art solution.

title: Segmented Progressive Assessment in Mechanics Education: A Full-Process Model for Improving Learning Outcomes
authors: Keming Zhang, Pei Zheng, Zixuan Chen
abstract: Traditional assessment methods in basic mechanics courses often prioritize final outcomes over learning processes, leading to passive student engagement and limited development of practical skills. In response to these limitations, a three-phase assessment framework was designed that integrates formative evaluation, gamification, and real-time feedback throughout the teaching cycle. This model comprises three phases: 1)adaptive preassessment (e.g., class participation and quizzes), 2)competency reinforcement (e.g., group simulations and error correction tassks); and 3) comprehensive application (e.g., theory visualization and dual-teacher evaluation). Implemented in a semester-long mechanics course (N = 120), the staged formative performance assessment (SFPA) cohort showed higher participation (+28%) and higher average exam scores (+7.8 points) than the comparison cohort. Qualitative feedback revealed enhanced teamwork abilities (reported by 85% of students) and stronger motivation through gamified elements like ”lucky draws” and ”beauty discovery” activities. However, challenges such as increased teacher workload and subjective grading criteria were identified. This study contributes a scalable framework for engineering education reform, emphasizing the synergy between process-driven assessment and student-centered learning. Its findings provide actionable insights for educators seeking to balance academic rigor with engagement in foundational STEM courses.

title: Reduced Maximum Curvature-Based Linear Path Smoothing Method with Enhanced Kinematic Performance for 6R Robot Manipulators_supp1-3685073.mp4
authors: Yaqiu Liu
abstract: To address dynamic instability, efficiency loss, and precision degradation from tangential and normal discontinui-ties in linear paths, a C<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>-continuous local smoothing method is proposed for microscale, high-precision 6R robot trajectories. With maximum curvature control and analytical error control as the primary objective, an analytical smoothing framework based on “outward-extended control point + error tube con-straint” is established. Rather than confining splines to corners, outward-extended control points are analytically optimized under internal and external error constraints to reduce maxi-mum curvature, suppress curvature peaks and enhance conti-nuity and execution smoothness. To ensure global continuity, a C<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>-continuous path structure sharing the same parameter is constructed to synchronize position and orientation. For 6R robot with highly nonlinear kinematics, an enhanced orientation spline strategy and a consistent analytical model of internal and external orientation errors are developed to ensure robustness under complex joint constraints. A unified path-classification framework is also introduced to meet varying requirements in curvature control and execution stability. Sim-ulations and experiments demonstrate that, for 6R robots with highly nonlinear kinematics, the proposed method achieves curvature reduction, orientation smoothness, and joint motion stability while satisfying both geometric and kinematic con-straints of the robot’s joints.

title: Detection of Larval Foci Through Computer Vision in Multispectral Drone Images Using Faster R-CNN
authors: Angelica M. Vidal, Mathias P. Solis, Maira Santacruz, Derlis O. Gregor
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: The automatic detection of larval breeding sites was addressed through the use of a convolutional neural network based on Faster R-CNN, trained with multispectral imagery (beyond conventional RGB) captured by a DJI Mavic 3 Multispectral drone. For data acquisition, the cities of San Bernardino and Piribebuy, located in the Cordillera Department (Paraguay), were selected due to their high incidence of potential breeding grounds. The images were annotated to identify objects such as swimming pools and tires. The model, developed in PyTorch, was trained over 100 epochs using the AdamW optimizer and techniques such as normalization, batch normalization, and dropout. The results yielded a precision of 82.11 % and a recall of 76.47 %, demonstrating effective performance in detecting potential larval breeding sites. This highlights the added value of multispectral data compared to RGB imagery in capturing subtle environmental features, which strengthens its utility in early detection strategies. Moreover, the proposed framework has direct implications for public health interventions, vector control programs, and large-scale environmental monitoring in urban and peri-urban areas.
