title:
Combined Impedance Spectroscopy and Physical Characterization Unveils Kinetics of Ag Cations in the SET State of Ag/ZrO
                    <sub>x</sub>
                    :Al/Pt-RRAM with Variable Oxygen Vacancies
authors: Suman Gora, Mandeep Jangra, Deepak Yadav, Jay Singh, Monojit Bag, Arnab Datta
publish: IEEE Transactions on Materials for Electron Devices
abstract: Kinetics and complementary roles of Ag cations and oxygen vacancies to the formation of dual-conduction filament (dual-CF) in the SET state of an Al-doped ZrO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub> (ZrO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub>:Al)-based (Ag/ZrO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub>:Al/Pt) resistive random access memory (RRAM) have been investigated here. Al doping increased oxygen vacancies in ZrO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub>, which impacted diffusion and reduction of the injected Ag<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">+</sup> ions (from top contact) during the SET state of this RRAM. Detail impedance spectroscopy demonstrates faster Ag<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">+</sup> diffusion in a vacancy richer background, which was eventually tailored by Al doping. Traces of Ag in ZrO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub>:Al film was found through separate XPS and EDX characterization. Furthermore, a vacancy migration until the counter electrode (Pt) complemented the said Ag<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">+</sup> diffusion and reduction, which was corroborated by dielectric relaxation behaviour of Ag/ZrO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub>:Al/Pt RRAM for variable Al doped ZrO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub>. In this regard, dual-CF was assessed utilizing XPS, which revealed synergy between the field driven excess vacancies and O<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2-</sup> migration that led to phase transition in ZrO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub> (tetragonal to monoclinic) under the applied SET bias

title: Fortress: A Case Study in Stabilizing Search Recommendations Via Temporal Data Augmentation and Feature Pruning
authors: Milind Pandurang Jagre, Jia Huang, Dayvid V. R. Oliveira, Zhinan Cheng, Babak Seyed Aghazadeh, Puja Das, Chris Alvino, Jinda Han, Kailash Thiyagarajan
abstract: In search and recommendation systems, predictive models often suffer from temporal instability when certain input features introduce volatility in output scores. This instability can degrade model reliability and user experience especially in multi-stage systems where consistent predictions are critical for downstream decision making. We introduce Fortress, a general framework for enhancing model stability and accuracy by identifying and pruning features that contribute to inconsistent prediction scores over time. Fortress leverages historical snapshots temporally partitioned datasets capturing score fluctuations for the same entity across periods and follows a four-step process: (1) collect historical snapshots, (2) identify samples with unstable predictions, (3) isolate and remove instability- inducing features, and (4) retrain models using only stable features. While semantic features from LLMs and BERT-based models improve generalization, they often lack full query or entity coverage. Engagement-based features offer strong predictive power but tend to introduce temporal instability. Fortress mitigates this trade-off by suppressing the volatility of engagement signals while retaining their predictive value leading to more stable and accurate models. We validate Fortress on a query-toapp relevance model in a large-scale app marketplace. Offline experiments demonstrate notable improvements in prediction stability (measured by Coefficient of Variation) and classification performance (measured by PR-AUC).

title: Improved Split-Window CNN-LSTM Architecture for Stress and Cardiac Monitoring Heart Rate Variability Analysis
authors: Sudhangshu Sarkar, Sudipta Santra, Anilesh Dey, Aniruddha Chandra
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: Heart rate variability (HRV) is a physiological indicator that effectively identifies stress and evaluates cardiac health. This study presents a deep learning architecture for resilient HRV signal processing that integrates Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) units enhanced by a Split-Window Approach (SWA). The SWA improves the model's ability to detect transient patterns associated with cardiac irregularities and stress by effectively segmenting HRV data into temporally consistent slices. The proposed CNN-LSTM-SWA model significantly outperforms conventional HRV analysis techniques, as demonstrated by studies on standard HRV datasets. It attains exceptional precision in both stress identification and cardiac condition classification. These findings indicate that real-time applications in wearable and mobile health monitoring systems could be feasible.

title: SmartTrip: An AI-Powered Travel Planning Platform with Intelligent Agent Support, Voice and Multilingual Capabilities
authors: Dhivya Guru, Senthil Raj Subramaniam
abstract: This paper presents Smart Trip, an AI-enhanced travel planning and booking platform designed to serve both travelers and travel agents through a unified interface. The system incorporates role-based logins, enabling travelers to search, plan, and manage trips while allowing agents to perform bookings and manage client interactions. Smart Trip features intelligent chatbots for both travelers and agents, supporting voice interaction and multilingual capabilities to ensure inclusive and accessible communication. It leverages invoice and expense scanning using OCR(Optical Character Recognition) and AI(Artificial Intelligence), enabling users to manage financial records seamlessly within chat interfaces. Embedded summarization tools provide real-time financial summaries for enhanced decision-making. The backend leverages Google Vertex AI with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and embeddings for contextual responses and personalization. The platform is designed with scalability in mind, and a React/Next.js frontend ensures a modern, userfriendly experience. This paper presents the system architecture, technology stack, testing results, and future directions for Smart Trip, highlighting its potential to revolutionize personalized travel management and agent workflows.

title: Photovoltaic Defect Detection Based on the Improved Yolov8
authors: Baoluo Chai, Ling Tang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)

title: DFLADMM-M: Decentralized Federated Learning via Momentum-Accelerated ADMM
authors: Shaohe Xiao, Jia Yu, Gang Wang, Weikang Liu, Qinquan Hu, Bin Cao
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Decentralized Federated Learning (DFL) has emerged as a viable alternative to traditional federated learning by eliminating the central server and enabling peer-to-peer collaboration. Despite its benefits in scalability and communication efficiency, it suffers from model inconsistency across clients and local overfitting due to data heterogeneity. To address these challenges, we propose DFLADMM-m, a DFL algorithm that integrates the Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM) with momentum method. The ADMM enforces consensus among local models, while stochastic gradient descent (SGD) with momentum accelerates updates and alleviates oscillations. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks such as MNIST and CIFAR-10 demonstrate that DFLADMM-m achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art DFL algorithms, offering improved accuracy under heterogeneous data settings and various communication topologies.

title: CNN-Powered Brain Tumor Classification and Detection Using MRI Based on Deep Learning Methods
authors: Bingshati Mondal, Ritwika Mukherjee, Sekh Aship, Sanjida Khatun, Mukesh Kumar, Priyanshu Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: It is very essential to use magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) to tell the differences between brain tumors, so that doctors could make a quick diagnosis and plan treatment. The study presents an automated model for the detection and classification of brain tumors using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). The proposed method prepares MRI images, identifies significant features, and categorizes tumors into four distinct groups. There are a lot of convolutional layers in the CNN architecture, and they use batch normalization and max pooling. Then it has dense layers with dropout regularization. We used a publicly available MRI dataset of brain tumors to train and test the model. The method is much better at classifying tumors than traditional machine learning models, as shown by experimental results.

title: A Comparative Study of Machine Learning Models for Multiclass Employee Absenteeism Prediction with Explainability
authors: Rafael Silva Taveira, Helena S. Balbino, Jurandir Silva, Rogerio Salvini, Juliana Felix
abstract: Employee absenteeism poses a significant challenge for organizations, affecting productivity and operational planning. This paper investigates the use of machine learning techniques for multiclass prediction of employee absenteeism severity based on workplace and personal attributes. Seven classification models are evaluated using stratified <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0}$</tex>-fold cross-validation, with performance assessed using accuracy and the macro-averaged F1-score. Statistical significance is analyzed using the Wilcoxon signed-rank test. The results show that ensemble-based models achieve the best performance, with LightGBM and XGBoost presenting comparable predictive capability. To enhance transparency, SHAP-based explainability is employed to identify class-specific factors influencing absenteeism predictions. The findings demonstrate that combining robust evaluation protocols with explainable machine learning yields reliable, interpretable models that support human resource decision-making.

title: Electric Vehicles in Microgrids: A Review of Control Architectures and Integration Approaches
authors: Ignacia Loyola, Enrique Espina, Karina Barbosa
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: The integration of electric vehicles (EV) into micro-grids (MG) introduces new control challenges due to the vari-ability of charging demand and the need to coordinate multiple distributed energy resources. In response, a variety of control schemes have been proposed in the literature. This work presents a functional classification of control architectures implemented in MG with EV, structured according to hierarchical control levels. The classification e nables a d etailed a nalysis oft he relationship between each control level and the specific s ervices i t provides to the MG. The proposed framework facilitates the comparative evaluation of existing strategies and offers a more structured perspective on current research trends and gaps in the field.

title: Reliable AI for Predictive Modeling: From Training-Time Accuracy to Operational Forecasting
authors: Dmytro Smirnov
abstract: Predictive modeling is now commonly used for forecasting demand, assessing risk, and guiding operational decisions. However, many promising projects fail to progress beyond initial experiments due to issues such as reduced performance in real-world settings, changing feature pipelines, or stakeholders' inability to interpret model confidence. We present a practical framework for building reliable predictive models that remain effective after deployment. We connect modern learning methods, sequence models, and attention-based architectures with engineering decisions that help maintain model stability. Our workflow integrates leakage checks, evaluation, interpretability, and continuous monitoring at every stage of model development. An experimental study illustrates how different model families behave and compared how various model types manage an imbalanced failure-prediction task and show why relying only on accuracy can be misleading. This work offers a comprehensive framework for teams aiming to achieve reliable, sustainable, and verifiable predictive outcomes.

title: A Comparative Study of Grid Search and TPE for Tuning PPO on Atari Games
authors: Enrique Escalona V., Patricio Olivares R.
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)

title: Redesigning Home Energy Management Systems with Energy Harvesting and Integrated Control in Distributed Energy Resources
authors: Eshwara Prasath Ramesh, Maciej A. Noras
abstract: Modern residential energy systems increasingly integrate distributed energy resources (DERs) such as rooftop solar, battery storage, and bidirectional electric vehicle chargers. Conventional Home Energy Management System (HEMS) architectures rely on uninterruptible power supplies (UPS), local controllers, and residential routers to coordinate energy flows during grid outages. This introduces higher costs, complex installation requirements, and reliability risks when backup devices fail. This paper proposes a redesigned architecture that eliminates the need for standalone UPS and controllers by introducing an Energy-Harvesting Signal Unit (EHSU) and utilizing the control functions directly from the DER devices. The EHSU is a low-power module that harvests ambient energy and transmits outage-triggered signals directly to the inverter, which then transitions into grid-forming mode. Cloud connectivity is provided through an integrated cellular communication module, removing dependence on consumer routers. Comparative analysis shows that the proposed design reduces system cost by up to 70%, improves resilience by eliminating single points of failure, and reduces outage response latency from hundreds of milliseconds to less than 100 ms in CAN-based signaling. The result is a simplified, resilient, and future-ready residential energy system architecture that aligns with emerging DER trends and regulatory requirements.

title: Modelling of Structural Vibrations Excitation on RTG Cranes
authors: Nelson Cáceres Venegas, Constanza Ahumada Sanhueza, Doris Saez Hueichapan
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)

title: Mislabel Identification Using Transfer Learning-Based Ensemble Method
authors: Md Shariful Islam, Min Jun Kim, Prashanta Dutta
abstract: Accurate labeling of training data is essential for reliable supervised machine learning, particularly in sensitive applications such as virus classification, autonomous driving, precision manufacturing, and medical diagnostics. However, the labeling process is labor-intensive and error-prone. Even widely used datasets such as MNIST and ImageNet contain numerous mislabeled samples. To address this challenge, we developed a transfer learning based ensemble method that identifies mislabeled data through majority filtering and consensus filtering using fine-tuned pretrained deep neural networks, including ResNet-50, ResNet-101, VGG-16, EfficientNet, MobileNet, and Inception. Our approach was first validated on the MNIST dataset, where the ensemble detected approximately 751 label inconsistencies, which closely aligns with previously reported estimates of mislabeled samples. Additional experiments with synthetically injected mislabels demonstrated that the method could recover up to 100% of known corrupted labels using majority and consensus voting strategies. The method was then applied to a highly pure adeno-associated virus (AAV) nanopore dataset, where artificial mislabels were introduced for evaluation; the ensemble successfully identified most mislabeled samples and correctly recovered their true labels. Experiments on balanced and unbalanced AAV datasets further showed improved performance on the balanced subset, where all injected mislabels were detected. Compared to classical filtering techniques such as KNN, k-means clustering, and advanced machine learning based mislabel detection (e.g., DivideMix), the proposed ensemble method demonstrated superior accuracy, stability, and true-label recovery, establishing it as a strong general-purpose mislabel detection framework—particularly well-suited for complex, fine-grained datasets such as nanopore signals and other biological measurement data.

title: Beyond the Black Box: Applying XAI to Supervised Multiclass Intrusion Detection
authors: Jiaming Ding, Peng Shan, Rui Ding, Elly Yijun Zhu, Heng Li, Yue Wu, Tianze Kang, Yuxin Qiao, Chufeng Jiang

title: Adaptative Serious Game as a Support Tool for Children with Reading and Writing Difficulties
authors: Matias Ricardo Villanueva Paz, Hernan Alejandro Quintana Cruz
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Learning disabilities affect fundamental psychological processes related to language comprehension and usage, resulting in significant reading and writing difficulties. Additionally, conventional methods have become outdated and unengaging for many individuals experiencing these challenges. For this reason, the present study assesses the effectiveness of a game designed with Dynamic Difficulty Adjustment (DDA) as a supportive tool for children with learning disabilities in their reading and writing processes. A group of children aged 7 to 12 with reading difficulties participated in a two-week intervention. Their reading skills were assessed using the Prolec <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}$</tex> battery before and after the intervention. Results indicated significant improvements in word reading, pseudoword reading, discrimination between identical and different words, and error detection. Additionally, usability results demonstrated the game's ease of use and participants' high level of acceptance. These findings underscore the potential of adaptive serious games as complementary tools in the psycho-pedagogical treatment of learning disabilities.

title: A Sampling Frac-N PLL with Current Steering Inverse-Constant-Slope DTC
authors: Ergen Tao, Guofu Niu, Fa Dai
abstract: We present a current-steering, inverse-constantslope digitally controlled delay line (DTC) for coarse tuning. We analyze the proposed DTC architecture and its key performance metrics. We then describe a fractional-<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N$</tex> PLL that incorporates the proposed DTC and a CDAC-based sampling phase detector. The PLL is designed using GlobalFoundries' 22 FDX technology. Simulation results are presented.

title: Scalable Multi-Agent DRL for Joint Offloading and Resource Allocation in MEC Networks
authors: Yaoyi Hu, Hua Qu, Jihong Zhao
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)

title: Dynamic and Fair Allocation of Shared Energy Resources in Energy Communities
authors: Eduardo J. Salazar, Sebastián Salazar-Pérez, Veronica A. Rosero Morillo, Mauro Jurado, Sebastián Vaca
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper presents a mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) model for the dynamic and fair allocation of shared energy in small-scale energy communities. The model computes hourly coefficients to distribute energy from a shared photovoltaic (PV) plant and a community battery energy storage system (BESS). Its primary goal is to minimize the total electricity bill while enforcing quantitative fairness among members. Fairness is achieved through daily constraints on each member's energy share and a minimax term that limits the maximum cost any single member pays for grid electricity. The model also includes revenues from flexibility services sold to the grid. Simulation results for a four-household community show weekly bill reductions of at least 78 % compared to individual operation. The model guarantees each member at least 25 % of the shared PV and BESS energy, achieving fair allocation in a single optimization step with an equity penalty below 3%.

title: Beyond Content Filtering: A “Circuit Breaker” Architecture for Autonomous Agent Action Safety
authors: Harini Sridharan, Reshma Nair

title: Balancing Privacy and Model Performance: An Experimental Analysis of Data Anonymization Techniques
authors: Nachiappan Chockalingam, Aswathnarayan Muthukrishnan Kirubakaran, Adithya Parthasarathy, Sumit Saha, Abhirup Mazumder, Mayilsamy Palanigounder, Nitin Saksena, Bikesh Kumar
abstract: Balancing data privacy with model performance remains a central challenge in deploying machine learning systems on sensitive datasets. This paper presents an experimental analysis of three widely used syntactic data anonymization techniques k -anonymity, l -diversity, and t -closeness and evaluates their impact on both privacy protection and downstream machine learning utility. Using the Adult Census and German Credit benchmark datasets, we systematically measure privacy outcomes through re-identification risk and attribute disclosure risk, and utility outcomes through classification accuracy, AUC, and information loss across multiple learning models. The results demonstrate that <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{t}$</tex>-closeness provides the strongest privacy guarantees, achieving up to 94.2% reduction in re-identification risk, but incurs the highest degradation in model performance. K -anonymity preserves the greatest utility while exhibiting significant vulnerability to attribute disclosure under skewed sensitive attribute distributions. L-diversity consistently offers a balanced tradeoff, maintaining moderate privacy protection with acceptable utility loss. The study further analyzes the influence of dataset characteristics, model choice, and computational overhead on anonymization effectiveness. Based on empirical findings, we derive practical guidelines to support informed selection of anonymization techniques according to application sensitivity and performance requirements, contributing actionable insights for privacy-aware machine learning practice

title: Hybrid Architectures with Attention Mechanisms and Evolutionary Hyperparameter Optimization for Robust Pneumonia Detection
authors: Marco Fidel Mayta Quispe, Leonid Alemán Gonzales, Charles Ignacio Mendoza Mollocondo, Juan Carlos Juarez Vargas
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: For medical artificial intelligence, automated X-ray-based pneumonia diagnosis is a major challenge. This paper suggests a thorough and reliable method that combines CNNs with hybrid CNN-Transformer architectures and visual attention mechanisms (Grad-CAM, CBAM). Stratified cross-validation is used to optimize hyperparameters using evolutionary methods (both Bayesian and genetic). Numerous open Kaggle databases are used to evaluate the method, guaranteeing patient demographic variety across imaging modalities and clinical contexts. AUC, sensitivity, and accuracy gains over baseline models are statistically significant, according to the results. The suggested framework aids in the creation of clinical decision support systems that are both explanatory and adaptive, making them appropriate for practical application in a variety of healthcare environments.

title: Convolutional Multi-Scale Feature Enhanced Industrial Anomaly Detection
authors: Dan Li, Xujun Li, Zhaowei Zeng
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Industrial anomaly detection aims to identify samples that deviate from normal patterns and plays a critical role in quality inspection. Although CLIP demonstrates strong generalization ability through vision-language alignment, its Vision Transformer backbone is limited in modeling fine-grained local attributes, which restricts its performance in industrial anomaly detection. To address this issue, we propose a CLIP-based industrial anomaly detection framework with convolutional multi-scale feature enhancement. A multi-scale patch embedding strategy is introduced to capture local information at different resolutions, while a dual-branch architecture combines CNN-based local feature extraction with Transformer-based global modeling. Furthermore, a differential attention mechanism is designed to enhance anomaly-sensitive regions and suppress background interference. Multi-scale and image-level contrastive learning are jointly employed to improve feature discrimination. Experimental results on multiple industrial anomaly detection datasets demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches.

title: Blockchain Transaction Anomaly Detection
authors: Xing Ying Chia, Ming Quan Goh, ZhengXu Lin, Shawn Kuik, Kek Peng Tan, Kai Wei Jake Wong, Huaqun Guo

title: Scaling YOLOv8n for Road Object Detection Under Limited Data: A Comparative Study Using BDD100K Subsets
authors: Jateen Joharapurkar, Sahar Hooshmand, Benyamin Ahmadnia, Ali Jalooli, Reza Roofegari Nejad

title: A Game-Theoretic Federated Learning Approach with Heterogeneity-Sensitive Multimodal Policy for Dynamic Scheduling in Baseband Processors
authors: Jie Peng, Wei Chen
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)

title: An Interactive Beam Element Analyzer for Problem-Based Learning and Rapid Assessment in Mechanics of Materials
authors: Stephen Andrew Wilkerson
abstract: This paper presents an interactive MATLAB Live Script-based Truss Builder developed to support problem-based learning, formative assessment, and conceptual understanding in undergraduate engineering statics courses. The tool enables students and instructors to construct, modify, and analyze planar truss structures through a graphical user interface, allowing joints, members, supports, and loads to be defined interactively and visualized in real time. By coupling immediate graphical feedback with underlying equilibrium-based analysis, the software encourages students to explore “what-if” scenarios and develop intuition about force paths, support reactions, and internal member forces. The Truss Builder is implemented using a modular and welldocumented code structure, enabling straightforward customization by instructors for course-specific needs or extensions. Beyond its role as a visual demonstration aid, the tool functions as an assignment and quiz generator by allowing instructors to rapidly create unique truss configurations, as well as a problem-checking and self-evaluation platform that students can use to verify hand calculations and identify conceptual errors. These features support formative assessment by emphasizing process, exploration, and iterative learning rather than reliance on static, pre-defined textbook problems. The software is distributed freely through the MATLAB File Exchange, accompanied by instructional video tutorials that demonstrate classroom and independent study use cases. The paper situates the tool within the broader literature on problem-based learning and visual learning aids in engineering education and discusses how interactive, student-constructed problem environments can enhance engagement and conceptual understanding in statics. Example truss problems are presented to illustrate the tool's analytical accuracy and instructional value. The results suggest that the Truss Builder provides an effective, accessible platform for reinforcing core statics concepts while supporting modern pedagogical approaches in engineering education.

title: Engineering Reliable Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Regulatory and Compliance Systems
authors: Thiyagarajan Kalyanasundaram, Shravya Bussari, Dinalan Sundaram, Kapil Kumar Sharma
abstract: Regulatory systems in domains such as healthcare, finance, and legal compliance require more than accurate question answering; they demand reliability, traceability, and stability as policies evolve over time. While Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) improves factual grounding compared to standalone large language models, many deployments remain fragile in production due to retrieval mismatches, unsupported generation, and silent behavioral regressions following document updates. This paper presents a reliability-engineering approach to RAG for regulatory systems. We integrate hybrid densesparse retrieval with cross-encoder reranking, faithfulness guardrails that enforce evidence-backed generation, and continuous regression testing triggered by policy changes. Together, these mechanisms treat RAG as an evolving software system rather than a static inference pipeline. Experimental evaluation on a validated regulatory question-answer set shows that hybrid retrieval improves Recall@5 by up to 18% over dense-only baselines, while faithfulness validation reduces unsupported statements by 67%. A regression framework detects policy-induced answer changes in controlled update scenarios, supporting audit-ready operation. The proposed framework provides a practical blueprint for engineering reliable and auditable RAG systems in compliance-heavy enterprise environments.

title: Nomad-AMR: Generative Edge Intelligence and Predictive Modeling for Antimicrobial Stewardship in Data-Scarce Populations
authors: Pooja Walia, Rajat Rawal
abstract: Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) is a rapidly increasing threat to global health, driven in part by delayed or unavailable diagnostics that force empiric antibiotic prescribing. These challenges are amplified in rural and resource-constrained environments where laboratory access, logistics, and connectivity limitations prevent timely antimicrobial susceptibility insights. We propose Nomad-AMR, a decentralized multi-modal edge-AI (Artificial Intelligence) framework for antimicrobial stewardship in data-scarce populations as witnessed in Region 3. The system combines (i) nanopore signal classification for genomic resistance markers, (ii) computer vision-based phenotypic monitoring via low-cost microscopy imaging, and (iii) volatile organic compound (VOC) time-series analysis using electronic-nose (e-nose) sensing. To address limited rural data availability and rare resistance profiles, Nomad-AMR incorporates generative modeling for synthetic data augmentation, including sequence-to-signal simulation for nanopore voltage traces and diffusion-based augmentation for microscopy variability. The framework employs late-fusion probabilistic estimation to generate a unified resistance risk score and supports offline deployment on low-power edge hardware (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson-class embedded GPUs) using quantized small language models (SLMs) for clinical decision support. Nomad-AMR is designed to improve diagnostic responsiveness, robustness, and deployability in rural clinical and agricultural-adjacent environments.

title: Blending Potential Fields and Hough Transform for Advanced Obstacle Avoidance in Mobile Robotics Systems
authors: Esteban Pérez, Richard López, William Chamorro, Viviana Moya, Marco Herrera, Alvaro Javier Prado-Romo
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Omnidirectional robots with mecanum wheels offer enhanced maneuverability, making them ideal platforms for realtime obstacle avoidance in static and dynamic environments. In this work, we propose an obstacle avoidance framework that combines 2D LiDAR data with the Hough transform to detect and reconstruct line segments, enabling structured mapping of the environment. These segments are integrated into an Artificial Potential Field (APF) algorithm, generating repulsive and attractive reactive navigation forces. The method is implemented on a Hiwonder omnidirectional robot, utilizing a Jetson Nano and ROS Noetic architecture, which enables the modular integration of different trajectory controllers. We evaluate the performance of the APF algorithm in combination with three controllers, including Sliding Mode Control (SMC) and null space controllers. The experiments conducted in both static and dynamic environments demonstrated that a fast, structured reconstruction enables the handling of obstacles with varying sizes and morphologies, particularly when combined with robust trajectory controllers such as SMC and null space. All implementation code is made available to support reproducibility and future developments.

title: A Novel Butterfly Slotted CMPA with Peripheral Ring Slot for 5G Sub-6 and ISM Band Applications
authors: Anurima Majumdar, Antara Ghosal, Suparna Biswas, Moupali Roy, Arpita Santra, Swati Barui
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: In this paper, a new compact, ultra-wideband (UWB) patch antenna is proposed using a Coplanar Waveguide(CPW) fed technique by inserting a butterfly slot on the circular patch. The proposed antenna is designed to operate in Sub-6GHz frequency range of 5G Communication. The proposed antenna is also operating at 2.4 GHz (ISM bands) which makes it suitable for biosensor applications. The proposed antenna shows multiband resonance. The proposed antenna demonstrates a simulated impedance bandwidth(IBW) of 187% ranging from 0.4 GHz to 12.4 GHz covering the whole 5G Sub-6GHz lower band along with ISM bands. The proposed antenna is simulated and optimized using Ansoft HFSS Software tool. The simulated and measured results are in good agreement with each other.

title: A Method of 3D Path Planning for Multi-Drone Based on Improved Genetic Algorithm
authors: Liuyuan Qiao, Haihui Zhou
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: When multiple UAVs perform missions together in the urban area, they need to consider the energy consumption balance, the shortest three-dimensional flight path, and no collision between each other. To solve this problem, an improved genetic algorithm (IGA) is proposed. The goal of this method is to minimize the total path length of multiple UAVs, balance the task allocation as much as possible, and make the flight path as collision free as possible. Based on the traditional genetic algorithm, the population initialization strategy, fitness function design, crossover and mutation operators, elite retention mechanism, and multi strategy mutation strategy are optimized. The simulation results show that the improved genetic algorithm proposed in this paper can converge to the optimal solution within 100 generations in the simulation scenario of 200 m long * <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 0 0 m}$</tex> wide * <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 0 m}$</tex> high, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3 0}$</tex> must pass targets, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{6}$</tex> UAVs, and 8 tall buildings. Compared with the traditional unimproved genetic algorithm, it has certain advantages in the total path length, task allocation balance, and convergence speed.

title: Heterogeneous Graph Neural Tangent Kernel
authors: Ruiyang Chen
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Graph neural networks play an important role in data processing on both homogeneous and heterogeneous graphs. Although many heterogeneous graph neural networks(HGNNs) exists and achieved great results, they cannot well capture both structure and semantic information. In this paper, we incorporate the random walk strategy and the neural tangent kernel to build a Heterogeneous Graph Neural Tangent Kernel (HG-NTK), and incorporating it into HGNNs. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to introduce neural tangent kernel into the field of HGNNs, and extensive experiments demonstrate that HG-NTK outperforms a wide range of baselines on three real-world datasets.

title: Multi-Objective Optimal Scheduling of Smart Microgrid with Uncertainty Analysis of Electric Vehicle
authors: Moslem Dehghani, Seyyed Mohammad Bornapour, Jose Rodriguez
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: To develop the concept of Microgrids (MGs), distributed generation units, Energy Storage Systems (ESSs), and Electric Vehicles (EVs) are being combined, which in turn automate distribution networks and also better integrate renewable energy sources into the MG. The objectives of this study are to simultaneously reduce the daily cost and pollution emission of a small MG system incorporating Photovoltaics (PVs), Wind Turbines (WTs), ESSs, and EVs while considering ESS and EV charging requirements, as well as consumer loads. Besides minimizing operational costs and emissions, the proposed Energy Management System (EMS) takes into account the initial energy level of the ESS and EV, as well as the uncertainties associated with the EV in the proposed EMS. The prices of PV and WT energy are considered, and a time-of-use tariff is applied to the energy bought from the utility. The gray wolf algorithm is employed to solve the optimization problem, and a weighted sum method is applied to address the multiobjective problem alongside the suggested algorithms. In the smart MG, diverse combinations of resources, including WT, PV, ESS, and EV with uncertainties, are considered to demonstrate the effectiveness and performance of the suggested EMS. The results show that by considering the ESS and EV, both goals, such as cost and emission, are reduced. Furthermore, by increasing the participation time of EVs, the cost and emissions are reduced by 5.4 % and 0.14 %, respectively.

title: A Multi-Level Intelligent Framework for Unknown Vulnerability Capture and Alert Screening
authors: Jiming Bai, Baojiang Cui, Wenchuan Yang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Addressing the limitations of current detection methods against complex threats like APTs, we propose a four-layer intelligent framework. It integrates a Base Platform for efficient high-speed traffic filtering; a Vulnerability Discovery System targeting unknown zero-day exploits (e.g., command injection, buffer overflow); an Alert Aggregation System to minimize false positives; and a Deep Analysis System utilizing semantic models for threat interpretation. This architecture effectively resolves challenges in real-time processing and high-dimensional data analysis, significantly enhancing vulnerability capture accuracy.

title: Trajectory Tracking Control of Omnidirectional Mobile Robots: A Comparison between SMC and Null-Space Controllers
authors: Richard López, Esteban Pérez, William Chamorro, Viviana Moya, Marco Herrera, Alvaro Javier Prado-Romo
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Sliding Mode Control (SMC) and Null-Space Controllers (NS) are widely recognized for their robustness and adaptability in handling nonlinear systems and multi-objective control tasks, making them suitable for mobile robot trajectory tracking. This study presents the implementation and comparative analysis of several trajectory tracking strategies for an omnidirectional mobile robot. The evaluated methods include SMC approaches (lyapunov-based and transfer function) and NS controllers (PI, PD, PID, and Lyapunov-based variants). The controllers were assessed on a rectangular and a lemniscate shaped trajectory, with fixed and trajectory-facing orientation modes. All controllers were deployed on a real robotic platform using ROS Noetic. The results were evaluated against a visual ground-truth system, which used a top-down recording rig and a low-error 2D visual estimator for precise trajectory measurement. The results demonstrated that the Lyapunov-based controllers (named SMC-LY and NS-LY) outperformed the other variants, achieving enhanced trajectory and orientation tracking accuracy, improved disturbance rejection, and greater energy efficiency. These findings underscore their robustness and practical applicability for trajectory tracking in autonomous mobile robotics.

title: Intelligent Super-Resolution Fusion Method for Satellite and UAV Data Based on Differential Neural Networks
authors: Guozhu Yang, Chao Gao, Haitao Cheng, Shirui Sun, Huamin Sun, Yuanyuan Li
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Shallow fusion of spectral spatial information from satellite and UAV data results in low super-resolution fusion quality. To address this, a super-resolution intelligent fusion method based on differential neural networks for satellite and UAV data is proposed. Multi-source remote sensing data from different satellites and UAVs are collected according to research needs. Metadata is recorded and uniformly stored in GeoTIFF format. Two-step geometric correction is performed on SAR images, radiometric correction is applied to all images, a reference image is selected for registration, and the data is integrated into a complete dataset. Considering the characteristics of multimodal data, a dynamically learnable differential convolution kernel is designed, a hierarchical channel attention module is proposed, and a combination of cross-modal feature interaction and sub-pixel convolution is used to extract data features based on differential neural networks. A super-resolution reconstruction method based on generative adversarial networks is employed. Utilizing multisource datasets and extracted features, a generator and discriminator with an encoder-decoder structure are alternately optimized and trained to generate high-quality, high-resolution fused images, achieving intelligent fusion. Experiments show that the research method stabilizes the PSNR at a high level during the final convergence phase for both 3 x and 5 x magnification tasks. The SSIM (Signal Simulation Index) fluctuates narrowly across multiple test sets, and in some scenes it approaches the level of native satellite data. The average NIQE (National Information Quality Index) on the multi-scene test set is as low as 2.84, approximately 0.73 lower than the best value of the comparative method, significantly improving the quality of super-resolution fusion.

title: Explainability First: Designing Trustworthy Machine Learning Models for Real-World Deployment
authors: Mounica Achanta, Dharanidhar Vuppu
abstract: Machine learning models are increasingly shaping decisions of vital importance across industries. However, their implementation in real-world systems is often limited by issues of trust, transparency, and governance. Although predictive accuracy is still the main goal in most machine learning workflows, explainability is simply seen as a post hoc diagnostic. This document claims that, for machine learning to be reliable, auditable, and trusted by stakeholders, explainability should be the means trusted throughout the lifecycle. We put forward the idea of an Explainability First framework for structured data systems that helps to bring about feature transparency, model diversity, cross, model explanation consistency, and stability analysis into model development and evaluation. We argue using public datasets and several model families that similar predictive performance of models does not guarantee that they will have similar explainability behavior or retraining stability. Our findings emphasize that explanation stability and cross-model agreement are more reliable deployment readiness indicators than mere accuracy. The suggested framework is independent of the model used, it can be reproduced, and it is applicable to any industry. It provides practical advice to those who are involved in the designing of trustworthy machine learning systems.

title: Brain MRI Image Segmentation and Neurological Disease Diagnosis Using Deep Learning Model
authors: Sabyasachi Mukherjee, Shatabdi Mondal, Bhaskar Roy, Annesha Dubey, Bikas Mondal, Pallav Dutta
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: A comprehensive Deep Learning (DL)-based system for automatically detecting and segmenting brain tumours from magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is presented in this work. A pre-processing pipeline that consists of resizing, converting to greyscale, normalising, and diminishing noise makes sure that the input data is the same before it is broken down into groups and classified. To find tumour areas, segmentation is done using classical methods like thresholding and region growing, as well as a deep segmentation stage based on U-Net. A custom five-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is trained on 1,500 MRI images of tumours and 1,500 MRI images of non-tumors. The model is assessed with standard performance metrics: accuracy (82.05%), precision (88.46%), recall/sensitivity (85.19%), F1score (86.79%), and ROC-AUC (0.9915). Results obtained after simulation show that the proposed pipeline differentiates between tumour and non-tumor cases while also making segmentation maps to make the results easily understandable. The system segments and classifies to help make diagnoses faster and more consistent in places where resources are limited. In the future, the framework will be expanded to include multi-class tumour grading and a wider range of neurological disorder detection.

title: Reliable mmWave Blockage Detection Under Severe Imbalance: RUS/SMOTE with Ensemble Models
authors: Mohamed I. Ismail, Eslam Hasan, Rhana Elsayed, Quazi Rian Hasnaine, Zubair Md Fadlullah, Mostafa M. Fouda
abstract: Millimeter-wave (mmWave) communication systems are highly vulnerable to blockage, leading to abrupt link degradation and service interruptions. Reliable blockage detection is therefore essential for enabling proactive beam management and maintaining link robustness. However, real-world mmWave blockage datasets are inherently imbalanced, leading to biased learning and poor blockage detection performance when conventional machine learning models are applied directly. In this paper, we present a comprehensive performance evaluation of machine learning-based blockage classification across different data-balancing strategies. Decision Tree (DT) and Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) classifiers are evaluated using the original unbalanced dataset as well as balanced datasets generated via Random Under-Sampling (RUS) and the Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE). Simulation results demonstrate that data imbalance severely degrades blockage recall, resulting in 91 missed blockage events in the unbalanced case. Applying SMOTE reduces false negatives to 69, while RUS further decreases them to 36. Moreover, the MLP model trained with SMOTE achieves the best overall performance, attaining a recall of 0.932 and an ROC-AUC of 0.958, significantly enhancing blockage sensitivity and classification robustness. These findings highlight the importance of combining data-balancing strategies with advanced learning models to enable reliable, proactive blockage detection in mmWave and future 6G wireless systems.

title: Fine-Tuning Large Vision Models for Robust Diabetic Retinopathy Detection
authors: Nada Elmenabawy, Eslam Hasan, Mostafa M. Fouda
abstract: Diabetic Retinopathy (DR) has emerged as a global public health challenge and the leading cause of blindness among the working-age population. While deep learning offers a scalable solution for automated screening, existing models often struggle with consistent performance across diagnostic metrics, particularly recall, or lack open-source availability for real-world deployment. To address these limitations, this study proposes a generalizable Large Vision Model (LVM) based on the ConvNeXT Tiny architecture, fine-tuned on the APTOS 2019 dataset for binary DR detection. The methodology incorporates rigorous preprocessing and augmentation to mitigate class imbalance, ensuring robust feature extraction. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves superior performance, recording an accuracy of 98.09 %, a recall (sensitivity) of 98.09 %, and a specificity of 99.45 %. Furthermore, a Quadratic Weighted Kappa (QWK) score of 0.9618 indicates near-perfect agreement with ground truth labels. This work benchmarks the LVM against state-of-theart methods and releases the model as an open-source tool to facilitate reproducibility and clinical application.

title: Privacy Without Compromise: Benchmarking Local Open-Weight LLMs for Financial Data Extraction
authors: Yash Sharma
abstract: Financial data extraction has traditionally required a compromise: sacrificing data privacy for the convenience and assumed superior accuracy of cloud-based solutions. We challenge this paradigm by benchmarking local open-weight LLMs on consumer-grade hardware (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\leq 18 \text{GB}$</tex> VRAM). Using a 752 -transactions proprietary dataset, we evaluated <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 6}$</tex> model configurations across syntactic compliance, extraction accuracy, and semantic categorization. We distinguish between ‘hallucination’ and ‘filter failure’ in financial contexts, revealing that specific models (e.g., Ministral 3, Qwen 3) can match state-of-the-art accuracy. Our findings demonstrate that high-fidelity financial parsing does not require a trade-off between data sovereignty and extraction precision.

title: A Four-Pillar Framework for Achieving 99.9% Reliability in Early-Stage Distributed Systems
authors: Sandhya Vinjam
abstract: Early-stage (0-to-1) distributed products face unique reliability challenges that existing frameworks do not adequately address. Traditional approaches assume organizational maturity, established processes, and dedicated reliability teams-luxuries unavailable to startups and new product initiatives. We present a systematic four-pillar framework-Detection, Mitigation, Resolution, and Prevention-specifically designed for achieving 99.9% reliability in resource-constrained environments. Through production deployment at a large-scale platform serving millions of daily users, we demonstrate 80% incident reduction, 95% improvement in Mean Time to Detection, and 90% improvement in Mean Time to Mitigation. Theoretical analysis projects that widespread framework adoption could prevent $3.6-7.2B in annual losses across e-commerce, food delivery, fintech, and logistics industries. The framework provides concrete implementation patterns, measurable KPIs, and achieves ROI in 6-12 months with 3-4 month implementation timeline.

title: Characterization of Solar Radiation Effects on Free Space Optical Communication Using High-Altitude Balloon Measurements
authors: Lucas Marsh, Hemani Kaushal, Nirmalkumar G. Patel
abstract: Free-space optical (FSO) communication offers high bandwidth and secure data transmission but remains vulnerable to environmental impairments. This study experimentally investigated the impact of solar-induced radiation on the operating environment of FSO communication links during a period of increased solar activity. Radiation measurements were collected using a Radiacode detector deployed on a high-altitude balloon flight reaching 28,304m in May 2025. The results show that solar-induced background noise increases the bit error rate (BER), particularly under low transmitter power, larger beam divergence, and higher attenuation conditions.

title: Multi-Agent-Based Network Traffic Dataset Quality Evaluation Framework
authors: Linzhe Li, Jun Pan
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Data-driven network traffic analysis has become the mainstream method for network traffic classification. However, many publicly available network traffic datasets still exhibit significant shortcomings in terms of authenticity, accuracy, and other aspects, leading to difficulties in maintaining excellent performance of network traffic classification algorithms under real-world network environments. This paper proposes a multi-agent-based network traffic dataset quality evaluation framework, aiming to assess the deficiencies of datasets in terms of authenticity, accuracy, selection bias, and other factors. By introducing a network traffic data shaping method, designing authenticity, accuracy, and selection bias evaluation agents, and formulating workflows among the agents, the framework enables efficient quality evaluation of network traffic datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that this framework effectively evaluates the multi-dimensional quality of datasets and identifies potential risks associated with the use of mainstream network traffic datasets.

title: From Argumentation to Action: Building Science from REDIVJ Through the TZEPIRC Methodology
authors: Carlos Astengo Noguez, Lorena Martínez Elizalde, Eduard Leonardo Sierra Ballén, Daniela López De Luise, Mario Andrés Bruno Meléndez, Daniel López Salas
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This article presents the conceptual foundations of TZEPIRC, its application in collaborative projects, and the results obtained from four experiences developed by students from universities that are members of the International Network for Video Game Development (REDINVJ), with an emphasis on strengthening transversal competencies such as scientific argumentation, academic writing, and the critical understanding of video games as a cultural phenomenon and object of study.

title: Vision-Based Path Planning for a Small-Scale Autonomous Vehicle with Neural Networks
authors: Rodrigo Báez, Micaela Jara Ten Kathen, Mario Arzamendia
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This work presents the design and simulation of a route planner for a scale autonomous vehicle, based on computer vision and convolutional neural networks. The system goal is to keep the vehicle centered on the route while completing a planned path, using semantic segmentation to interpret navigable areas. Two deep learning models were trained and evaluated: Fast-SCNN and DeepLabV3 with MobileNetV2 as backbone. Both were tested in simulated environments to assess their performance in route segmentation and control response. The evaluation focused on multiple metrics, including segmentation accuracy, inference speed (FPS), and the planner behavior in terms of turning precision and velocity adaptation. Fast-SCNN outperformed DeepLabV3 in all aspects, showing lower turning error, better speed adjustment, and higher real-time processing capabilities. However, the planner showed limitations in detecting and reacting to extreme deviations, such as 90° turns, indicating the need for a complementary global path planner and object detection model. The results highlight the potential of lightweight CNN architectures for real-time autonomous navigation on resourceconstrained platforms, while also identifying the necessity of enhancing perception modules for complex scenarios.

title: A Heterogeneous Neural Network With Multi-Modal Butterfly Attractors for Secure Medical Image Transmission
authors: Hairong Lin, Yanfen Zhang, Fei Yu, Herbert H. C. Iu, Wei Yao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: This work proposes a novel minimal hybrid memristive neural network (HT-MNN) for secure grayscale medical image transmission in the Internet of Medical Things (IoMT). The HT-MNN integrates a Hopfield neuron and a Tabu learning neuron via dual memristive couplings, generating rich multi-modal chaotic butterfly attractors including multi-wing and initial-boosted coexisting structures. The attractors’ number and spatial distribution are flexibly tunable via memristive parameters and initial conditions for controllable dynamical complexity, with circuit implementation validated via PSIM simulations. Leveraging its high-entropy chaotic sequences, we design a medical image encryption scheme combining row–column permutation and multi-stage diffusion. For 512×512 clinical grayscale images, performance evaluation shows encrypted images achieve Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) below 8.5 dB, Mean Squared Error (MSE) over 9200, information entropy of 7.9993 (near the ideal 8.0) and an ultra-large 2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">816</sup> key space. Compared with state-of-the-art medical image encryption methods, the scheme achieves lower encrypted-image PSNR and higher throughput of 11.27 Mbps. With its balanced lightweight design, high-grade security, and strong antinoise robustness, the HT-MNN is well-suited for fast and secure IoMT clinical scenarios such as bedside portable imaging.

title: Nano-Printed Spiral Phase Plate on Fiber Ferrules for Modal Filtering in Free-Space Optical Communications
authors: Samuel Fritsch, Joshua Dugre, R. Krishna Mohan
abstract: In this work, we demonstrate the fabrication as well as immediate application of 3D printed spiral phase plates on the ends of FC/PC fiber adapter ferrule tips using a NanoOne 1000 two-photon polymerization 3D printer from UpNano. The spiral phase plates were fabricated to alter the orbital angular momentum of incoming light, and a direct application of modal filtering in an indoor, short-range, free-space optical communication link was shown. The mode conversion quality of six different 3D printed spiral phase plates on ferrule tips is presented. An <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$l=4$</tex-math></inline-formula> and an <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$l=-4$</tex-math></inline-formula> spiral phase plate were chosen to demonstrate the ability to suppress an aggressing Gaussian signal in a free-space communication link, with the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$l=4$</tex-math></inline-formula> being printed on a FC/PC ferrule tip used as the transmit, and the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$l=-4$</tex-math></inline-formula> printed on a glass slide in the receive architecture of the system. Multiple iterations of the experiment were performed, and it was found that the 3D printed structures were able to suppress the aggressing Gaussian beam on average by approximately 32.7 dB and allowed for error-free communication at 10 Gbps on a vortex beam with the co-propagating aggressor at an identical wavelength to the communication link.

title: A Compact CMOS W -Band Transmitter Front-End With 7-Bit Phase Shifter Resolution
authors: Tal Elazar, Eran Socher
abstract: Vector modulation circuits such as cartesian phase shifters and IQ modulators are integral components of mm-wave transceivers, relying on quadrature signal summation. This work analyzes conventional quadrature summation techniques and provides an in-depth study of the widely used current-summation method, identifying its limitations and the resulting distortion of the output constellation. Guided by these insights, we present a W-band 65 nm bulk CMOS transmitter front-end that integrates a voltage-summation vector modulator (VM) and a power amplifier. The VM features a transformer-based combiner with a single secondary inductor that performs a weighted summation of the quadrature fluxes generated by two primary inductors. Using a low-complexity predistortion algorithm, a linear and predictable 1024-point constellation is achieved with 9% Error Vector Magnitude (EVM). Additionally, phase shift keying (PSK) constellations were measured, demonstrating true 7-bit phase resolution across the 95–97.5 GHz range with minimum 1° phase RMSE.

title: Exploring the Security Dimensions of Identity-Based Proxy Signature Schemes: A Comprehensive Review
authors: Saddam Hussain, Ali Tufail, Haji Awg Abdul Ghani Naim, Muhammad Asghar Khan, Gordana Barb
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Computer Society
abstract: IDentity-Based Proxy Signatures (IDBPS) are a key cryptographic primitive that enables a designated proxy to sign messages on behalf of the Original Signer (OS). This process ensures that the identity of the OS remains authentic and integral. IDBPS employs the concept of Identity-Based Cryptography (IDBC), a framework in which a user's public key is directly derived from a publicly known identity string, such as an email address or a personal name. Such properties make IDBPS suitable for the use of distributed systems, secure communication and digital identity management. Considering these benefits of IDBPS, this survey presents a comprehensive analysis of the existing IDBPS schemes in terms of its contributions, vulnerabilities and real-world deployment. It also evaluate the current IDBPS schemes by comparing their security models, provable security, inspecting the underlying algorithms, and measuring their computational overhead. Additionally, the survey also highlights on the current limitations and gaps, including the importance of proper cryptographic designs, in terms of efficiency and robust security. Finally, we promote the emergence of more secure and efficient constructions in order to overcome the limitation of current IDBPS approaches.

title: Adaptive filtered feedback–driven Nash equilibrium seeking for structurally uncertain nonaffine multiagent systems
authors: Tianli Xu, Shengli Du, Daniel W. C. Ho, Honggui Han, Junfei Qiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper addresses two challenging issues in distributed Nash equilibrium seeking for a class of nonaffine, high-order nonlinear systems. The first lies in the complexity explosion that arises in adaptive feedback control when dealing with intricate system nonlinearities. The second concerns the oscillations caused by uncertain high-order nonaffine dynamics with unknown control directions. To overcome these challenges, a modified finite-time nonlinear tracking differentiator with linear damping components is established, settling down the high sensitivity of traditional filters. A unified framework is developed to handle input nonlinearities, including backlash-like hysteresis and dead zones, by integrating neural network approximation mechanisms with advanced Nussbaum functions, enabling effective compensation for such nonlinear effects. Building on leader-following consensus protocols and gradient-based game theory, distributed adaptive filtered feedback Nash equilibrium seeking strategies are constructed, with both centralized and decentralized control gains designed accordingly. Finally, a simulation example under different input nonlinearity scenarios is presented to demonstrate the validity of the proposed strategy.

title: Modeling and Validation of Rate-Integrating Gyroscopes With $0.01~^{\circ}/\sqrt{\text{h}}$ ARW and $100~\mu^{\circ}/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$ AWN
authors: Riccardo Nastri, Stefano Zoia, Davide Pavesi, Pietro Peliti, Valentina Zega, Gabriele Gattere, Luca Falorni, Giacomo Langfelder, Paolo Frigerio
abstract: This work presents a predictive model for the estimation of noise on the state variables of rate-integrating gyroscopes. Linearization of the state equations allows the design of an optimal control strategy and to analytically calculate the effect of noise on the control system. After cross-checking the model against behavioral noise simulations, the control system is implemented on FPGA and the model is validated on MEMS devices fabricated in a commercial process, employing an open-loop compensation of the stiffness asymmetry to enable a part-to-part repeatability of the scale-factor within 0.15%. Experimental results show a scale-factor linearity of 0.007%, a temperature stability of ±59.5 ppm, and a bias instability (BI) less than 0.2 °/h. An unprecedented angle white noise (AWN) floor of 100 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu ^{\circ }/\sqrt {\text {Hz}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> is measured, matching the predictions of the proposed model.[2025-0207]
